name: Home
title: Home
description: Home page of the discussion forum that shows trending, popular, and latest discussions, and provides navigation to discussion threads, categories, and search.

core_features: Load and display grouped discussion lists (trending, popular, latest) by calling getHomeFeed with dynamic limits.
Allow users to open a specific discussion by clicking its title, navigating to discussion.html with the corresponding discussionId URL parameter.
Allow users to navigate to a discussion category by clicking the category label, navigating to category.html with the corresponding categorySlug URL parameter.
Provide a global search form in the header that, on submit, navigates to search_results.html with the query URL parameter.
Allow users to focus or deep-link into a specific section (trending, popular, latest) via URL parameters, without additional API calls.
Automatically refresh the visible lists when URL parameters that affect getHomeFeed limits change (e.g., per-section item limits).
user_workflows:
Browse discussions from landing on Home:
1. User opens index.html without URL parameters.
2. home-feed-controller calls getHomeFeed using default per-section limits from application configuration.
3. home-feed-controller passes the returned trending, popular, and latest arrays to three discussion-section-list components.
4. User scrolls the page and reviews discussion-list-item elements under each section.
5. User clicks a discussion title; the router navigates to discussion.html?discussionId=<selectedDiscussionId>.

Navigate from a discussion to its category from Home:
1. After getHomeFeed data loads, the user scans a section (e.g., trending) and sees the category label rendered for each discussion-list-item.
2. The user clicks a category label.
3. The browser navigates to category.html?categorySlug=<selectedCategorySlug>, using the slug derived from the clicked item’s category context.

Use header search from Home:
1. User types a search term into the header-search-form input.
2. User submits the search form (via button click or pressing Enter).
3. onSubmit handler prevents page reload and updates location to search_results.html?query=<encodedSearchTerm>.
4. No APIs are called from the Home page for searching; search_results.html is responsible for executing the search.

Deep-link directly into a specific section on Home:
1. User opens index.html?section=<sectionName> where sectionName can be trending, popular, or latest.
2. home-feed-controller parses the section parameter and stores it as activeSection state.
3. feed-section-switcher uses activeSection to mark the corresponding section as active (e.g., scrolled into view or highlighted in a local tab control).
4. user can switch to another section via the UI; feed-section-switcher updates the URL’s section parameter (using pushState or replaceState) and updates activeSection without re-calling getHomeFeed.

Adjust visible item counts via URL parameters:
1. User opens a URL such as index.html?trendingLimit=<number>&popularLimit=<number>&latestLimit=<number>.
2. home-feed-controller reads these parameters and passes them as arguments to getHomeFeed.
3. getHomeFeed returns arrays sized according to the provided limits.
4. discussion-section-list components render exactly the returned items; changing limits and reloading the page will show more or fewer items per section.
interactions: Click on discussion titles: In any section (trending, popular, latest), clicking the discussion title triggers navigation to discussion.html?discussionId=<id> using the id from the bound Discussion entity.

Click on category labels: Clicking the category label under a discussion navigates to category.html?categorySlug=<slug> using the appropriate slug derived from the Category associated with the discussion.

Click on tags: Clicking any tag name associated with a discussion triggers navigation behavior (e.g., to a tag or search page) as defined by global routing outside this page; the Home page discussion-list-item simply exposes an onClick handler with the tag identifier so other routing logic can act on it.
Submit header search: Submitting the header-search-form reads the input value and changes the browser location to search_results.html?query=<encodedQuery>, without invoking any API from this page.

Switch active section (if a tab-like control is used): Interacting with feed-section-switcher (e.g., clicking a section tab) updates the activeSection state and synchronizes the URL’s section parameter so the current section can be bookmarked or shared.

Browser navigation (back/forward): When users navigate via the browser back/forward buttons, the page reads the current URL parameters on popstate events (especially section and per-section limits) and updates internal state (e.g., activeSection) and displayed sections without necessarily re-invoking getHomeFeed unless limits changed.
page_functionality.state_logic: The Home page uses URL parameters to determine initial state and API call configuration. Supported parameters include: section (string: 'trending', 'popular', 'latest'), trendingLimit (integer), popularLimit (integer), and latestLimit (integer). On initial load, home-feed-controller reads these parameters. If limit parameters are present and valid, they are passed directly to getHomeFeed(trendingLimit, popularLimit, latestLimit). If any are missing, the controller uses application-level default values that are not hardcoded in this document. The returned data is stored in in-memory page state and is not persisted beyond the session. The section parameter controls which section is considered active. feed-section-switcher uses this value to focus or highlight the corresponding section. When a user changes the active section through the UI, the component updates the section parameter in the URL via history.pushState or history.replaceState, keeping the section parameter synchronized with the current state. Changing section does not re-call getHomeFeed because the underlying data sets remain valid; only presentation changes. If a user manually changes the URL to alter any of the limit parameters and reloads the page, home-feed-controller detects the new values and calls getHomeFeed again with the new limits. Navigation to other pages always uses URL parameters as defined: discussion.html?discussionId=<id> for discussions, category.html?categorySlug=<slug> for categories, and search_results.html?query=<query> for search results.

components.functionality: Provides global navigation including a link to Home, access to categories, bookmarks, user settings, and contains the header-search-form used to initiate searches from any page including Home.
Captures a search query string from the user and navigates to the search results page with the query as a URL parameter.

Central controller for loading and managing the Home feed data. On page load, reads URL parameters for section, trendingLimit, popularLimit, and latestLimit, then calls getHomeFeed with the derived limits. Stores the resulting trending, popular, and latest arrays in internal state and passes them to discussion-section-list components. Maintains derived loading and error states to control rendering of lists and empty states.

Manages which section (trending, popular, latest) is currently active or focused. Initializes its active section from the section URL parameter or a default when not present. Updates the URL’s section parameter when the user switches sections and updates local state to keep the UI in sync. Does not make API calls itself; it only orchestrates presentation based on already-fetched data from home-feed-controller.

Displays the list of trending discussions returned from getHomeFeed. Renders a sequence of discussion-list-item components using the 'trending' array from home-feed-controller. Handles empty data by delegating to home-feed-empty-state when the array is empty.

Displays the list of popular discussions returned from getHomeFeed. Renders a sequence of discussion-list-item components using the 'popular' array from home-feed-controller. Handles empty data similarly to the trending section.

Displays the list of latest discussions returned from getHomeFeed. Renders a sequence of discussion-list-item components using the 'latest' array from home-feed-controller. Handles empty data similarly to other sections.

Represents a single discussion entry within a section list. Displays discussion.title, originalAuthorDisplayName, categoryName, tagNames, replyCount, viewCount, score, timestamps such as createdAt and lastReplyAt, and indicators such as isSubscribed, isBookmarked, and hasPoll when provided by the Discussion object. Provides click targets for the discussion title, category label, and tags, each triggering the appropriate navigation.

Displays a generic message or placeholder when a given section (trending, popular, or latest) receives an empty array from getHomeFeed. The component is purely functional, deciding whether to render based on the length of the bound list.

Listens to changes in browser URL (including initial load and popstate events) and updates in-memory page state related to section and per-section limits. Notifies home-feed-controller when relevant parameters used by getHomeFeed (trendingLimit, popularLimit, latestLimit) change so it can refresh data, and notifies feed-section-switcher when section changes so presentation stays in sync.

layout.grid_system: Implicit centered column layout likely based on a 12‑column grid with a wide central content span (approx. 8–10 columns) and large left/right gutters.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a single, centered content column beneath a full-width black global header. Within that column, place a spacious page-intro band that holds the feed section switcher on the left and the search control on the right, followed by a vertical stack of discussion cards (only one section list visible at a time: trending, popular, or latest). Each discussion item is a full-width card with a compact two-column header row (author/meta left, overflow icon right) and a single text column below, mirroring the forum post card structure from the mockup.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a single, centered content column beneath a full-width black global header. Within that column, place a spacious page-intro band that holds the feed section switcher on the left and the search control on the right, followed by a vertical stack of discussion cards (only one section list visible at a time: trending, popular, or latest). Each discussion item is a full-width card with a compact two-column header row (author/meta left, overflow icon right) and a single text column below, mirroring the forum post card structure from the mockup.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Reserve the top ~8–10% of the viewport for the global black header. Below that, create an off-white header band occupying roughly 20–30% of the viewport height before the first visible card, maintaining a large expanse of empty space. The main content column should span about 60–70% of the viewport width and remain centered, leaving 15–20% gutters on each side. Within this column, cards use generous padding (24–32px) and are separated by 24–32px vertical gaps, with 48–64px gaps between major structural zones (header → band → first card).
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain low content density by limiting visible controls (few nav items, a simple tab row, a single search affordance, and sparse card content), using large vertical spacing, and avoiding tight clusters of elements. Typically only one to two full cards appear within the initial viewport, reinforcing a calm, reading-first experience rather than an information-dense dashboard.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Guide the eye from the high-contrast black global header at the top, left-to-right across navigation, then down into the quiet off-white band. Within that band, the left-aligned section switcher provides the primary reading anchor, while the right-aligned search icon or field becomes a secondary focal point. From there, the eye naturally drops to the first centered discussion card, flows horizontally across the avatar and meta information, then vertically down through the card body text, ending on the right-aligned overflow icon as a subtle interaction affordance.

layout.overall_description: The page opens with a solid black navigation bar pinned to the very top, stretching edge-to-edge across the viewport. Immediately underneath, the background shifts to an off-white canvas that feels open and mostly empty at first glance. In the middle of this off-white area, a centered content column—about two-thirds of the viewport width—anchors the experience. Toward the lower part of this band, a clean horizontal row aligns the feed section tabs on the left with a minimalist search control on the far right, leaving generous breathing room above and below. As you scroll your gaze downward, the first discussion card appears roughly in the middle of the viewport, framed by wide left and right gutters. Each card spans the full width of the content column, with soft internal padding, a concise header row (avatar and meta left, overflow icon right), and a quiet block of body text underneath. Cards are stacked vertically with ample space between them, and only one section’s cards (Trending, Popular, or Latest) are visible at a time. The overall impression is of a restrained, monochrome reading surface where focus naturally falls on the content of each discussion rather than on interface chrome.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: A full-width, black horizontal bar is fixed along the very top edge of the viewport, occupying roughly 8–10% of the viewport height (about 64–80px). Its content is centered within the same main page column width (about 60–70% of the viewport, capped at a comfortable max like 1040–1120px), leaving equal gutters on both sides. Inside this bar, navigation elements are arranged in a single row: any logo or title is aligned to the left side of the central column, while optional secondary navigation items align to the right or cluster next to the logo, all with ample horizontal padding (24–32px) from the column edges. The bar stays visually dense and high-contrast but uses minimal elements and no separators, setting the top anchor for the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed to the top of the viewport; remains visible while the main content column scrolls beneath it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Positioned in the off-white area immediately below the global header, the search form sits inside the central content column and is aligned to the far right edge of that column. It shares a horizontal row with the feed section switcher on the left. Vertically, this row is placed so that there is a generous 48–64px gap from the bottom of the black global header and another 32–48px of empty space below before the first discussion card begins. Visually, the search form reads primarily as a simple icon or very compact field: its visible control might be a 24–32px square search icon with an invisible hit area extending to around 32–40px, keeping the interface visually minimal while ensuring usability. The field or icon does not stretch across the page; it hugs the right edge of the central column, leaving clear negative space between it and the content to its left.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main scroll area; it scrolls away with the content once the user scrolls down.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This is a non-visual data layer that conceptually sits between the header band and the section lists. It does not occupy any visible space or alter the layout directly. Its presence is felt only through which discussion section list (trending, popular, or latest) is currently rendered within the central column beneath the header band.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; no spatial behavior. Influences which section list is mounted in the central column but does not render a container of its own.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section switcher appears in the off-white band below the global header, aligned to the left side of the central content column. It is part of the same horizontal row as the search form, with the switcher on the far left and the search on the far right, and an open expanse of off-white space between them. The switcher itself is a low-profile row of text tabs—e.g., 'Trending', 'Popular', 'Latest'—laid out horizontally with 24–32px horizontal spacing between labels. The active tab may have a subtle underline or weight change, but there are no heavy borders or pill backgrounds, preserving the minimalist aesthetic. The entire row sits roughly 48–64px below the bottom of the black header and about 32–48px above the first discussion card, giving it clear breathing room and making it an unobtrusive but obvious control for changing the visible section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main scrollable content. All tabs share a single line; switching tab content updates which section list is visible below without moving the switcher.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When active, the trending section list occupies the central content column directly beneath the off-white header band that contains the section switcher and search form. The list begins after a vertical gap of approximately 32–48px from the switcher/search row, so the first card appears around the middle of the initial viewport height. The list is a vertical stack of discussion cards (discussion-list-item components), each stretching to the full width of the central column. Between each card is a consistent vertical gap of about 24–32px. The background behind the cards remains the off-white page background, while each card appears as a slightly elevated or bordered panel to differentiate it. The list may extend beyond the fold, with the page scrolling smoothly so that cards glide under the fixed black header.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Occupies the main scrollable area within the central column. Only one section list (trending, popular, or latest) is visible at a time in this position.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The popular section list shares the exact same spatial slot as the trending list: it is anchored in the central content column immediately below the section switcher and above the page bottom, beginning 32–48px beneath the switcher/search row. When this section is active, its own stack of discussion-list-item cards appears in place of the trending list, using the same full-width card layout with 24–32px vertical gaps between cards and large off-white gutters on both sides. The background and spacing remain consistent, so switching between sections feels like swapping the content of a single, stable feed area rather than jumping between distinct pages.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Mutually exclusive with the other section lists; fills the same central scroll area when selected via the feed-section-switcher.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Like the trending and popular lists, the latest section list is positioned in the same central vertical feed area directly after the header band. It begins 32–48px below the switcher/search row and runs downward as a stack of full-width discussion-list-item cards. Each card maintains 24–32px vertical separation, and the left/right gutters remain 15–20% of the viewport width, emphasizing the centrality of the feed. The spatial treatment is identical to the other lists to maintain visual continuity; only the card content changes to reflect the latest discussions.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Rendered in the same scrollable region as the other section lists, shown only when 'Latest' is the active tab.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each discussion list item is a card that spans the full width of the central content column, visually sitting on the off-white background. The card has uniform internal padding of about 24–32px on all sides. At the top of the card, a header row runs left-to-right: on the left, a small circular avatar (approx. 32–40px diameter) is aligned flush with the card’s left padding, followed by a vertical stack of author name and meta details (timestamp, category) to its right, separated by 8–16px of horizontal space. On the far right of this header row, a vertical overflow (kebab) icon is aligned with the card’s right padding, with an invisible hit area sized around 24–32px square. This header row occupies the full width of the card with clear separation between left and right regions. Below the header, after a vertical gap of 16–24px, the card presents the discussion title and/or excerpt as left-aligned text flowing in a single column, typically taking up 2–4 lines before truncation. Optional tags or subtle metadata can sit below the main text, separated by an additional 8–16px if used. The card’s height is flexible but, at typical content lengths, each card occupies enough vertical space that only one to two full cards appear within the initial viewport, reinforcing the low-density, reading-focused feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element within the section lists, stacked vertically with consistent gaps; no floating or overlapping behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty state message appears in the same central column area where section lists would normally render, directly beneath the header band. It replaces the list of discussion cards entirely when there are no items to show for the active section. The empty state content is vertically offset so it appears roughly centered within the initial viewport: there is a large vertical gap (about 64–96px) from the section switcher row above, then a compact block of text (and optionally a very simple monochrome icon) is centered horizontally within the column. The messaging is short and set in a single narrow text column, with line lengths similar to card content. Surrounding whitespace dominates, emphasizing the absence of content and keeping the calm, minimalist tone.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Occupies the same scroll region as the section lists but with minimal height; the page remains scrollable if other layout constraints require it, though typically the message fits within a single screen.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This URL state manager is invisible in the UI and does not render any visual container. It conceptually sits alongside the home-feed-controller, ensuring that the active section (trending, popular, latest) and any search parameters are reflected in the URL and restored on load, without impacting layout or spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; no spatial footprint or behavior in the rendered layout.

name: Categories Overview
title: Categories
description: Displays all discussion categories with key metrics and links into each category’s discussion list, and provides access to global search.

core_features: Retrieve all discussion categories using the getCategoriesOverview interface on initial page load.
Display each category with its name, optional description, discussion count, and latest activity timestamp.
Allow users to navigate to a specific category’s discussion list by clicking on a category item, passing the category slug as a URL parameter to category.html.
Provide a header search form that redirects users to search_results.html with the search term as a URL parameter.
Allow users to change the sort order of categories (for example by default order, name, or latest activity) and reflect the chosen sort in the page URL for shareable state.
user_workflows: Browse categories: 1) User opens categories.html. 2) Page reads any existing sort parameter from the URL. 3) Page calls getCategoriesOverview to obtain the list of categories. 4) Page sorts the received categories according to the current sort parameter. 5) Page renders the category list with name, description, discussion count, and latest activity for each category.

Open a category: 1) User reviews the list of categories. 2) User clicks on a specific category item. 3) The click handler reads the slug field of the selected Category. 4) Browser navigates to category.html with categorySlug set to the selected category slug in the query string.

Search from header: 1) User focuses the header search input. 2) User types a search term. 3) User submits the search form. 4) Submit handler prevents any in-page filtering and redirects to search_results.html with query set to the entered search term in the query string.

Change category sort order: 1) User opens the sort control on the categories page. 2) User selects a sort option (for example default order, alphabetical by name, or latest activity). 3) Sort control updates the sort parameter in the categories.html URL without adding other state. 4) Categories controller re-reads the sort parameter, re-sorts the in-memory list of categories obtained from getCategoriesOverview, and re-renders the list according to the new sort order.
interactions: On page load, the page logic reads the current URL query parameters (such as sort) and calls getCategoriesOverview to obtain the Category array used throughout the page.

When a user clicks on a category item (any clickable area configured for the category), the page constructs a navigation URL to category.html with categorySlug set to the Category.slug value and navigates the browser to that URL.
When a user submits the header search form, the page captures the search text, URL-encodes it, and navigates to search_results.html with query set to the submitted term.

When a user changes the sort option in the sort control, the page updates the sort value in the categories.html URL query string and triggers a re-sorting of the currently loaded Category list based on the new sort mode.
page_functionality.state_logic: The categories.html page does not require any specific incoming parameters from the application router but uses URL query parameters to represent UI state. A sort parameter in the query string (for example categories.html?sort=name or categories.html?sort=activity) determines how the Category array returned by getCategoriesOverview is ordered before rendering. When the sort parameter is absent, the page applies a default sort rule (such as using the position field if present). The page does not persist state in localStorage; instead, any change to the sort control updates the sort parameter in the URL, allowing the current view to be shareable and restorable by reloading or sharing the URL. Navigation to other pages uses URL parameters only: clicking a category navigates to category.html with categorySlug set to the selected Category.slug, and submitting the header search navigates to search_results.html with query set to the user-entered search term.

components.functionality: Coordinates loading and presentation of the categories overview. On page load or when the URL changes, it reads the sort parameter from the query string, calls getCategoriesOverview to obtain the list of categories, applies client-side sorting according to the sort parameter (using fields such as position, name, or lastActivityAt from each Category), and exposes the sorted list, loading state, and error state to child components.

Provides a single search input in the global header while on categories.html. Captures the user’s free-text query and, upon form submission, redirects the browser to search_results.html with the query parameter set to the entered term. It does not filter the categories in-place; its sole responsibility is navigation to the search results page.

Allows the user to select the sort mode for the category list (for example default order, alphabetical, or latest activity). Reads the initial sort value from the sort parameter in the URL, keeps the UI selection in sync with that parameter, and updates the URL query string when the user changes the sort option. Triggers the categories-overview-controller to re-apply sorting to the currently loaded Category array.

Receives the sorted array of Category objects from the categories-overview-controller and renders an item for each category. For each Category, it displays the name, optional description, discussionCount if provided, and a formatted representation of lastActivityAt when available. It wires click events on each category item to trigger navigation to the corresponding category page using the Category.slug value.

Displays an appropriate empty-state message or indicator when the categories-overview-controller provides an empty array of categories. This component is shown only when the data load has completed successfully and the resulting Category array has no items.

Shows a loading indicator while getCategoriesOverview is in progress and hides itself when the categories-overview-controller reports that loading has completed (either successfully or with an error).
Displays an error message or indicator if the call to getCategoriesOverview fails. Optionally provides a retry action that triggers the controller to call getCategoriesOverview again.

Encapsulates the navigation logic for a single category item. It receives a Category object from the category-list component and constructs a target URL to category.html using the Category.slug field as the categorySlug query parameter. When the user clicks the item, it navigates the browser to that URL.

layout.grid_system: Implicit centered column layout likely based on a 12‑column grid with a wide central content span (approx. 8–10 columns) and large left/right gutters.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a strict top-to-bottom flow. A full-width black global navigation bar sits at the very top (outside this component set), followed by a tall, off-white main area. Within this main area, all forum content is constrained to a centered column that spans roughly 60–70% of the viewport width, with generous left and right gutters (about 15–20% each). At the top of this column sits a sparse header row (page title + header search on the right). Below that, a single, large, centered 'categories' card acts as the main focus, containing the sort control in its header row and the category list in its body. Loading, empty, and error states all appear in place of the list within this same card, preserving the linear, card-based narrative.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a strict top-to-bottom flow. A full-width black global navigation bar sits at the very top (outside this component set), followed by a tall, off-white main area. Within this main area, all forum content is constrained to a centered column that spans roughly 60–70% of the viewport width, with generous left and right gutters (about 15–20% each). At the top of this column sits a sparse header row (page title + header search on the right). Below that, a single, large, centered 'categories' card acts as the main focus, containing the sort control in its header row and the category list in its body. Loading, empty, and error states all appear in place of the list within this same card, preserving the linear, card-based narrative.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Roughly the top 8–10% of the viewport is taken by the black navigation bar. Below it, the off-white background extends across the viewport. The first 20–30% of the viewport height under the nav is intentionally sparse: a centered header row with the page title on the left and the header search form aligned to the right, surrounded by ample vertical whitespace (48–64px above the categories card). The primary categories card occupies the central 50–60% of the viewport height on first load, with 24–32px padding on all sides inside the card. The categories column itself spans 60–70% of the viewport width, with stable, empty gutters left and right. Vertical spacing between major sections (header row vs. card, between card and subsequent content) follows a 48–64px rhythm, while spacing inside the card and between list items adheres to 16–32px increments.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is deliberately low. Above the fold, users see the navigation bar, a very lightweight header band with minimal text and a small search control, and then a single prominent categories card. Inside that card, category rows are well spaced with 16–24px vertical padding each and clear separation (16–24px gaps) between them. There are very few controls visible at once: the header search, a single sort control inside the card, and the list of category links. Loading, empty, and error states all use simple centered text or iconography with plenty of whitespace, avoiding any clutter or dense information tables.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye is first drawn to the strong black navigation bar at the top. It then falls downward into the quiet off-white main area, where the centered column and large empty space guide attention toward the subtle header row. Within this row, the left-aligned page title anchors the start of the reading flow, while the right-aligned header search form provides a secondary focal point. From there, the eye naturally descends to the large, centered categories card. Inside this card, the top header row (left: categories meta; right: sort control) establishes a left-to-right scan, then the user moves vertically down the category list, scanning each category name from left to right. When loading, empty, or error states are active, the attention is pulled to the centered message inside the card, as there are no competing elements nearby.

layout.overall_description: The Categories Overview page sits on a calm, off-white canvas beneath a stark, full-width black navigation bar that occupies the top sliver of the viewport. Below the nav, the interface breathes: a wide band of mostly empty off-white space is pierced only by a slim centered content column. At the top of this column, a modest header row places the page title on the left and a compact search form aligned to the right, with generous vertical margins above and below. Farther down, filling the visual center of the screen, a single large white card floats in the middle of the page, spanning about two-thirds of the width and framed by comfortable left and right gutters. This card is the core of the layout: its header row mirrors the forum post pattern, with descriptive category meta on the left and a subtle sort control on the right, while its body holds a vertically stacked list of category links, each row separated by measured whitespace. When there are no categories or while data is loading, the interior of this same card shows a centered, minimalist state indicator instead of the list, preserving the quiet, editorial feel. The overall effect is a spacious, linear reading experience that gently guides the user from the navigation, into search, and then down into browsing categories without visual noise.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The categories-overview-controller is an invisible structural container that spans the full width of the centered content column beneath the global navigation bar. It begins just below the nav's bottom edge, extending downward to encompass the entire main content area: the sparse header band (page title + header search) and the main categories card. It does not render visible chrome itself; instead, it orchestrates which child components (category-list vs. empty, loading, or error indicators) appear inside the central categories card. Spatially, it defines the content column boundaries (approximately 60–70% of viewport width, centered) and ensures all internal components respect the consistent left and right padding from this virtual column edge.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard document flow container occupying the centered main column from just below the navigation bar to the bottom of the page; no fixed or sticky behavior, it scrolls naturally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header-search-form sits within the off-white header band at the top of the centered content column, approximately 64px below the bottom of the black navigation bar. It shares a single horizontal row with the page title (not part of this component list): the title is left-aligned within the column, while the header-search-form is right-aligned on the same baseline. The search form itself is visually minimalist: it presents as an understated text field or icon-triggered field about 240–320px wide, but with an invisible tap area of roughly 24–32px padding around its visible affordance. It does not stretch full-width; instead, it occupies the rightmost 20–25% of the content column, leaving clear space between it and the column center. There is generous vertical space (about 32–48px) below this header row before the top edge of the main categories card begins, reinforcing the feeling that the search is a lightweight, global utility rather than a dense control cluster.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline element within the header row of the centered column, right-aligned; it scrolls with the page and has no sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The category-sort-control is placed inside the top header row of the main categories card, following the two-column header pattern from the mockup. Within the card’s top padding (24–32px from the card edges), the left side of the header row contains the card’s descriptive text (e.g., 'All categories' and a count), while the category-sort-control is aligned to the far right edge of the card’s content area. The header row spans the full width of the card; the sort control itself is compact, resembling a simple text label with a down chevron or a minimal dropdown about 120–160px wide. It does not dominate the header visually; instead, it anchors the right side of the row, providing a subtle secondary interaction. There is a 16–24px gap between the bottom of this header row and the beginning of the category-list body content, ensuring clear separation between meta/sorting and the list items.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline control within the card header, right-aligned in a two-column header row; it moves with the card as the page scrolls and does not become sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The category-list occupies the entire body area of the main categories card beneath its header row. The card itself is centered on the page, spans about 60–70% of the viewport width, and floats on an off-white background, with 24–32px padding on all sides inside the card. Within this padded region, the category-list is a vertical stack of category rows running full-width from the left padding edge to the right padding edge of the card. Each row maintains a low-density feel: the category item’s tappable area is at least 48px tall, with around 16–24px vertical spacing between rows. Rows use a simple, editorial layout: the category name (rendered via category-item-link) starts near the left padding edge, and any secondary metrics or labels align to the right within the same row, leaving open horizontal space in between. The list extends downward, and on initial load, only a handful of rows (e.g., 4–6) are visible inside the card’s central viewport area, with additional categories scrolling below the fold within the page. There is at least 24px of empty space between the last visible row and the bottom padding of the card, ensuring the content doesn’t feel cramped against the card’s edge.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Block-level content area within the central categories card; it expands vertically as needed and scrolls with the page, without any internal scrollbars or fixed headers.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The category-list-empty-state appears inside the same central categories card where the category-list would normally be rendered. When active, it replaces the list entirely. The card retains its centered position and dimensions (about 60–70% viewport width, padded 24–32px on all sides) but its body area is dominated by a single, simple empty-state message. This message is vertically and horizontally centered within the card’s inner content area, with no other competing elements. The text is short and neutral in tone, possibly accompanied by a very small, monochrome icon above it, with 16–24px spacing between icon and text. The surrounding space remains mostly blank, emphasizing the minimalism and focusing the user’s attention on the message and any primary call to action (if present). The card still maintains at least 48px of space between its content and its edges, preserving the airy, editorial look.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Mutually exclusive with the category-list inside the central card body; displayed in place of the list, centered within the card, and scrolling with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The categories-loading-indicator also uses the main categories card as its stage. While categories are being fetched, the card’s header row (including the category-sort-control) may still appear at the top, but the body area that would normally contain the category-list is replaced by a centered loading indicator. This indicator could be a monochrome spinner or a subtle pulsing dot, placed directly in the middle of the card’s internal content area. There is no additional text clutter; if helper text is present (e.g., 'Loading categories…'), it sits just below the spinner with around 8–16px vertical spacing. The large amounts of empty, off-white space inside the card emphasize the transient nature of the state without adding visual noise. The card remains centered, with consistent padding and gutters, so the layout doesn’t shift when the loading state ends.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily replaces the category-list within the central card body during loading; it is centered within the card and scrolls with the rest of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The categories-error-indicator occupies the same body area of the central categories card, appearing when the category data fails to load. The card stays centered and retains its usual dimensions, but instead of the category-list, a concise error message is displayed in the center of the card. The message is horizontally centered and placed near the vertical center, with enough space (at least 24–32px) above and below to prevent visual crowding. A small monochrome icon or a leading keyword (like 'Error') may appear above the message with 8–16px spacing, and a minimal retry control (if included) appears below with similar spacing. There are no loud colors or heavy borders; the emphasis is on clarity and calmness. The surrounding interior of the card remains mostly blank, allowing the error state to be noticeable yet understated within the overall minimalist aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Displays in place of the category-list inside the central card body when an error occurs; it is centered within the card and participates in normal page scrolling.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each category-item-link is the primary interactive element within a row of the category-list. Within a given row, the link is left-aligned, starting near the left padding edge of the card (about 24–32px from the card’s outer edge). It occupies the majority of the row’s horizontal space—roughly 60–70% of the row’s width—leaving room to the right for secondary metrics such as topic counts or last activity dates. The clickable area of the link is generous: at least 48px tall with 16–24px vertical padding above and below the text, and it extends horizontally across the row to create a comfortable hit target without drawing heavy visual borders. The text styling is simple and readable, with enough contrast against the white card background to stand out but without bright accent colors. There is a consistent gap (16–24px) between consecutive links/rows, maintaining the low-density, editorial rhythm of the list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within each row of the category-list, aligned to the left; it expands to fill the row’s tap area and scrolls naturally as part of the list and page.

name: Category Discussions
title: Category Discussions
description: Displays discussions for a specific category, allows sorting and basic filtering, and lets users create new discussions (optionally with a poll in supported categories).

core_features: Load and display discussions for the category specified by the categorySlug URL parameter using getCategoryDiscussions.
Allow users to change sorting within the category (e.g., latest, top, most replied) and reload the list by updating the sortBy URL parameter and calling getCategoryDiscussions.
Support basic filtering of the displayed discussions via the filters parameter of getCategoryDiscussions (filters are controlled by UI elements on this page).
Provide a "New Discussion" action that reveals a discussion creation form on this page.
Allow users to create a new discussion with title, body, tags, and an option to subscribe, by calling createDiscussion with the current categorySlug.
For categories that support polls (e.g., product feedback), show a poll configuration section in the new discussion form and pass pollConfig to createDiscussion.
Navigate to discussion.html with the appropriate discussionId when a user clicks an existing discussion title or after successfully creating a new discussion.
Allow users to submit a search via the header search bar and navigate to search_results.html with a query URL parameter.
user_workflows: View discussions in a category: 1) User navigates to category.html with categorySlug in the URL. 2) On page init, the page reads categorySlug, sortBy (if present), and page (if present). 3) The page calls getCategoryDiscussions with categorySlug, sortBy (or an application-level default if missing), filters derived from the current UI filter state, page (or an application-level default if missing), and pageSize (application-level value). 4) The returned category and discussions are bound to the header and discussion list components for display.

Change sorting of discussions: 1) User selects a different sort option in the sort control. 2) The sort control triggers navigation to category.html with categorySlug set to the current category and sortBy set to the selected sort key, page reset to 1. 3) On navigation, the page re-runs getCategoryDiscussions with the new sortBy and displays the updated list.

Paginate through discussions: 1) User clicks a pagination control for a specific page. 2) The pagination component triggers navigation to category.html with categorySlug unchanged, sortBy preserved from the current URL, and page updated to the selected page number. 3) On navigation, the page calls getCategoryDiscussions with the updated page and displays the corresponding slice of discussions.

Apply basic filters: 1) User changes one or more filter controls (for example, toggling a "has poll" or "subscribed" filter, depending on what the UI exposes). 2) The filter component updates its in-memory filter state and triggers a fresh call to getCategoryDiscussions using the current categorySlug, sortBy and page from the URL, and the updated filters object. 3) The list is refreshed using the new response. (Filters do not modify the URL parameters; they are local to the current viewing session.)

Open an existing discussion: 1) User clicks on a discussion title in the discussion list. 2) The discussion-list component reads the associated discussion.id and navigates to discussion.html with discussionId set to that value. 3) The discussion thread page handles the rest of the flow.

Start a new discussion without a poll: 1) User clicks the "New Discussion" button in the category header or toolbar. 2) The new discussion form component becomes visible. 3) User fills in title, body, optional tags, and optionally checks a subscribe checkbox. 4) User submits the form, which calls createDiscussion with categorySlug from the URL, and the form values mapped to title, body, tagNames, subscribe, and pollConfig omitted or set to null. 5) On success, the createDiscussion response includes the created discussion; the page navigates to discussion.html with discussionId set to the created discussion.id.

Start a new discussion with a poll (in poll-enabled categories): 1) User navigates to a category whose metadata indicates poll support (e.g., a slug that the application maps to poll-enabled behavior). 2) User clicks "New Discussion"; the form shows an additional poll configuration section. 3) User fills title, body, tags, subscribe, and configures the poll (question, single/multiple choice, options, and duration). 4) On form submission, the page constructs a pollConfig object based on the poll fields and calls createDiscussion with categorySlug, title, body, tagNames, subscribe, and pollConfig. 5) On success, the page navigates to discussion.html with discussionId from the created discussion.

Search from within a category: 1) User types a search term in the header search bar while on category.html. 2) User submits the search form. 3) The header search component navigates to search_results.html with query set to the entered term in the URL parameters.
interactions: On page load (or when the URL changes to category.html), the page reads categorySlug, sortBy, and page from the URL, then triggers getCategoryDiscussions to fetch and bind category and discussion data.

When the sort dropdown value changes, the sort-bar component triggers navigation to category.html with updated sortBy and page=1 while preserving categorySlug; the page then re-fetches data via getCategoryDiscussions.

When the pagination controls are used, the pagination component triggers navigation to category.html with updated page and existing categorySlug and sortBy; data is fetched again via getCategoryDiscussions.

When any filter control changes, the filter component updates its internal filters object and re-calls getCategoryDiscussions with the same categorySlug, sortBy, and page from the URL but a new filters parameter.
When a discussion title is clicked, the discussion-list component reads the clicked discussionId and navigates to discussion.html with that discussionId in the URL.

When the "New Discussion" button is clicked, the new-discussion-form component toggles its visibility and initializes empty form state for title, body, tag inputs, subscribe option, and poll configuration if applicable.

When the new discussion form is submitted, the form validates its fields (title, body at minimum; optional tags; optional poll fields if poll is enabled and activated), then calls createDiscussion with parameters derived from categorySlug and current form state. On success, the success handler navigates to discussion.html with the returned discussion.id.

When the category supports polls and the poll toggle within the form is turned on, the poll-config-editor component becomes active, enabling poll question, multipleChoiceAllowed, option list management (add/remove options), and duration fields, and contributes a pollConfig object to the createDiscussion call.
When the header search form is submitted, the header-search-form component navigates to search_results.html with a query URL parameter matching the search term.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary cross-request state for this page is managed through URL parameters, not localStorage. The categorySlug URL parameter identifies which category's discussions are displayed. It is required for calling getCategoryDiscussions and is passed unchanged when changing sortBy or page. The sortBy URL parameter represents the selected sorting mode. If present, it is used directly in getCategoryDiscussions; if absent, the application uses a predefined default sort mode when calling the interface. Changing the sort dropdown triggers navigation to category.html with categorySlug set to the current category, sortBy updated to the new selection, and page reset to 1. The page URL parameter specifies the current page index for pagination. If present, it is used as the page argument in getCategoryDiscussions; if absent, the application uses a predefined default (such as the first page). Pagination interactions update only the page parameter, keeping categorySlug and sortBy in sync with the current view. Filters are held in in-memory component state and passed as the filters argument to getCategoryDiscussions; they are not encoded into the URL, so they reset when the page is reloaded from a URL alone. After createDiscussion successfully returns, the page does not update URL parameters on category.html but instead navigates to discussion.html with a discussionId parameter derived from the created discussion. Search interactions on this page navigate away to search_results.html with query set to the user-entered term.

components.functionality: Reads URL parameters on load or navigation to category.html, calls getCategoryDiscussions, and initializes shared data for the page.
data_binding: Pagination metadata (page, pageSize, totalCount from getCategoryDiscussions)
event_handlers: onInit: Read categorySlug, sortBy, and page from the URL query string; if categorySlug is missing, invoke the application's error/redirect behavior; determine effective sortBy and page values (using application defaults where omitted); construct a filters object based on any currently active filter component state (initially empty) and call getCategoryDiscussions(categorySlug, sortBy, filters, page, pageSize).

onUrlChange: When the router detects navigation to category.html (or a change in its query parameters), re-run the same logic as onInit to reload the list for the new categorySlug/sortBy/page combination.

onFiltersChange: When notified by the filter-bar component that filters have changed, call getCategoryDiscussions again with the current categorySlug and sortBy and page from the URL and the updated filters object.

components.functionality: Displays the current category's name, description, and optionally discussion statistics, and exposes the main "New Discussion" action.
event_handlers: onClickNewDiscussion: Toggle visibility of the new-discussion-form component from hidden to visible, and notify it of the current categorySlug and whether polls should be enabled based on the category's properties (e.g., slug or other configuration).

components.functionality: Provides sorting controls, basic filtering controls, and may surface the "New Discussion" button (especially on smaller screens) for convenience.
data_binding: Current sortBy value (derived from URL parameter sortBy or application default)
Filter state object (local to this component but used as filters in getCategoryDiscussions)
event_handlers: onChangeSort: When the user selects a sort option, construct a new URL for category.html that keeps the current categorySlug, sets sortBy to the selected value, and page to 1, then navigate to that URL (triggering category-discussions-init to re-fetch data).

onChangeFilter: When a filter control changes, update the local filters object and emit a filtersChanged event to category-discussions-init so it can call getCategoryDiscussions with the updated filters.
onClickNewDiscussion: Same behavior as category-header.onClickNewDiscussion; shows the new-discussion-form when clicked.

components.functionality: Renders the list of discussions returned by getCategoryDiscussions for the current category and wires navigation when a user selects a discussion.
event_handlers: onClickDiscussionTitle: When a discussion title is clicked, read the associated discussion.id and navigate to discussion.html with discussionId set to that id.
onClickDiscussionRow: Optionally, treat clicking elsewhere in the row as equivalent to onClickDiscussionTitle, navigating with discussionId.

onRefreshRequested: When notified (for example, after a new discussion is created and the application chooses to keep the user on the category page), trigger category-discussions-init to re-call getCategoryDiscussions with the current URL parameters.

components.functionality: Displays pagination based on total results and controls navigation between pages of discussions.
event_handlers: onClickPageNumber: When a new page number is selected, construct and navigate to category.html with categorySlug taken from the current URL, sortBy preserved from the current URL, and page set to the selected page number.
onClickNextPage: If a next page exists, navigate to category.html with page incremented by one, preserving categorySlug and sortBy.
onClickPreviousPage: If a previous page exists, navigate to category.html with page decremented by one, preserving categorySlug and sortBy.

components.functionality: Provides the UI for creating a new discussion within the current category, including title, body, tags, subscription option, and optional poll configuration.
data_binding: categorySlug (from URL parameter, passed in when the form is opened)
Form state: title (string), body (string), tagNames (array of strings), subscribe (boolean)

Poll configuration state (if enabled): question (string), multipleChoiceAllowed (boolean), options (array of option strings), startAt/endAt or duration fields, mapped to pollConfig for createDiscussion
createDiscussion response: Discussion, Post, Tag[], Poll (if any) for post-submit handling
event_handlers: onShow: Initialize empty form state when the form is first shown, using the current categorySlug and any category-specific defaults (e.g., whether polls are offered in this category).
onChangeField: Update internal form state as the user edits title, body, tagNames, and subscribe fields.
onTogglePollEnabled: If the category allows polls, toggling this control shows or hides the poll-config-editor component and resets or initializes poll configuration state.

onSubmit: Validate that required fields (at minimum title and body) are present; construct a pollConfig object only if the poll controls are enabled and valid; call createDiscussion with parameters: categorySlug from URL, title, body, tagNames from form state, subscribe, and pollConfig (or a null/undefined value if no poll is configured). On successful response, read the returned discussion.id and navigate to discussion.html with discussionId set to that id.
onSubmitError: Display validation or API error messages without navigating away; allow user to correct and resubmit.
onCancel: Hide the form and optionally clear its state without calling createDiscussion.

components.functionality: Manages the poll configuration portion of the new discussion form for categories that support polls.
data_binding: PollOption[] (local configuration mapped to pollConfig.options with optionText values)
Poll.startAt and Poll.endAt (or an equivalent duration representation, mapped into pollConfig)
event_handlers: onInitForCategory: When new-discussion-form opens and the current category supports polls, initialize an empty poll configuration state.
onChangeQuestion: Update the local poll question field.
onToggleMultipleChoice: Update the local multipleChoiceAllowed flag.
onAddOption: Append a new empty poll option entry to the options list, to be filled by the user.
onChangeOptionText: Update the optionText of a specific option in the options list.
onRemoveOption: Remove an option from the options list.
onChangeDuration: Update the duration or end date/time fields that will be mapped to pollConfig.startAt and pollConfig.endAt when the form is submitted.
onProvidePollConfig: On request from new-discussion-form (e.g., during onSubmit), return a pollConfig object representing the current poll settings so it can be passed to createDiscussion.

components.functionality: Provides basic filtering controls for the discussion list and maintains the filters object passed into getCategoryDiscussions.
data_binding: Local filters object (structure defined by the application, passed to getCategoryDiscussions.filters)
Current categorySlug and sortBy (read-only, used to inform the user but not changed here)
event_handlers: onChangeFilterControl: When a filter control (such as a toggle or select) is changed, update the local filters object accordingly and emit a filtersChanged event to category-discussions-init.
onClearFilters: Reset local filters to an empty state and emit filtersChanged so that getCategoryDiscussions is called without additional filters.

components.functionality: Handles search submissions from the header while the user is on category.html and navigates to the search results page.
data_binding: Search query input value (string, local to this component)
event_handlers: onSubmit: Prevent default form submission behavior, read the current search query, and navigate to search_results.html with a query URL parameter set to the entered search term.
onChangeQuery: Update internal query state as the user types, so that onSubmit can use the latest value.

layout.grid_system: Implicit centered column layout likely based on a 12‑column grid with a wide central content span (approx. 8–10 columns) and large left/right gutters.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a linear, top-to-bottom flow: a full-bleed, fixed black navigation bar at the very top; below it, a tall off-white header band that contains the category header on the left and a minimalist search control on the right. Beneath this band, keep a single centered content column where the filter bar and discussion toolbar sit as light horizontal rows, followed by a vertically stacked list of full-width discussion cards. The new discussion form appears as a card in the same central column (just above the list) when invoked, with the poll editor nested inside it as a secondary card-like block.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a linear, top-to-bottom flow: a full-bleed, fixed black navigation bar at the very top; below it, a tall off-white header band that contains the category header on the left and a minimalist search control on the right. Beneath this band, keep a single centered content column where the filter bar and discussion toolbar sit as light horizontal rows, followed by a vertically stacked list of full-width discussion cards. The new discussion form appears as a card in the same central column (just above the list) when invoked, with the poll editor nested inside it as a secondary card-like block.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Reserve the top ~8–10% of viewport height for the fixed black nav bar. Below that, allocate roughly 20–30% of the viewport height for the off-white category header band and open whitespace before the first discussion card. Keep left and right gutters at about 15–20% each, leaving a central content column of around 60–70% page width. Discussion cards and the new discussion form occupy the full width of this column with generous padding. Pagination sits at the bottom of the column with substantial vertical margins above and below.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain low density: few visible controls per row, large vertical gaps between sections (48–64px), and around 24–32px spacing between cards. Inside cards and forms, keep 24–32px padding and 16–24px between header rows and body content. Forms should feel airy, with full-width fields stacked vertically rather than dense multi-column layouts, except for optional poll options which may use a loose two-column grid at large widths.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Guide the eye from the dark, high-contrast nav bar at the top, left-to-right across nav items, then downward into the calm off-white header band. The left-aligned category title is the first focal point, then the gaze is pulled to the right by the small search icon. From there, the eye drops into the central content column, encountering the lightweight filter/toolbar row and then the large first discussion (or new discussion form) card as the main reading focus. Within each card, the eye moves left-to-right across the avatar and meta header row, then down through the main text, with a vertical ellipsis on the far right as the final interaction anchor.

layout.overall_description: The page presents as a quiet, editorial forum view: a solid black bar spans the very top edge, fixed in place, while all content below scrolls. Under this, a tall off-white band stretches the full width of the page, but the actual content is constrained to a centered column occupying about two-thirds of the page, flanked by wide empty gutters. On the left of this band, the category title and brief description sit with lots of breathing room; on the far right, a single minimalist search icon aligns visually with the heading. As you scroll down within this same central column, a spare filter row and a light toolbar row appear, then after a noticeable vertical gap the first large discussion card (or the new discussion form when open) fills most of the visible height. Cards are clean rectangles with generous internal padding, header info aligned left and a tiny overflow icon at the top right, followed by comfortably spaced text. Only one or two discussions are visible at a time, emphasizing reading rather than scanning. At the bottom, centered pagination controls float in ample white space, clearly marking the end of the list without crowding the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page-init component defines the full viewport and overall structure. A full-bleed black navigation bar sits at the very top, fixed in position, occupying about 8–10% of the viewport height. Below this bar, the rest of the page is a scrollable off-white canvas. Within this canvas, a single centered content column runs from just below the nav bar down to the bottom of the page. This column spans roughly 60–70% of the page width, leaving equal left and right gutters of about 15–20% width each that remain mostly empty to preserve the minimalist, editorial feel. All other components (category header, search, filter bar, toolbar, list, forms, pagination) are vertically stacked within this central column, separated by large vertical margins that conform to the 48–64px section spacing described in the design DNA.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Defines a fixed top navigation region with a scrollable main content area below; the centered content column remains horizontally centered at all viewport widths within a max-width constraint, while the background and gutters extend to the full browser width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The category header sits near the top of the main content column, directly under the black nav bar but with a noticeable 48–64px vertical gap to create a calm transition. It occupies the left two-thirds of the central column’s width, beginning at the left edge of the column. The category title appears as a large, bold line at the top, with any optional description text stacked below it, left-aligned and constrained to roughly 60% of the column width so that the right portion of the band stays mostly empty. The header as a whole forms part of a tall off-white band: above and below the text block there is generous empty vertical space, such that the band plus the empty area beneath it collectively account for about 20–30% of the viewport height before the first discussion card appears. On the same horizontal band, the header-search-form occupies the far right side, but the majority of the area between the heading and the search remains open, reinforcing the sparse editorial look.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the scrollable main content; acts as an anchoring section at the top of the central column with no sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The discussion toolbar is placed in the central column just below the filter-bar, with a vertical gap of about 16–24px above and 32–40px below it. It is a horizontal row that spans the full width of the content column but uses only a few discreet elements. On the far left, a small label such as “Sort by” with a simple dropdown or minimal control aligns with the left edge of the column. The middle portion of the row remains mostly empty, preserving the open feel. On the far right, a single, low-chrome “New discussion” button sits aligned to the column’s right edge; its hit area is around 32px tall but visually understated (e.g., simple outline or subtle fill, no heavy shadows). The toolbar does not form a boxed container; instead, it floats on the off-white background, reading as a light utility strip between the header/filter area and the discussion list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static horizontal row within the scrollable column; expands and contracts with the width of the central column while keeping its primary controls pinned to the extreme left and right edges of that column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The discussion list occupies the central portion of the page as a vertical stack of full-width cards inside the centered column. The first card appears only after a generous empty vertical gap (so that from the bottom of the black nav bar to the top of the first card is roughly 20–30% of the viewport height, including the category header, filter bar, and toolbar spacing). Each discussion card stretches from the left to the right edge of the content column, with internal padding of about 24–32px on all sides. Within each card, the top is a header row: on the left, a circular avatar (approximately 32–40px in diameter) is aligned flush with the card’s left padding; immediately to its right, author name and meta information (timestamp, reply count, etc.) are stacked or arranged in one or two lines, occupying roughly 70% of the card’s width. On the right side of this header row, aligned to the top-right corner of the card, sits a small vertical ellipsis icon with an invisible hit area of about 24–32px, providing a subtle affordance for more actions. Below the header row, a 16–24px gap leads into the discussion title and excerpt, which occupy the full width beneath, left aligned, with line lengths constrained by the central column. Cards are separated from one another by 24–32px of vertical space, ensuring that typically only one or two cards are fully visible at a time within the viewport, reinforcing the low-density, reading-focused feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, vertically flowing list within the central column; card widths respond to the column width but maintain consistent padding and inter-card spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Pagination controls are placed at the bottom of the central content column, directly under the last discussion card but separated by a generous 40–48px vertical gap. The controls themselves are centered horizontally within the column and occupy only a small fraction of its height. Individual page numbers and previous/next controls are laid out on a single line, with modest spacing between them (e.g., 8–16px), relying on typography and subtle emphasis rather than heavy containers or buttons. Above and below the pagination row there is enough whitespace (at least 32–48px below) that the controls feel like a soft terminus to the list rather than a crowded footer.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element anchored to the bottom of the discussion list; remains centered within the main column and scrolls naturally with the page content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The new discussion form presents as a large card in the same visual language as the discussion cards, occupying the full width of the central content column with 24–32px of internal padding on all sides. When active, it appears immediately below the discussion-toolbar and above the first discussion card, separated from each by approximately 32–40px of vertical space. The top of the form mirrors the discussion card header: on the left, the user’s avatar and a brief label such as “Start a new discussion” align with the card’s left padding; on the right, a small close or collapse icon aligns with the top-right corner in place of the overflow menu. Below this header row, a 16–24px gap introduces the form fields, which are stacked vertically: a full-width title input at the top, followed by a larger multi-line body textarea, then any category or tag selectors, each separated by 16–24px. The entire form is tall enough that it may occupy 50–70% of the viewport height when open, but its fields remain generously spaced and not cramped. The poll-config-editor, when used, appears as a nested block near the bottom of this form, full-width, visually grouped but still minimal.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears inline within the central column as an expanded card when triggered, pushing the discussion list downward; in its inactive state it is represented only by the toolbar action and does not occupy vertical space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The poll config editor sits inside the new discussion form as a subordinate block, aligned to the same left and right padding as the form’s other fields. It is introduced below the main body textarea and any basic metadata fields, with a 24px gap above to mark it as a separate sub-section. The top row of this subform contains a left-aligned label such as “Poll” and, optionally, a small toggle or remove icon right-aligned in this row. Below, the poll question input spans the full width of the card, followed by poll option fields arranged in a loose grid: on larger screens, options are laid out in two equal-width columns (each about 48% of the card width, with a small gap between), while on smaller screens they stack in a single column to preserve readability. Each option field has 8–16px vertical spacing from the next. The subform has no heavy border, but may be separated from the rest of the form by a light top rule or slightly increased vertical spacing, maintaining the overall minimal monochrome aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Embedded dynamically within the new-discussion-form; appears and disappears inline based on whether a poll is being configured, without affecting the layout of elements outside the form.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter bar is a slim horizontal row located within the central column between the category-header band and the discussion-toolbar, with about 24–32px vertical spacing above and below. It spans the full width of the column but contains only a few minimal controls on the left: for example, a label like “Filters” followed by a small number of pill-shaped filter chips or a dropdown, all left-aligned. The center and right portions of the row remain mostly empty, emphasizing the low-density look. Controls have generous hit areas (24–32px tall) but are visually light (thin outlines, subtle text), allowing the eye to glide past them unless the user is specifically looking to refine the list. The filter bar does not have its own background panel; it sits directly on the off-white background.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static bar within the scrollable central column; its content aligns to the left edge of the column and wraps gracefully on smaller widths while maintaining generous vertical margins from neighboring sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header search form lives in the same horizontal band as the category-header, anchored to the far right edge of the central content column. Visually, it is reduced to a single search icon or a very short input-plus-icon, approximately 24–32px tall, with a wide invisible hit area to keep it easy to use while maintaining visual minimalism. It aligns vertically with either the baseline of the category title or the middle of the heading block, depending on the exact typography, so that the title on the left and the search icon on the right form a clean, balanced row. The space between the left-aligned heading text and the right-aligned search remains largely empty, reinforcing the airy, editorial feel. When expanded (on interaction), the input field extends leftward from the icon but stays within the width of the central column, without adding extra height to the header band.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the scrollable header band; right-aligned within the central column and may expand horizontally on focus while staying within the header’s vertical space.

name: Discussion Thread
title: Discussion Thread
description: Displays a single discussion with its original post, replies, poll (if any), and tools for replying, reacting, reporting, subscribing, bookmarking, and navigating within the thread.

core_features: Load and display a single discussion thread using the discussionId URL parameter via getDiscussionThread.
Show the original post with title, body, metadata, tags, and current upvote score.
Display all replies in either chronological or threaded view, based on a page-level view mode.
Allow users to add a new top-level reply to the discussion using replyToDiscussion.
Allow users to reply inline to a specific post using replyToPost, with support for inserting @mentions into the reply body.
Provide upvote and like controls on the original post and replies using upvotePost and likePost.
Allow users to subscribe/unsubscribe to the discussion’s notifications via setDiscussionSubscription.
Allow users to bookmark/unbookmark the discussion via setDiscussionBookmark.
Provide per-post actions including Reply, Like, Upvote, and Report/Flag, with report submission via reportPost.
If a poll is attached, allow users to vote and see updated poll results using voteInPoll, respecting Poll.multipleChoiceAllowed and Poll.startAt / Poll.endAt / Poll.isClosed.
Display tags for the discussion and navigate to the related topic page (topic.html) when a tag is clicked using URL parameters.
Enable navigation to user_profile.html when clicking a post author’s name or avatar using the authorProfileId.
Include a header search form that navigates to search_results.html with a query parameter.
Provide a breadcrumb/title control that returns the view to the top of the thread (scroll-to-top behavior).
user_workflows: View a discussion thread: 1) User navigates to discussion.html with a discussionId URL parameter. 2) On page load, thread-loader reads discussionId and calls getDiscussionThread. 3) The page displays the discussion title, category, tags, original post, poll (if hasPoll is true), and replies based on the returned data. 4) User can scroll through replies, switch between chronological and threaded view if the view mode control is used, and jump back to the top via the thread title link.

Post a new top-level reply: 1) User scrolls to the main reply editor at the bottom (or a fixed location). 2) User enters reply text, optionally including @mentions. 3) On submit, main-reply-editor calls replyToDiscussion with the current discussionId and body. 4) On success, the newPost is appended to the replies list and updatedReplyCount is applied to the Discussion replyCount. 5) The page may scroll to the newly created reply’s position in the thread.

Reply to a specific post with inline editor: 1) User clicks Reply on a particular reply or the original post. 2) inline-reply-editor opens directly under that post, pre-filling an @mention token if desired based on the authorDisplayName. 3) User writes the reply and submits. 4) inline-reply-editor calls replyToPost with parentPostId and body. 5) On success, newPost is inserted as a child of the parent post in threaded view (or at the appropriate position in chronological view), and updatedParentReplyCount is applied to the parent post’s replyCount.
