Upvote or like a post: 1) User clicks the Upvote control on the original post or a reply. 2) reaction-controls determines whether this is an add or remove action and calls upvotePost with postId and the desired upvote boolean. 3) On success, newUpvoteCount updates the post’s upvoteCount and userHasUpvoted updates the visual state. 4) For likes, user clicks Like; reaction-controls calls likePost with postId and like boolean. 5) On success, newLikeCount and userHasLiked update the post’s state.

Subscribe or unsubscribe to the discussion: 1) User toggles the subscription control at the thread level. 2) thread-subscription-toggle uses setDiscussionSubscription with discussionId and subscribed boolean. 3) On success, the returned Discussion object updates discussion.isSubscribed and a status message is optionally displayed.

Bookmark or unbookmark the discussion: 1) User toggles the bookmark control at the thread level. 2) thread-bookmark-toggle calls setDiscussionBookmark with discussionId and bookmarked boolean. 3) On success, discussion.isBookmarked is updated from the returned Discussion.

Report/flag a post (e.g., spam): 1) User clicks Report/Flag on a specific post. 2) report-modal opens, showing reason options based on ContentReport.reason values and an optional note field. 3) User selects a reason (such as spam) and optionally enters a note. 4) On submit, report-modal calls reportPost with postId, reason, and note. 5) On success, the returned ContentReport and message are used to confirm submission; the modal closes and the post’s UI can mark that it has been reported by the current user if desired.

Vote in an attached poll and view results: 1) If discussion.hasPoll is true and poll exists in the loaded data, poll-panel displays the poll question and options. 2) If the poll is not closed (based on Poll.isClosed and Poll.startAt / Poll.endAt), user selects one or more options in the UI. 3) For a single-choice poll, selecting one option and submitting causes poll-panel to call voteInPoll once with pollId and optionId. 4) For a poll where multipleChoiceAllowed is true, poll-panel calls voteInPoll separately for each selected option when the user submits. 5) On each successful response, options returned by voteInPoll update the PollOption.voteCount values and pollResponse updates the user’s PollResponse. 6) Poll results (e.g., percentages or counts) are updated immediately to reflect the current totals.

Navigate to a member profile or topic page: 1) User clicks an author’s display name or avatar associated with a Post. 2) author-link reads authorProfileId and navigates to user_profile.html with memberProfileId set to that value. 3) User clicks a tag displayed under the discussion title. 4) tag-list reads the Tag.id of the selected tag and navigates to topic.html with tagId set accordingly.

Use search from within the thread: 1) User enters a search term in the header-search-form. 2) On submit, header-search-form navigates to search_results.html with a query URL parameter containing the entered search term.

Return to thread top using title link: 1) User scrolls down the page. 2) User clicks the thread title or a dedicated back-to-top control in thread-breadcrumbs. 3) thread-top-link scrolls to the top of the discussion (original post position) without changing the discussionId.
interactions: On page load or when discussionId in the URL changes, thread-loader calls getDiscussionThread and initializes child components with the returned discussion, category, tags, originalPost, replies, poll, pollOptions, and pollResponse.
Clicking the thread title or breadcrumb top link triggers a scroll-to-top behavior, bringing the original post into view.
Clicking an author’s display name or avatar triggers navigation to user_profile.html with memberProfileId set to the clicked post’s authorProfileId.
Clicking a tag under the discussion title triggers navigation to topic.html with tagId set to the clicked tag’s id.
Submitting the header search form triggers navigation to search_results.html with query set to the entered search term.

Typing text into main-reply-editor or inline-reply-editor updates local form state for reply body; clicking submit calls replyToDiscussion or replyToPost respectively and, on success, merges newPost into the replies collection.
Clicking Reply on a post toggles visibility of inline-reply-editor for that post and can insert an @mention token for the target author into the input.
Clicking Upvote on a post toggles the upvoted state and calls upvotePost; on success, the post’s upvoteCount and current user upvoted state are updated based on the response.
Clicking Like on a post toggles the liked state and calls likePost; on success, the post’s likeCount and current user liked state are updated based on the response.
Toggling the subscription control calls setDiscussionSubscription; on success, the discussion.isSubscribed binding updates and any related UI indicators change.
Toggling the bookmark control calls setDiscussionBookmark; on success, the discussion.isBookmarked binding updates and any related UI indicators change.
Clicking Report/Flag on a post opens report-modal pre-bound to the relevant postId.
Selecting a report reason and submitting the report-modal calls reportPost; on success, a confirmation message is shown and the modal closes.
Selecting poll options and submitting within poll-panel calls voteInPoll for each relevant option; on success, PollOption.voteCount and PollResponse state update and the visual results are refreshed.
Changing the reply view mode control (e.g., threaded vs chronological) updates local state in reply-list and re-renders the replies using the same replies data without calling additional interfaces.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary state driver for this page is the discussionId URL parameter. When discussion.html is loaded, thread-loader reads discussionId from the query string. If discussionId is missing or invalid, the page should show an error state and avoid calling getDiscussionThread. When a valid discussionId is present, getDiscussionThread is called exactly once per page load (or again if discussionId changes, such as via navigation). The returned Discussion, Category, Tag, Post, Poll, PollOption, and PollResponse objects populate the page-level state that is passed down to child components.

Additional view state such as reply ordering (e.g., chronological vs threaded), current reply page, or the currently expanded inline reply editor can be held in client-side state and may optionally be reflected in additional URL parameters (for example, viewMode and focusPostId). If these parameters are used, changing them should not call new interfaces; they only control how the already loaded replies list from getDiscussionThread is rendered. For example, if viewMode is set in the URL, reply-list reads it when initializing and chooses the appropriate rendering strategy.

After user actions that call interfaces (replyToDiscussion, replyToPost, upvotePost, likePost, setDiscussionSubscription, setDiscussionBookmark, reportPost, voteInPoll), the page does not rely on a full reload; instead, it updates the existing state with the returned values. For example, replyToDiscussion returns newPost and updatedReplyCount, which are merged into the existing replies and Discussion.replyCount. Upvote and like actions update only the corresponding Post’s counts and current user state. Subscription and bookmark actions update the existing Discussion object. Poll changes rely on the options and pollResponse returned from voteInPoll rather than re-calling getDiscussionThread. All persisted state is controlled through these interfaces, while temporary UI state (open editors, modal visibility, scroll position) is managed on the client and is not saved in localStorage.

components.functionality: Initializes the discussion thread view by reading discussionId from the URL, calling getDiscussionThread, and distributing the returned data to child components.
Displays the discussion title, optional category label, and provides a control (title link or breadcrumb) that scrolls the view to the top of the thread.
Captures a search term from the user and navigates to search_results.html with the query URL parameter.

Shows the original post including title, body, timestamps, upvote and like counts, author, and attaches reaction-controls, thread-subscription-toggle, thread-bookmark-toggle, poll-panel (if a poll exists), and tag-list.
Displays tags associated with the discussion and handles navigation to topic.html when a tag is clicked.
Renders a clickable author name or avatar for a post and navigates to user_profile.html using the authorProfileId.
Provides upvote and like controls for a post, calling upvotePost and likePost to toggle the current user’s reaction and update the post’s reaction counts.
Toggles the user’s subscription to the current discussion by calling setDiscussionSubscription and updating discussion.isSubscribed.
Toggles the user’s bookmark state for the discussion by calling setDiscussionBookmark and updating discussion.isBookmarked.

Displays an attached poll with its options, determines whether the poll is open or closed, allows the user to select options, and submits votes using voteInPoll, then updates poll results and the user’s pollResponse.
Renders all replies for the discussion, supports switching between chronological and threaded views using local state, and instantiates reply-item for each reply post.
Displays a single reply, including author, body, reaction-controls, per-post Reply and Report actions, reply count, and an inline-reply-editor when the user chooses to reply to this post.
Provides a form to add a new top-level reply to the discussion using replyToDiscussion; on success, injects the newPost into reply-list and updates the discussion’s replyCount.
Provides an inline reply form under a specific parent post, optionally pre-populated with an @mention token, and calls replyToPost to create a child reply.
Collects a report reason and optional note for a specific post and submits a content report via reportPost, then displays confirmation based on the returned ContentReport and message.
Scrolls the viewport to the top of the discussion (original post) when activated, without changing the current URL parameters.

layout.grid_system: Implicit centered column layout likely based on a 12‑column grid with a wide central content span (approx. 8–10 columns) and large left/right gutters.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a single, centered content column beneath a full‑width, black global navigation bar (outside this component list). Inside the thread page, all elements flow linearly from top to bottom: a spacious off‑white header band containing breadcrumbs on the left and a compact search form plus thread-level toggles on the right; then the main original post as a large, prominent card; followed (if present) by a poll card; then a vertical stack of reply cards; finally the main reply editor at the bottom. Utility elements like loaders, report modal, and 'back to top' control sit on top of or adjacent to this central column while preserving its integrity.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a single, centered content column beneath a full‑width, black global navigation bar (outside this component list). Inside the thread page, all elements flow linearly from top to bottom: a spacious off‑white header band containing breadcrumbs on the left and a compact search form plus thread-level toggles on the right; then the main original post as a large, prominent card; followed (if present) by a poll card; then a vertical stack of reply cards; finally the main reply editor at the bottom. Utility elements like loaders, report modal, and 'back to top' control sit on top of or adjacent to this central column while preserving its integrity.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The black navigation bar occupies the top ~8–10% of the viewport. Below it, the off‑white header band spans full width and uses generous vertical padding (~48–64px total) before the first card. The centered content column occupies roughly 60–70% of the viewport width, with symmetrical left/right gutters of ~15–20% each. Cards (original post, poll, replies, main editor) span the full width of this content column with 24–32px internal padding and 24–32px vertical gaps between them. Vertical whitespace is intentionally large so that only the thread header area and one major card are typically in view at once on standard laptop screens.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain low density across the page: few controls per screen, large padding inside cards, and clear separation between functional areas. Text lines within posts have comfortable line height and are not crowded by controls. Actions (reactions, subscribe, bookmark, reply) are grouped into thin, unobtrusive rows beneath content rather than surrounding it, reducing visual noise. Inline elements like author links and tags are light and understated, prioritizing the reading experience over heavy chrome or iconography.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye lands first on the dark navigation bar, then drops into the light off‑white header band. Breadcrumbs on the left establish context; the eye then travels to the right to find the search field and thread-level toggles. From there, the large, centered original-post card pulls attention down into the body text. Within cards, the flow is left‑to‑right across the avatar and author meta, then down through the content, finishing on subtle action controls along the bottom edge. Secondary anchors on the right edge—the header search, subscription/bookmark toggles, overflow actions, the scroll-to-top link, and the report modal trigger—provide lateral balance without disrupting the dominant vertical reading path.

layout.overall_description: The Discussion Thread page is a quiet, centered column layered under a stark black navigation bar. Beneath the nav, a wide off‑white header band stretches across the viewport with ample breathing room, holding slim breadcrumbs on the left and a compact, icon‑lean search form with thread subscription and bookmark toggles on the right. The eye is then drawn down into a single, large card: the original post, which fills about two‑thirds of the page width, framed by generous left and right gutters of empty space. Inside this card, a simple two‑column header presents the avatar and author meta on the left and a minimal overflow icon on the right, with the body text flowing calmly beneath. If the thread contains a poll, a second, slightly smaller card follows, mirroring the same centered width and padding. Below, a column of reply cards continues this rhythm—each a pared‑back version of the original post layout—leading down to a full‑width reply editor card at the bottom. A subtle scroll‑to‑top control hovers near the lower right edge of the content column, while occasional overlays (the loader and report modal) appear centered over the column, dimming or softening the background. The overall effect is that of a clean editorial layout: a single reading channel down the middle of the screen, with minimal chrome, ample whitespace, and a gentle vertical progression from context, to main content, to conversation, to participation.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The thread loader appears as a minimalist overlay centered over the main content column, activating whenever thread data is being fetched. It covers the vertical region from just below the black navigation bar down to slightly past the fold, but remains constrained horizontally to the same 60–70% content width as the thread cards, keeping the gutters lightly visible. Inside this overlay, a small, unobtrusive loading indicator is centered both horizontally and vertically within the column, with large empty off‑white space around it to preserve the calm aesthetic. When inactive, the loader is fully hidden and does not take up space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily overlays the central content column with a semi‑opaque off‑white layer; positioned fixed within the viewport, horizontally aligned to the content column and vertically centered.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumbs sit within the off‑white header band, aligned to the left edge of the central content column. They start approximately 48px below the bottom edge of the black navigation bar, creating a large visual gap between nav and content. The breadcrumb text runs horizontally but is kept short and low‑contrast, occupying only the top-left portion of the header band and leaving plenty of empty space below and to the right. They do not span the full width; instead, they hug the left margin of the content column with about 8–16px of vertical space beneath before any thread title/subtitle content or before the first card if no title is shown.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block at the top of the content column within the header band; scrolls away naturally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header search form is positioned on the far right side of the off‑white header band, horizontally aligned with the breadcrumbs but anchored to the right edge of the central content column. Visually it appears as a minimal icon‑driven control: a small search icon (and, if needed, a slim input) with an invisible hit area of about 24–32px square. It floats in the ample negative space of the header band, with roughly 48–64px of whitespace above and below, serving as a subtle counterweight to the breadcrumbs. The form does not extend across the column; it occupies only about 10–15% of the column width, leaving a clear expanse of off‑white space between it and the breadcrumbs.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, right‑aligned within the header band; scrolls normally with the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The original post panel is the first and largest card in the main content column, appearing directly beneath the header band with a generous vertical gap of about 48–64px. It spans the full width of the central column (roughly 60–70% of the viewport width) and is horizontally centered, leaving wide off‑white gutters on both sides. Inside, a two‑column header row sits at the top: on the left, the avatar, author link, post meta, and tags arranged in a compact vertical/horizontal cluster; on the right, a small overflow (more) icon aligned to the top edge. This header row uses about 24px of vertical height. Below, there is a gap of 16–24px before the post body text, which flows in a single full‑width column with comfortable line spacing and margins. At the bottom of the card, a thin, understated horizontal area contains reaction controls and any thread-level actions associated with the original post, separated from the text by another 16–24px of vertical space. Overall card padding is approximately 24–32px on all sides, giving the post a calm, spacious presence that occupies roughly half to two‑thirds of the viewport height on first view.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, central card within the scrollable page; drives the main vertical rhythm of the layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tag list appears within the header region of the original post panel, positioned directly below or to the right of the author meta on the left side of the header row. Tags are laid out in a horizontal row that wraps if necessary, but visually they occupy only the left half of the card header, never reaching the overflow icon on the right. Each tag is small and text‑based with subtle styling, separated by 8px gaps. There is about 8–12px of vertical space between the main author/title line and the first row of tags, keeping them visually subordinate. The tag list does not extend beyond the padded bounds of the card and does not add substantial height; it simply enriches the top of the original post without compromising the minimalist feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the original post header; flows with the header's left column and wraps as needed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The author link is a text element directly associated with the avatar in both the original post panel and each reply item. In the original post, it sits to the immediate right of the avatar within the left side of the header row, forming a horizontal cluster: avatar on the far left, author name next, followed by subtle metadata (time, role) in lighter text. The author name itself is slightly bolder, but overall remains modest to fit the editorial tone. Vertically, this cluster is centered within the header row height. In reply items, the author link appears in the same relative position but at a slightly smaller scale, keeping the original post visually dominant.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the header rows of post panels; no independent spatial behavior beyond text flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Reaction controls appear as a horizontal strip of small icons and text labels placed at the bottom of each post card (original and replies). They are left‑aligned within the card’s inner padding, starting about 16–24px below the last line of post content. The controls form a single row—like and other reactions, reply, share—spaced evenly with 16–24px between each item, and they never extend beyond half to two‑thirds of the card width, leaving empty space toward the right side. Their visual weight is light: thin icons, small type, no heavy borders. This placement creates a clear end to the reading flow for each card, offering interaction options without visually competing with the text.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within each card; anchored to the bottom edge of the card’s content area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The thread subscription toggle is placed in the top header band, near the header search form on the right side of the content column. It appears as a compact icon or small text+icon pill aligned horizontally with the search control, usually to its immediate left, sharing the same 24–32px invisible hit area. The toggle cluster (subscription + bookmark + search) occupies roughly the rightmost 20–25% of the central column, with ample blank space separating it from the breadcrumbs on the left. There is no heavy button styling; instead, a simple state change (e.g., subtle underline or icon fill) indicates whether the user is subscribed.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, right‑aligned in the header band; scrolls with the rest of the header content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The thread bookmark toggle sits directly adjacent to the subscription toggle in the header band’s right‑aligned control cluster, typically between the subscription icon and the search control or just to the left of search. It shares the same compact, icon‑first minimal styling and 24–32px hit area. Vertically, it aligns along the same baseline as the subscription toggle and search icon, forming a clean horizontal row. Because of the large amount of surrounding whitespace in the header band, this small trio of controls reads as a subtle, lightweight control strip rather than a dense toolbar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, part of the right‑aligned utility row in the header band; scrolls with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When present, the poll panel appears as its own card directly beneath the original post panel, separated by a vertical gap of about 24–32px. It uses the same centered width and card styling—spanning the full central column with 24–32px padding—so it visually aligns with the original post and reply cards. Internally, the poll layout is columnar: a short poll question at the top, followed by vertically stacked options with large clickable areas and comfortable spacing (16–24px between options). A compact results/status line and action (e.g., vote/change vote) appear at the bottom with subdued styling. The poll card is slightly shorter than the original post card, typically occupying one‑third to half of the viewport height, ensuring it doesn’t overshadow the main post but is clearly associated with it in the vertical flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static card in the central column, maintaining the same alignment and margins as post cards.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reply list occupies the central column beneath the original post (and poll, if present), starting about 32–40px below the preceding card. It is structured as a vertical stack of reply-item cards, each aligned perfectly with the original post card edges. The list stretches downward, extending beyond the initial viewport and creating the main scrollable content. Between each reply item card there is a consistent gap of 24–32px, preserving the airy rhythm. The list itself does not have additional borders or background; it relies on the individual cards and the central alignment to create structure within the off‑white page background.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static vertical container; scrolls as the main body of the thread beneath the original post.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each reply item is a card mirroring the original post panel but with slightly reduced vertical bulk. It spans the full width of the central column, centered with the same gutters. At the top of the card, a header row matches the two‑column structure: avatar, author link, and reply meta on the left; a subtle overflow icon on the right. This header is followed by a 16–24px gap and then the reply body text in a single full‑width column. At the bottom, reaction controls and a compact 'reply' trigger are arranged in a light horizontal strip similar to the original post. Replies that have an inline reply editor open have that editor appear directly below the reaction controls, inside the card’s vertical flow, increasing the card height but maintaining the same left/right padding. Overall, each reply card occupies roughly one‑third of the viewport height, with enough whitespace that only one or two replies are fully visible at a time.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static card within the reply list; height expands when inline reply editors are shown.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The main reply editor is the final major card in the central column, appearing beneath the last reply with a larger vertical gap of about 40–48px to clearly indicate the transition from reading to composing. It spans the full width of the content column and is centered like the other cards. Inside, a simple vertical structure prevails: at the top, a small label or heading ('Write a reply') aligned to the left; below it, a large, single-column text area taking up most of the card’s height, with comfortable padding around all sides so the text does not touch the edges. Minimal formatting controls may appear in a slim row either above or below the text area, kept low contrast and limited in count. The card typically occupies about half the viewport height when focused, reinforcing its importance without feeling cramped.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static card anchored at the bottom of the thread content; scrolls into view as the user reaches the end of replies.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The inline reply editor appears on demand within a specific reply-item card. When activated, it is inserted directly beneath that reply’s reaction controls, maintaining the card’s internal left and right padding. The editor consists of a smaller text area and minimal controls, arranged in a single vertical column similar to the main reply editor but with reduced height—typically occupying a quarter to a third of the viewport height. There is a 16–24px gap separating the existing reply content from the inline editor, and another 16px gap below the editor before the bottom of the card, preserving breathing room. This keeps the context of the parent reply visually obvious while allowing the user to compose inline.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Contextual, expanding the height of the associated reply-item card; appears and disappears without affecting the alignment of other cards.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The report modal appears as a centered overlay floating above the entire page content. It is a compact rectangular panel occupying roughly 30–40% of the viewport width and about 30% of the viewport height, with off‑white background and subtle shadow or border. Horizontally, it is centered with respect to the full viewport, but its inner content aligns with the same minimalist vertical structure as the cards: a short title at the top, a single-column form (reason dropdown, text area) in the middle, and a sparse row of actions (Submit, Cancel) at the bottom, all with 24px internal padding. Behind the modal, the rest of the page is dimmed with a semi‑transparent dark layer that extends edge to edge, while the main navigation and content are visually deactivated but still visible, reinforcing the modal’s focus without breaking the overall aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed position overlay centered in the viewport; blocks interaction with underlying content until dismissed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The thread-top link is a small, unobtrusive control positioned near the lower right edge of the central content column. On larger screens it appears as a subtle text+icon or icon-only button that hovers just inside the right gutter of the column, roughly 24–32px above the bottom of the viewport. Its hit area is about 32px square, but visually it remains delicate, with no heavy background. As the user scrolls down into the replies, this control stays in a consistent vertical position relative to the viewport, acting as a quiet affordance to jump back to the top. It never touches the page edge; instead, it aligns optically with the right padding of the cards, preserving the centered column’s geometry.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky/fixed relative to the viewport, anchored to the right side of the central column near the bottom; appears after scrolling past the original post.

name: Search Results
title: Search Results
description: Displays discussion threads that match a text query, with filtering, sorting, and pagination. Users can refine results via a filter panel, change sort order, and open any discussion thread from the list.

core_features: Execute discussion searches via the searchDiscussions interface using the current URL parameters (query, dateRangeId, minReplies, sortBy, page).
Retrieve available filter options (date ranges and reply count presets) via the getSearchFilterOptions interface and render them in a filter panel.
Display a paginated list of discussion search results, each showing key metadata (title, category name, tag names, author display name, snippet, and discussion metrics from Discussion).
Provide a filter panel to narrow results by date range and minimum reply count, updating the URL parameters and re-running searchDiscussions.
Provide a sorting control to sort search results by criteria (e.g., relevance, newest, most_upvoted) via the sortBy parameter passed to searchDiscussions.
Provide pagination controls that update the page URL parameter and re-run searchDiscussions.
Allow users to open a discussion by clicking its title, navigating to discussion.html with the appropriate discussionId parameter.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Run a search from another page
1. User types a query into the header search bar on any page (e.g., index.html, categories.html).
2. User submits the search form in the header.
3. The application navigates to search_results.html with the query parameter set in the URL.
4. On initialization, the Search Results page reads query (and any other existing URL parameters), calls getSearchFilterOptions, then calls searchDiscussions with:
   - query: value from URL.
   - filters: object built from dateRangeId and minReplies URL parameters (if present).
   - sortBy: value from URL (if present) or omitted/left to backend default.
   - page: value from URL (if present) or initial page provided by the application.
   - pageSize: a page-size value supplied by the application configuration.
5. The page displays the results list, total result count, and current filters/sort state derived from the URL and API responses.

Workflow 2: Refine results using filters
1. User is on search_results.html with an existing query and visible results.
2. The filter panel is populated using the response from getSearchFilterOptions (dateRanges and replyCountPresets).
3. User selects a date range option.
4. The filter panel constructs a new URL for search_results.html by updating/adding dateRangeId to the selected option's id, preserving query and any other parameters, and resetting page to 1.
5. The browser navigates to the updated URL.
6. On page initialization, search_results.html reads the updated URL, then calls searchDiscussions with filters including the new dateRangeId.
7. The results list updates to show only discussions matching the selected date range.
8. Optionally, user selects a minimum reply count preset. The panel updates minReplies in the URL in the same way, navigates, and the search is re-run.

Workflow 3: Change sort order
1. User is viewing search results and opens the sorting dropdown.
2. User chooses a sort option (e.g., relevance, newest, most_upvoted) that maps directly to a sortBy value used by searchDiscussions.
3. The sorting control updates/sets the sortBy URL parameter, preserves query and existing filters (dateRangeId, minReplies), sets page to 1, and navigates to the updated URL.
4. On initialization, the page uses the new sortBy value when calling searchDiscussions.
5. Results are displayed in the new sort order.

Workflow 4: Paginate through results
1. User is viewing search results and scrolls to the bottom of the list where pagination controls are shown.
2. User clicks a page number, "Next", or "Previous" control.
3. The pagination component updates the page parameter in the URL (e.g., page=2), preserving query, dateRangeId, minReplies, and sortBy, and navigates to the updated URL.
4. On initialization, the page calls searchDiscussions with the new page value.
5. The results list shows the appropriate subset of discussions for that page.

Workflow 5: Open a discussion from search results
1. User scans the results list and identifies a discussion of interest.
2. User clicks the discussion title in the result item.
3. The results list component triggers navigation to discussion.html with discussionId set to the clicked discussion's Discussion.id.
4. The discussion page loads and shows the full thread for that discussion.

Workflow 6: Clear filters and return to unfiltered search for the same query
1. User has an active dateRangeId and/or minReplies filter.
2. User clicks a "Clear filters" action in the filters summary or filter panel.
3. The filters component constructs a URL for search_results.html containing query and any sortBy parameter, but removes dateRangeId and minReplies parameters, and sets page to 1.
4. Browser navigates to the new URL.
5. On initialization, the page calls searchDiscussions without dateRangeId or minReplies in the filters object, effectively returning to an unfiltered search for the current query.
interactions:
Interaction: Initial page load / URL change
- Trigger: Browser navigates to search_results.html with some combination of query, dateRangeId, minReplies, sortBy, page.
- Behavior: A controller component reads all URL parameters, calls getSearchFilterOptions, then calls searchDiscussions with parameters built from the URL. The responses are distributed to child components (filters, results list, pagination, summary). Any component that displays selections (e.g., selected date range) uses the URL parameters as the single source of truth.

Interaction: Change date range filter
- Trigger: User selects a different date range option in the filter panel.
- Behavior: The filter panel reads the selected date range's id, merges it into the existing query parameters, sets page to 1, and updates the browser location to search_results.html with the new dateRangeId in the URL. A full page re-initialization then re-fetches results via searchDiscussions.

Interaction: Change minimum replies filter
- Trigger: User selects a minimum reply count preset in the filter panel.
- Behavior: The filter panel reads the preset's minReplies value, updates/sets the minReplies URL parameter, preserves query, dateRangeId, and sortBy, sets page to 1, and navigates to the new URL. The page reload triggers a new call to searchDiscussions with the updated filters.

Interaction: Clear all filters
- Trigger: User clicks a "Clear filters" control.
- Behavior: The filters summary or panel constructs a URL that preserves query (and optionally sortBy) but removes dateRangeId and minReplies. It sets page to 1 and navigates to this URL. The page re-executes searchDiscussions with no filter constraints.

Interaction: Change sort order
- Trigger: User selects a sort option from the sort dropdown.
- Behavior: The sort control updates the sortBy parameter in the URL to the chosen value, preserves query and active filters (dateRangeId, minReplies), sets page to 1, and navigates to the updated URL. The page initialization then calls searchDiscussions with the new sortBy value.

Interaction: Pagination
- Trigger: User clicks on a pagination control (page number, next, previous).
- Behavior: The pagination component calculates the new page value and updates the page URL parameter while preserving query, dateRangeId, minReplies, and sortBy. The browser navigates to the updated URL, causing a fresh call to searchDiscussions with the new page.

Interaction: Open discussion
- Trigger: User clicks on a discussion title (or primary clickable area) in a search result item.
- Behavior: The results list component navigates to discussion.html with the discussionId URL parameter set to the Discussion.id of the clicked result.

Interaction: Modify query via header search bar
- Trigger: User types a new query in the global header search bar while on search_results.html and submits.
- Behavior: The global search form navigates to search_results.html with query set to the entered string and omits filter and sort parameters unless explicitly carried over by the application. The page reinitializes and calls searchDiscussions with the new query.

state_logic": "The Search Results page uses URL parameters as the single source of truth for search state, ensuring that every reload or shared link reconstructs the same search context.

- query (required for meaningful search):
  - Must be present for searchDiscussions to be called with user intent.
  - On page initialization, if query is present, it is passed directly to searchDiscussions.
  - Components displaying the current search term (e.g., summary) bind to this URL value.

- dateRangeId (optional filter):
  - When present, it is included in the filters object passed to searchDiscussions (e.g., filters.dateRangeId = dateRangeId).
  - The filter panel uses dateRangeId to mark the corresponding date range option as selected.
  - When removed from the URL (e.g., via Clear filters), it is omitted from filters when calling searchDiscussions.

- minReplies (optional filter):
  - When present, it is included in the filters object passed to searchDiscussions (e.g., filters.minReplies = minReplies).
  - The filter panel uses this value to mark the corresponding replyCountPresets option as selected (matching on preset.minReplies).
  - When removed from the URL, it is omitted from filters when calling searchDiscussions.

- sortBy (optional sort key):
  - When present, it is passed as the sortBy parameter to searchDiscussions.
  - The sort control uses this parameter to highlight the active sort option.
  - If absent, the component may show a default sort label that reflects the backend default; sortBy is then omitted or passed as an undefined value to searchDiscussions.

- page (optional pagination index):
  - When present, it is passed as the page parameter to searchDiscussions.
  - The pagination component uses it as the current page index.
  - If missing, the page uses an initial page index provided by the application configuration and does not add page to the URL unless needed.

State transitions:
- Any user action that changes filters or sort order constructs a new URL by modifying only the relevant parameter (dateRangeId, minReplies, sortBy, or page) while preserving the others (including query). Page is reset to 1 for filter and sort changes.
- Navigation to the updated URL is the mechanism that triggers any new search; no persistent state is stored in localStorage or other client-side storage.
- All downstream components consume state only through API responses and URL parameters, not from local component state for core search variables.

layout.grid_system: Implicit centered column layout likely based on a 12‑column grid with a wide central content span (approx. 8–10 columns) and large left/right gutters.
layout.layout_pattern: Apply the mockup’s linear, top‑to‑bottom flow: a full‑width, fixed‑height black navigation bar at the very top; directly beneath it, a tall off‑white header band that contains the page title and query summary aligned to the left and a subtle search/filters entry point aligned to the right; below that, a centered main content column that holds vertically stacked search‑result thread cards. Within each card, preserve the two‑column header row (thread starter avatar + meta on the left, overflow/quick actions on the right) followed by a single full‑width text area that summarizes the thread.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Apply the mockup’s linear, top‑to‑bottom flow: a full‑width, fixed‑height black navigation bar at the very top; directly beneath it, a tall off‑white header band that contains the page title and query summary aligned to the left and a subtle search/filters entry point aligned to the right; below that, a centered main content column that holds vertically stacked search‑result thread cards. Within each card, preserve the two‑column header row (thread starter avatar + meta on the left, overflow/quick actions on the right) followed by a single full‑width text area that summarizes the thread.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Reserve roughly the top 8–10% of the viewport for the black nav bar. Beneath it, allocate 20–30% of the viewport height to the off‑white header band with generous empty space above and below the search context elements. The main result list occupies the central band of the page (about 50–60% of viewport height as you scroll) with a single, prominent card in view at a time. Horizontally, maintain wide gutters: about 15–20% of page width on each side, leaving a central content column at about 60–70% width for the cards. Vertical spacing between major bands (nav → header → list) follows the large 48–64px gaps, with 24–32px gaps between individual cards.
layout_strategies.content_density: Keep content intentionally sparse. Limit the number of visible controls in the header band (e.g., minimal text for filters or sort, a single understated search icon) and avoid dense filter panels or multi‑row toolbars. Each search result card uses generous internal padding (24–32px on all sides), a single concise metadata line, and a short excerpt, rather than compact multi‑line metadata blocks. Maintain ample white space between cards so that only one or two cards are comfortably legible in the initial viewport, reinforcing a calm, reading‑focused experience rather than a dense list.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Guide the eye from the strong black navigation bar at the top (left‑to‑right across key nav items) down into the quiet off‑white header band. Within that band, the left‑aligned page title and query summary act as the primary anchor; a right‑aligned search icon and subtle ‘filters’ or ‘sort’ affordance provide a secondary anchor that pulls attention laterally to the right. From there, the gaze naturally drops into the centered search‑result card column. Within each card, the eye first registers the avatar and thread title/meta on the left of the header row, then scans right toward the overflow icon for additional actions, and finally moves downward through the thread excerpt and any minimal tags or metadata, ending near the lower right edge of the card before moving down to the next card.

layout.overall_description: The page reads like a minimalist editorial search experience. At the very top, a solid black navigation bar stretches full‑bleed edge to edge, with a modest fixed height that anchors the interface. Directly below, the black recedes into a wide, quiet off‑white band that fills roughly a quarter of the initial viewport height; its upper portion is mostly empty space, allowing the eyes to rest. Along the left side of this band, around the mid‑height, the page title (e.g., “Search Results”) and a single line summarizing the current query appear in a restrained, typographic style. Mirroring this, on the right side, a lone search icon and a minimal filter/sort trigger sit with ample padding, appearing almost to float in the open space. Below this header band, the layout narrows into a centered content column, with generous 15–20% gutters on both sides, creating a calm, focused reading channel. Inside this column, large, off‑white cards representing individual discussion threads are stacked vertically. Each card spans the full width of the central column, with 24–32px internal padding and 24–32px gaps between cards. The card header forms a subtle two‑column bar: on the left, a small circular avatar and a single line of metadata (author, date, and possibly forum section) flow horizontally; on the right, a discreet overflow icon floats at the same vertical level. Beneath this header, the thread title and a short excerpt occupy the full width of the card, with generous line spacing that encourages comfortable reading. As the user scrolls, the navigation bar remains visually dominant at the top, while the open header band scrolls away, leaving the user with a steady rhythm of centered cards and consistent white space, reinforcing the quiet, utilitarian forum aesthetic.

name: Topics & Tags
title: Topics & Tags
description: Browse, search, follow, and manage discussion topics/tags, with quick access to topics you already follow.

core_features: Tabbed view with an 'Explore' tab for searching all topics and a 'Following' tab for viewing topics the user already follows, controlled by the `tab` URL parameter.
Topic search form that calls `searchTopics(query, minFollowers)` and displays matching topics with their names, descriptions (when available), follower counts, and follow state.
Optional follower-count filter bound to the `minFollowers` URL parameter, including a quick option to focus on higher-interest topics (e.g., topics with at least 500 followers).
Inline follow/unfollow controls for each topic in both the Explore results and Following list, using `followTopic(tagId)` and `unfollowTopic(tagId)`.
A 'My Topics' / 'Following' list driven by `getFollowedTopics()` showing all topics where the user currently has `isFollowed = true`.
Navigation to a topic detail page (`topic.html?tagId=...`) when the user clicks a topic name from either the search results or the Following list.
Integration with the global header search: submitting the header search redirects to `search_results.html?query=...` while staying consistent with the rest of the site navigation.
URL-driven state so that `tab`, `query`, and `minFollowers` fully determine what is shown; changing filters or tabs updates the URL without requiring local storage.
user_workflows:
Explore topics by search:
1. User navigates to `topics.html` (optionally with `tab`, `query`, `minFollowers` in the URL).
2. If `tab` is not specified or set to `explore`, the Explore tab is active.
3. The search input is pre-filled from the `query` URL parameter when present.
4. The follower filter control is initialized from the `minFollowers` URL parameter when present.
5. On initial load (or when the user submits the search form), the page calls `searchTopics(query, minFollowers)`.
6. The resulting list of `Tag` records is rendered with topic name, optional description, follower count, and a follow/unfollow button.
7. The user can refine the search term or follower filter and re-submit; the URL is updated with new `query` and/or `minFollowers` and `searchTopics` is called again.

Focus on popular topics (e.g., ≥ 500 followers):
1. While in the Explore tab, the user activates a popularity filter control (e.g., 'Popular (500+ followers)').
2. The component sets `minFollowers` in the URL to a value such as 500 and triggers a new `searchTopics` call with the current `query` and the updated `minFollowers`.
3. The list refreshes to show only topics whose `followerCount` meets or exceeds the specified threshold.
4. The user can still adjust the search query; subsequent searches keep `minFollowers` until the user changes or clears it.

Follow a topic from search results:
1. In Explore, after `searchTopics` results appear, each topic row indicates whether it is already followed using `Tag.isFollowed`.
2. The user clicks the follow/unfollow control on a given topic.
3. If `Tag.isFollowed` is false, the component calls `followTopic(tagId)`; if true, it calls `unfollowTopic(tagId)`.
4. On success, the component updates the in-memory representation of that topic using the `tag` object returned by the interface so that `isFollowed` and `followerCount` remain in sync.
5. If the Following tab has been loaded previously in this session, its data is either refreshed by re-calling `getFollowedTopics()` or updated locally by adding/removing this topic there as well.

Review and manage followed topics:
1. The user switches to the Following tab by clicking the 'Following' / 'My Topics' tab control.
2. The URL is updated to `topics.html?tab=following` (preserving any other parameters that are relevant or clearing explore-only parameters as defined by the state logic).
3. On tab activation, the page calls `getFollowedTopics()`.
4. The returned list of `Tag` entities is rendered, showing the topics the user currently follows.
5. The user can unfollow directly from this list: clicking the unfollow control calls `unfollowTopic(tagId)`.
6. On success, the topic is removed from the Following list and, if visible in the Explore results, its follow state there is updated as well.

Open a topic detail page from the Topics & Tags page:
1. In either Explore or Following, the user clicks on a topic name.
2. The click triggers navigation to `topic.html?tagId=<Tag.id>` using the `id` of the selected `Tag`.
3. The topics page does not need to manage the topic detail state beyond passing the correct `tagId` parameter.

Use global header search from the Topics page:
1. While on `topics.html`, the user uses the global header search field.
2. On submit, the page navigates to `search_results.html?query=<searchTerm>`.
3. The Topics page does not intercept or alter topic-specific `tab`, `query`, or `minFollowers` parameters for this global search, as they are independent concerns.
interactions:
Tab selection:
- Interaction: Click on 'Explore' or 'Following' tab.
- Behavior: Updates the `tab` parameter in the URL and triggers the appropriate data fetch (`searchTopics` for Explore, `getFollowedTopics` for Following). Explore-only parameters like `query` and `minFollowers` can be preserved or cleared based on the defined state logic.

Search input and submission:
- Interaction: Type into the topic search field and submit the form (button click or Enter key).
- Behavior: Prevents default page reload, updates the `query` parameter (and optionally `minFollowers` if changed) in the URL, then calls `searchTopics(query, minFollowers)`; results are re-rendered when data is returned.

Follower-count filter adjustment:
- Interaction: Change value in a numeric follower filter control or toggle a 'Popular (500+)' option.
- Behavior: Updates `minFollowers` in the URL and triggers a new `searchTopics` call with the current `query` and new `minFollowers`.

Follow/unfollow topic:
- Interaction: Click on follow/unfollow button for a topic in either Explore or Following.
- Behavior: Depending on the current `Tag.isFollowed` state, calls `followTopic(tagId)` or `unfollowTopic(tagId)`. On success, merges the returned `tag` object into the existing data set (updating `isFollowed` and `followerCount`) and synchronizes lists across tabs.

Navigate to topic detail:
- Interaction: Click on the topic name in a list row.
- Behavior: Navigates the browser to `topic.html?tagId=<Tag.id>` using the topic's `id` field.

Global header search submission:
- Interaction: Enter text into the global header search and submit.
- Behavior: Navigates to `search_results.html?query=<searchTerm>`; does not call any topic-specific interface from this page.

Initial page load and direct URL entry:
- Interaction: User lands directly on a URL such as `topics.html?tab=following` or `topics.html?tab=explore&query=...&minFollowers=...`.
- Behavior: The page parses `tab`, `query`, and `minFollowers`, initializes components accordingly, and calls the relevant interface(s) (`searchTopics` or `getFollowedTopics`) without requiring further user input.

Browser navigation (back/forward):
- Interaction: User uses browser back/forward buttons after interacting with tabs, search, or filters.
- Behavior: The page listens for URL changes, re-reads `tab`, `query`, and `minFollowers`, and re-runs the appropriate interface calls so that the UI reflects the restored state.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is fully driven by the URL parameters `tab`, `query`, and `minFollowers`, without using localStorage. On initial load, the page reads these parameters and initializes the active tab and any search/filter controls. If `tab` is 'following', only the Following view is active and `getFollowedTopics()` is called; `query` and `minFollowers` are ignored for data fetching, though they may remain in the URL or be sanitized according to the implementation. If `tab` is 'explore' or absent, the Explore view is active. In this case, `query` and `minFollowers` are read (if present) and passed into `searchTopics(query, minFollowers)`. If they are absent, `searchTopics` is called with `query` and/or `minFollowers` set to undefined. When the user submits the search form, adjusts the follower-count filter, or toggles a popularity option, the page updates `query` and/or `minFollowers` in the URL using history APIs and re-executes `searchTopics` with the new values. When the user switches tabs, the page updates `tab` in the URL (and may clear explore-only parameters if a clean separation is desired) and calls either `searchTopics` or `getFollowedTopics` as appropriate. Clicking a topic name does not modify topics.html state; it navigates to `topic.html?tagId=<Tag.id>`. Submitting the global header search navigates to `search_results.html?query=<searchTerm>` and leaves the topics page. Browser back/forward actions trigger URL changes; the page responds by re-reading these parameters and dispatching any necessary interface calls to restore the view. All state transitions are derived from and reflected in the URL so that copying the URL reproduces the same tab, search term, and follower filter.

components.functionality: Controls whether the Explore or Following view is active, based on and updating the `tab` URL parameter. It does not fetch data itself but notifies child components when the active tab changes.

Provides an input for topic search terms and a follower-count filter control (including an option to focus on higher-follower topics). On submit or filter change, it updates the `query` and `minFollowers` URL parameters and triggers a `searchTopics` call when the Explore tab is active.

Displays the list of topics returned by `searchTopics(query, minFollowers)` when the Explore tab is active. Each row shows topic name, optional description, follower count, and current follow state, and provides navigation to the topic detail page and follow/unfollow actions.

When the Following tab is active, calls `getFollowedTopics()` and renders the user's followed topics. Supports unfollowing topics and navigation to topic detail pages, and synchronizes state with any Explore results already loaded.

Encapsulates the follow/unfollow logic for a single topic row. Given a `Tag` with `id`, `isFollowed`, and `followerCount`, it decides whether to call `followTopic(tagId)` or `unfollowTopic(tagId)` when clicked, and updates the in-memory Tag record with the returned `tag` object on success.

Shows contextual messages when there are no results in the Explore search or no followed topics in the Following tab. The messaging adjusts depending on whether the user has entered a search term or applied a follower-count filter.

Centralizes reading and writing of the `tab`, `query`, and `minFollowers` URL parameters. On load and on browser navigation events, it parses these parameters and notifies other components. When components request a state change (e.g., tab switch, new search, filter change), it updates the URL using history APIs and dispatches the updated state back to listening components.

Coordinates calls to `searchTopics`, `getFollowedTopics`, `followTopic`, and `unfollowTopic` based on events from UI components and current URL state. Maintains an in-memory cache of Explore search results and followed topics to keep both views synchronized without extra interface calls when possible.
Global header search component accessible on the Topics & Tags page. On submit, it navigates to `search_results.html?query=<searchTerm>` and does not interact with topic-specific interfaces.

layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a linear, top‑to‑bottom flow with a full‑width, black global navigation bar at the very top, and all forum-specific content centered in a single main column below. Directly beneath the nav is a tall off‑white hero band that introduces the Topics & Tags area: in this band, the topics-tab-switcher is centered horizontally and kept visually light, while the header-search-form appears as a right‑aligned, minimal search affordance. Below this breathing space, a single prominent card sits in the middle of the page and acts as the main interaction surface. The topic-follow-toggle lives at the right edge of each topic row inside those lists. When there are no topics to show, the topics-empty-state replaces the list content within the same card footprint. Only the essential interactive elements are exposed per view: a couple of tabs, one or two search controls (one in the hero, one inside the card), and a vertical list of topics accompanied by subtle follow toggles. Typography is given room to breathe, with plenty of vertical spacing (16–24px) between text blocks and list rows. Only a single dominant card is visible within the initial viewport, and even inside the card, list rows are not tightly packed; they use generous line height and row spacing. There are no visual dividers beyond simple rules or subtle separators, keeping the interface visually quiet.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye is first drawn to the high‑contrast black nav bar at the top, moving left‑to‑right across global navigation. Dropping into the off‑white hero band, attention is gently pulled toward the center where the topics-tab-switcher lives, then laterally to the right where the header-search-form icon provides a secondary focal point. From there, the eye naturally moves downward into the large, centered card in the main content column. Inside this card, the eye starts at the top with the topic-search-form (the most purposeful control), then flows down into the topic list. Within each topic row, the visual flow is left‑to‑right, from topic name and meta text toward the topic-follow-toggle at the far right, which acts like the row’s action endpoint. If no topics are present, the visual flow lands on the centered topics-empty-state message and then returns upward to the search field or the tabs for corrective action.

layout.overall_description: The Topics & Tags page is composed as a quiet, editorial-style canvas. A solid black navigation bar runs full‑bleed across the top, anchoring the interface. Beneath it, an expansive off‑white band stretches down the page, mostly empty, with a small, centered tab switcher for browsing vs. followed topics and a single, understated search icon floated to the right edge of the main content column. This upper region feels light and spacious, gently introducing the section rather than crowding it with controls. Below this band, after a generous vertical gap, a single, substantial card floats in the center of the viewport, framed by wide left and right gutters. Inside the card, a full‑width, minimalist search input for topics sits at the top, followed by a calm, vertically stacked list of topics or followed tags, each row lightly separated and aligned in two columns: descriptive text on the left, a small follow/unfollow control on the right. When there are no items, the interior of the card transforms into a centered empty state message, still framed by the same generous padding. The entire page reads as a clean, monochrome reading surface, with clear hierarchy from nav to hero band to central card and a strong emphasis on content clarity and breathing room.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The topics-tab-switcher is positioned within the off‑white hero band directly below the black navigation bar. It sits in the horizontal center of the main content column (which itself is centered on the page and approximately 60–70% of the total page width). Vertically, the switcher is placed roughly in the middle of the hero band, leaving a generous 32–48px of empty space above and below. The switcher presents its tabs (e.g., 'Browse All', 'Following') as a compact, pill-style or minimalist text row, with the entire control occupying no more than 30–40% of the main content column’s width. Tabs are arranged left‑to‑right but centered as a group, with 16–24px spacing between them, and no surrounding box that would visually compete with the central card below. The area to the left and right of the switcher remains mostly blank, reinforcing the minimalist feel and letting the tabs float in the middle of the off‑white band.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, positioned as part of the normal document flow within the hero band; it does not scroll independently or stick.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The topic-search-form lives inside the primary central card in the main content column. This card appears below the hero band and is centered horizontally, spanning about 60–70% of the page width with equal 15–20% gutters on both sides. The search form is anchored at the very top of the card’s content area, with 24–32px padding between it and the card’s top edge. It presents as a single, understated input field spanning roughly 100% of the card’s inner width minus side padding, resulting in a visual width of about 80–90% of the total page width. The input is aligned to the left within the card, using a simple, hairline border or underlined style consistent with the minimal aesthetic. A small amount of vertical spacing (16–24px) separates this search form from the list content below, forming a clear but gentle break. There are no extra labels or buttons cluttering the area; the form is concise and visually integrated into the card’s top section, serving as the obvious entry point for filtering topics once the user’s gaze has moved into the card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the card; it scrolls naturally with the page and remains at the top of the card’s content stack.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The topic-search-results-list occupies the main body area of the central card when the 'Browse All' or equivalent tab is active. It sits directly beneath the topic-search-form with a vertical gap of about 16–24px, maintaining a comfortable separation. The list stretches the full inner width of the card, respecting the card’s left and right padding (24–32px on each side). Each topic appears as a horizontally oriented row with two clear zones: on the left, the topic name in a medium-weight type with optional meta info (e.g., number of threads) stacked below in a lighter tone; on the right, the topic-follow-toggle is aligned flush with the card’s right content edge. Rows are separated vertically by 16–24px of whitespace, optionally with a very subtle horizontal rule or a light divider, keeping the overall look airy. The list’s height is determined by content but is intended to show only a modest number of rows (e.g., 4–6) in the initial viewport to preserve the low-density feel; additional rows extend downward with the main page scroll rather than introducing an inner scroll. When this list is active, it fills most of the vertical space inside the card, leaving 24–32px padding before the card’s bottom edge.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the card; it grows vertically as items are added and participates in normal page scrolling.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The followed-topics-list shares the same spatial footprint as the topic-search-results-list, occupying the body of the central card beneath the topic-search-form when the 'Following' tab is active. It aligns with the card’s inner left and right padding and begins 16–24px below the search form. Topic rows are styled and arranged identically to the main results list: left-aligned topic text and meta information, right-aligned topic-follow-toggle, and 16–24px vertical spacing between rows. Because followed topics are typically fewer, this list will often be shorter than the full search results, leaving additional empty space toward the bottom of the card, which reinforces the calm, spacious aesthetic. When the followed-topics-list is active, the topic-search-results-list is entirely hidden, ensuring only one primary list is visible at a time and maintaining the single-card focus within the viewport.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the card, mutually exclusive with topic-search-results-list; it is swapped in and out based on tab state without changing the card’s position.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The topic-follow-toggle appears inside each row of both the topic-search-results-list and the followed-topics-list. For every topic row, the toggle is anchored to the far right edge of the card’s content area, aligned vertically with the topic name on the left. It is a small, minimalist button or icon—such as an outlined pill labeled 'Follow' / 'Following' or a simple icon with text—that occupies roughly 10–15% of the card’s inner width, ensuring it remains visually contained while still being an obvious action endpoint. The control has an invisible hit area of about 24–32px in both width and height to satisfy touch target requirements, even if the visible element appears smaller. A narrow horizontal gap (8–16px) separates the text block on the left from this toggle, preserving the sense of a two‑column layout within each row. The toggle is visually light, using monochrome outlines or subtle fills to avoid overpowering the topic text while still reading as the interactive element that completes the row’s visual flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; it is positioned within each list row and moves with that row as the page scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The topics-empty-state occupies the interior of the central card whenever there are no topics to display—either no search results or no followed topics. In these cases, the empty state replaces the relevant list component within the same card boundaries. It is centered both horizontally and vertically within the card’s content area, respecting the existing 24–32px padding on all sides. The empty state content consists of a short, muted headline and one or two lines of explanatory text, stacked vertically with 8–16px spacing, all center-aligned. Optionally, a very subtle icon or glyph can appear above the text, but it should remain small and monochrome to avoid breaking the minimalist style. There is generous whitespace around this block, so the card appears mostly empty, reinforcing a calm, non-alarming feedback. The topic-search-form above remains visible, with 32–48px of space separating it from the top of the empty state, inviting the user to adjust their query or switch tabs.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the card, mutually exclusive with the list components; it occupies the list region without altering the card’s overall placement.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The url-state-manager is non-visual and does not render any UI elements on the page. Conceptually, it ‘sits’ behind the main content column, coordinating the state of the topics-tab-switcher, topic-search-form, and which list (topic-search-results-list or followed-topics-list) is visible by encoding these states into the URL. It has no direct spatial footprint, margins, or alignment; its presence is only reflected in how on-screen components respond when the URL changes or when a user interacts with tabs and search.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, purely behavioral; it does not participate in layout and has no position in the rendered UI.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The topics-data-controller is also non-visual, functioning as the behind-the-scenes orchestrator for retrieving and updating topic data displayed in the lists. It is logically associated with the central card region, feeding data into the topic-search-results-list, followed-topics-list, topic-follow-toggle states, and topics-empty-state. It does not occupy space on the screen, create any visible container, or affect the grid; instead, it ensures that when users interact with visible components, the content within the main card updates accordingly.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; it has no physical layout behavior and exists only as a data and state layer.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header-search-form is rendered as the right‑aligned search affordance within the off‑white hero band immediately below the black navigation bar. It aligns horizontally with the right edge of the main content column (the same column that contains the central card), not with the full browser edge, maintaining the centered grid. Vertically, it is aligned on the same horizontal line as, or slightly above, the topics-tab-switcher, creating a gentle diagonal tension between center and right. Visually, it may appear as a simple magnifying glass icon or a very compact input that only expands on interaction, occupying roughly 24–32px in visible width but with a larger invisible hit area (24–32px square) to ensure usability. There is ample whitespace to its left—at least 32–48px between it and the tab switcher—and above and below (24–32px to the band’s edges), so the control reads as a quiet, secondary search entry point rather than a dominant feature. It serves to anchor the user’s eye to the right side of the hero area before attention moves down to the main card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero band; it scrolls away with the rest of the hero content as the user scrolls down the page.

name: Topic Detail
title: Topic Detail
description: Displays a single topic with its details and associated discussions, and lets the user follow/unfollow the topic, change discussion sorting, and navigate to discussions or back to their topics.

core_features: Load and display topic details (name, description, follower count, follow state) and discussions for the topic using getTopicDetail(tagId, sortBy).
Allow the user to follow or unfollow the topic from the page using followTopic(tagId) and unfollowTopic(tagId), and update the displayed follower count and follow state.

List discussions associated with the topic, showing key metadata (category name, original author, and selected Discussion fields), and navigate to a discussion thread when a discussion title is clicked.
Provide sorting controls that update the sortBy URL parameter and reload the discussions list via getTopicDetail with the new sort order.
Provide breadcrumb navigation back to the topics list (topics.html) focused on the user's followed topics via tab=following.
Provide a search input in the page header that, on submit, navigates to search_results.html with a query URL parameter.
user_workflows:
View a topic and its discussions:
1. User lands on topic.html with a tagId URL parameter (and optionally sortBy).
2. The page reads tagId and sortBy from the URL.
3. The page calls getTopicDetail with tagId and sortBy.
4. The response Tag data populates the topic header (name, description, follower count, follow state).
5. The response discussions array populates the discussion list.
6. User browses the list and may click a discussion title to open discussion.html with the selected discussionId.

Follow a topic:
1. User is on topic.html with a valid tagId and sees the topic header.
2. User clicks the follow control when the topic is currently not followed.
3. The page calls followTopic(tagId).
4. On success, the returned Tag object updates the topic header (isFollowed and followerCount), and the follow control reflects the followed state.
5. If the user later returns to topics.html, this topic can appear in their followed topics view (handled on topics.html, not on this page).

Unfollow a topic:
1. User is on topic.html with a valid tagId and the topic is currently followed.
2. User clicks the unfollow control.
3. The page calls unfollowTopic(tagId).
4. On success, the returned Tag object updates the topic header (isFollowed and followerCount), and the follow control reflects the unfollowed state.

Change discussion sort order within a topic:
1. User is viewing a topic with discussions listed.
2. User interacts with sort controls (for example, selecting a different sort key).
3. The component updates the sortBy value in the URL (rebuilding the topic.html URL with the same tagId and new sortBy).
4. The page reloads or re-initializes, reads tagId and sortBy from the URL, and calls getTopicDetail again.
5. The new discussions ordering from getTopicDetail is rendered in the list.

Navigate back to followed topics:
1. User is on topic.html.
2. User clicks the breadcrumb or dedicated navigation control for returning to their topics.
3. The page navigates to topics.html with tab=following in the URL parameters.
4. topics.html handles displaying the followed topics view.

Search from the topic page:
1. User is on topic.html and sees a search input in the header.
2. User enters a search term and submits the form.
3. The page constructs a navigation to search_results.html with query set to the submitted term.
4. The search results page handles displaying results; topic.html does not call any search interface itself.
interactions:
Page initialization:
- Trigger: Page load or URL change to topic.html.
- Behavior: Read tagId and sortBy from the URL. If tagId is present, call getTopicDetail(tagId, sortBy). Show loading state until the call resolves. On success, bind Tag and discussions data to relevant components. On failure, display an error state with a retry option that re-invokes getTopicDetail with the same parameters.

Follow/unfollow topic toggle:
- Trigger: User clicks the follow/unfollow control in the topic header.
- Behavior when not followed: Call followTopic(tagId). While pending, disable the control or show a loading state. On success, update the Tag data (followerCount, isFollowed) in page state using the returned Tag and re-render dependent components. On error, revert any optimistic UI changes and show an error indicator.
- Behavior when followed: Call unfollowTopic(tagId) with similar loading, success, and error handling.

Discussion selection:
- Trigger: User clicks on a discussion title (or primary interactive area) in the discussion list.
- Behavior: Read the associated Discussion.id from that list item and navigate to discussion.html with discussionId set to that id in the URL parameters.

Sort control interaction:
- Trigger: User selects a new sort option from the sort controls (e.g., a dropdown or tab control).
- Behavior: The component constructs a new URL for topic.html that keeps the current tagId and sets sortBy to the selected sort key. It then navigates to that URL. The page initialization logic re-runs and calls getTopicDetail(tagId, sortBy) with the updated sortBy value.

Breadcrumb / back navigation:
- Trigger: User clicks the breadcrumb or back navigation control.
- Behavior: Navigate to topics.html with tab set to following in the URL parameters.

Header search submission:
- Trigger: User submits the search form in the header while on topic.html.
- Behavior: Prevent default form submission behavior, read the search term from the input, and navigate to search_results.html with query set to the search term in the URL parameters.

Retry loading topic details:
- Trigger: User clicks a retry action in an error state if getTopicDetail fails.
- Behavior: Re-invoke getTopicDetail(tagId, sortBy) using the same URL parameters and re-bind data on success.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page derives its primary state from URL parameters and interface responses. On initialization, the page reads tagId and sortBy from the topic.html URL. tagId is required to determine which Tag to load; if tagId is missing or invalid, the page displays an error state and does not call followTopic or unfollowTopic. sortBy is optional; if present, it is passed directly to getTopicDetail along with tagId. If sortBy is absent, the page either omits it or passes a null/undefined equivalent, and the backend determines the default sort order. After calling getTopicDetail(tagId, sortBy), the page stores the returned Tag and discussions in in-memory state for the current render only (no persistence beyond the current view). The isFollowed and followerCount fields of the Tag in state are updated exclusively based on results from followTopic(tagId) and unfollowTopic(tagId), which are called in response to user interactions. When the sort controls update sortBy, they do so by replacing the current topic.html URL with the same tagId and new sortBy, ensuring the URL is the single source of truth for the current sorting. Navigations to discussion.html, topics.html, and search_results.html always occur by constructing URLs with the appropriate parameters (discussionId, tab, query) so that each target page can derive its own state from URL parameters without relying on localStorage or shared client-side state.

components.functionality: Initializes the Topic Detail page by reading URL parameters, calling getTopicDetail with tagId and sortBy, managing loading and error states, and distributing the Tag and discussions data to child components.
Provides navigation back to the topics list view focused on followed topics and optionally displays a simple breadcrumb path including the current topic name for context.

Displays topic-level information (tag name, description, follower count, follow state) and exposes the follow/unfollow control using Tag data. It reacts to changes in Tag.isFollowed and Tag.followerCount from followTopic/unfollowTopic responses.
