Encapsulates follow/unfollow operations for the current topic. When activated, it decides whether to call followTopic or unfollowTopic based on Tag.isFollowed and updates the in-memory Tag state using the response.

Allows the user to select how discussions under the topic are ordered (for example, by latest or top). Updates the sortBy URL parameter and triggers reloading of topic data through page re-initialization.

Renders the list of discussions returned by getTopicDetail for the current topic. It iterates over each item containing a Discussion object, categoryName, and originalAuthorDisplayName, and delegates rendering of each item to a list item component.

Represents a single discussion in the topic's discussion list, showing the discussion title and selected metadata such as categoryName, originalAuthorDisplayName, and key Discussion fields (e.g., replyCount, viewCount, score, timestamps). Clicking the title navigates to discussion.html for that discussion.

Provides a search input in the page header. On submit, it reads the search term and navigates to search_results.html with query set to the submitted term. It does not call any search interface directly.
Shows a loading indicator while getTopicDetail is in progress and hides the main content until the data is ready.
Displays an error message and retry control if getTopicDetail fails or if tagId is missing or invalid. The retry control re-invokes getTopicDetail using the current URL parameters.

Displays an informative empty state when getTopicDetail returns a valid Tag but no associated discussions. It may suggest that the user follow the topic or check back later, without assuming any static content.

layout.grid_system: Implicit centered column layout likely based on a 12‑column grid with a wide central content span (approx. 8–10 columns) and large left/right gutters.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a strictly vertical, linear flow. At the very top sits a full-bleed, fixed black navigation bar. Directly beneath it, a tall off-white header band spans the width of the viewport but visually centers content in a single, wide central column. Within this band, the breadcrumb navigation appears at the top-left of the central column, the topic header (title + meta) sits just below it, and the follow toggle is aligned toward the upper-right of this same central column. A minimalist, icon-first search form is anchored at the far right of the header band, aligned horizontally with the topic header/follow controls. Below this header band, the topic sort controls appear at the top-right of the central column, followed by the topic discussion list: a vertical stack of full-width discussion cards (list items) centered in the content column. Loading, error, and empty states occupy the same central column region where the discussion list would normally appear, replacing the list when relevant.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a strictly vertical, linear flow. At the very top sits a full-bleed, fixed black navigation bar. Directly beneath it, a tall off-white header band spans the width of the viewport but visually centers content in a single, wide central column. Within this band, the breadcrumb navigation appears at the top-left of the central column, the topic header (title + meta) sits just below it, and the follow toggle is aligned toward the upper-right of this same central column. A minimalist, icon-first search form is anchored at the far right of the header band, aligned horizontally with the topic header/follow controls. Below this header band, the topic sort controls appear at the top-right of the central column, followed by the topic discussion list: a vertical stack of full-width discussion cards (list items) centered in the content column. Loading, error, and empty states occupy the same central column region where the discussion list would normally appear, replacing the list when relevant.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The black navigation bar occupies roughly the top 8–10% of the viewport height and spans the full width. The off-white header band directly below it consumes approximately 20–30% of the viewport height, with generous vertical breathing room above and below the topic header and search form. Left and right gutters take up about 15–20% of the page width each, leaving a central content column at about 60–70% width where all forum content (breadcrumb, topic header, controls, list items, and states) is aligned. Within that column, individual discussion cards (list items) have substantial internal padding (around 24–32px on all sides) and are separated by vertical gaps of roughly 24–32px. The discussion list itself occupies the central 50–60% of the viewport height on first load, with only one or two cards visible above the fold, reinforcing the low-density, reading-focused feel.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is intentionally low. Large vertical gaps are maintained between the navigation bar, the header band content, the sort controls, and each discussion card. Text elements are never crowded: the topic title has generous space above and below, meta information sits with comfortable 8–16px internal spacing, and list items use ample padding and relaxed line spacing. Interface controls are sparse and visually understated: the follow toggle and sort controls are simple text or minimal-button treatments, and the search form presents primarily as a single icon with a generous invisible hit area. At any given scroll position, only a few interactive elements are present, keeping focus on reading discussions rather than scanning controls.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye starts at the stark, high-contrast black navigation bar at the top, reading left to right. Dropping into the off-white header band, the user’s attention is guided to the left by the breadcrumb and large topic title, which anchors understanding of context. From there, attention is gently pulled to the right by the follow toggle and the right-aligned search icon, creating a lateral balance. As the user scrolls slightly, the central column of discussion cards becomes the dominant focal area; each card’s header row directs the eye left to right across the avatar and author/meta line, with a subtle overflow icon at the top-right. The eye then moves downward into the card body text. If loading, error, or empty states are present, they appear centered within the same main column, becoming the primary focus while still respecting the existing vertical rhythm and whitespace.

layout.overall_description: The Topic Detail page reads as a calm, editorial-style reading surface. A full-width black navigation bar pins to the top of the viewport, acting as a strong visual anchor and frame. Beneath it, a tall, quiet off-white band stretches across the viewport but visually centers all content in a wide central column, surrounded by generous empty gutters on both sides. At the top-left of this column, a small, understated breadcrumb indicates where the user is in the forum hierarchy, followed by a large, left-aligned topic title and its meta details. To the upper-right of the same column, a minimalist follow/unfollow control and a right-aligned search icon subtly balance the composition, providing clear but unobtrusive controls. Below this header region, the off-white background continues, and the eye is drawn down to a series of generous, card-like discussion items centered in the same column. Each card feels like an individual article: a header row with avatar and author on the left, a vertical ellipsis on the right, and body text flowing full-width beneath. Large vertical gaps between cards and sections maintain a sense of air and focus. When loading, empty, or error states appear, they occupy the same central content area, visually aligned with the discussion cards, ensuring consistency and preserving the quiet, reading-first aesthetic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This controller component does not render any visible UI on the page. Conceptually, it occupies the full viewport as an invisible layer responsible for deciding which visual components (loading state, error state, empty state, topic header, and discussion list) are shown. Spatially, it maps directly to the entire main content area below the black navigation bar, orchestrating whether the user sees the header band plus list of discussion cards, or a single centered state message.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; behaves as a background controller that conditions the presence or absence of other visual components in the main content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumb navigation sits inside the off-white header band, aligned to the top-left edge of the central content column. It is positioned directly below the black navigation bar with a generous vertical margin of about 32–48px, ensuring a clear separation between global navigation and in-page context. The breadcrumb spans only as much width as its text requires, typically no more than 30–40% of the central column, and remains left-aligned. It sits above the topic header, with approximately 16–24px of vertical space between the bottom of the breadcrumb and the top of the topic title, reinforcing a hierarchical relationship without crowding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls normally with the page; it is anchored within the header band and moves out of view as the user scrolls down into the discussion list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The topic header occupies the upper central area of the off-white header band, directly beneath the breadcrumb, within the same central content column. It is left-aligned and spans roughly 70–80% of the column width for the title text, leaving comfortable breathing room to the right for controls. The topic title appears as the dominant text block, with generous space above (16–24px from the breadcrumb) and below (16–24px before any meta or controls). Meta information (e.g., topic category, creation date, number of followers) appears immediately below the title, aligned on the same left edge and separated by about 8–16px of vertical spacing. The entire topic header block takes up roughly the upper half of the header band’s vertical height, leaving additional empty space beneath it before the sort controls and discussion list begin. This layout emphasizes the topic as the conceptual anchor of the page while still feeling light and uncluttered.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the header band; scrolls away with content as the user moves down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The follow/unfollow toggle is positioned within the same off-white header band, aligned to the right edge of the central content column, horizontally aligned with either the baseline of the topic title or slightly below it. Visually, it appears as a small, minimalist control (e.g., a text button or a subtle outlined button) that occupies a compact rectangle of around 96–128px in width but benefits from a generous invisible hit area of about 32px in height. There is a clear horizontal gap between the right end of the topic title text and the toggle, ensuring that the title never visually collides with this control. Vertically, the toggle shares the same region as the topic header, so that when users scan from the topic title on the left to the right side of the column, they naturally encounter the follow toggle. About 16–24px of space is maintained above and below the toggle within the header band to preserve the overall airy character.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls with the header; remains horizontally fixed to the right edge of the central content column and moves out of view as the user scrolls past the header band.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search form is represented primarily by a single search icon (e.g., magnifying glass) positioned at the far right of the off-white header band, aligned within the central content column’s right boundary. It sits in the upper-right quadrant of the header band, typically slightly above or horizontally aligned with the follow toggle, reinforcing its role as a global-in-page control. Visually, the icon itself is small and unobtrusive, but it is surrounded by an invisible touch target area approximately 24–32px square to maintain usability without adding visual clutter. There is at least 16px of space between the search icon’s hit area and the right edge of the viewport, and a similar gap between the icon and the follow toggle to avoid crowding. This right-aligned search anchor subtly pulls the eye laterally after the user reads the topic title on the left, echoing the mockup’s lateral visual flow from content to control.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls normally within the header band; remains pinned to the right edge of the central column and exits the viewport as the user scrolls down.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort controls are placed at the top of the main discussion area, immediately below the off-white header band but still within the same central content column. They are right-aligned, residing on a single horizontal line close to the column’s right edge, mirroring the position of the search icon above. On the left side of this same row, the space is mostly empty or may include a subtle label such as “Discussions,” but the emphasis remains on the control on the right. There is a vertical gap of about 32–48px between the bottom of the topic header band content and the sort controls, creating a clear transition from topic context to discussion list interactions. Below the sort controls, another 16–24px of space is maintained before the first discussion card begins. The sort UI itself is compact—a text label with a caret, segmented control, or low-profile dropdown—occupying only a small portion of width while preserving a generously padded clickable area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls with the discussion list; as the user moves down the page, the sort controls scroll out of view along with the top of the list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The discussion list occupies the main content region below the header band and sort controls, centered within the 60–70% width central column. It is a vertical stack of discussion cards (list items), each spanning the full width of the central column with equal left and right padding relative to the viewport gutters. There is a consistent vertical spacing of roughly 24–32px between the bottom of one card and the top of the next, ensuring each discussion feels like a distinct, readable unit. On initial load, the first one or two cards are visible within the central 50–60% of the viewport height, with ample off-white space above and below them, maintaining the low-density, reading-focused look. The list itself stretches downward as needed with scrolling, but never fills the entire width of the viewport, preserving wide, empty gutters to the left and right that echo the calm, editorial layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls vertically as a standard content list within the central column; does not have sticky or floating behavior, respecting the straightforward top-to-bottom reading flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each discussion list item is rendered as a flat, full-width card centered within the main content column. The card has generous internal padding on all sides—approximately 24–32px—resulting in a substantial whitespace border between the card’s content and its edges. At the top of the card, a header row spans the width: on the left, a small circular avatar is placed at the far-left edge of the card’s content area, followed by an 8–12px gap, then the author’s name and key meta information arranged in one or two short lines. On the right of the same row, aligned vertically with the avatar, a minimalist vertical ellipsis (overflow menu) icon sits flush with the card’s right content edge, again with an invisible hit area of about 24–32px. This header row takes up roughly the top 20–25% of the card’s height. Below the header row, separated by 16–24px of vertical spacing, the discussion text body extends full-width inside the card’s padding, with calm line spacing and no dense blocks of UI. Optional footer details (e.g., reply count, last activity) can appear below the body, separated by another 16px and aligned to the same left baseline, but they remain visually understated. The cards themselves are flat—no heavy borders, shadows, or gradients—relying on padding, spacing, and alignment for structure.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a standard block element in the vertical list, maintaining fixed internal spacing and alignment regardless of viewport height; rearranges only responsively by adjusting text wrapping within the central column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading state occupies the same central content column area as the discussion list, directly below the off-white header band and any sort controls (or in place of them if they depend on loaded data). The rest of the page chrome—the black navigation bar and header band—remains unchanged, maintaining context. Within the central column, a single column of minimalist skeleton elements or a centered, unobtrusive loading indicator is used. For example, one or two rectangular skeleton cards may be shown, each matching the size and padding of discussion list items, spaced 24–32px apart vertically, reinforcing the expected layout while data loads. The loading indicator and skeletons are vertically centered in the remaining viewport height as much as possible without overlapping the header band, leaving ample off-white space around them to keep the interface from feeling busy during loading.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily replaces the discussion list within the main content column; scroll behavior is minimal as content typically fits within one viewport height during loading.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error state also appears in the main central content column, aligned with where the discussion list would normally begin. It is positioned below the header band with a generous top margin of about 48–64px to separate it clearly from the topic header. The error message is centered horizontally within the column and may be slightly above the vertical midline of the visible viewport, ensuring it is immediately visible without scrolling. The text is succinct, with a calm tone, and is surrounded by ample whitespace: at least 24–32px of padding around the message block and 24–32px of space between any retry button and the message text. No heavy color fills or large icons disrupt the monochrome aesthetic; if an icon is present, it is simple and small, placed above the text with about 16px spacing. The overall effect is a quiet, non-alarming error presentation that still clearly communicates the issue.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Replaces the discussion list region; scrolls as a single block if content exceeds the viewport, but typically remains fully visible without scroll due to its minimal content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty state occupies the same space as the discussion list within the central content column when no discussions are available for the topic. It appears below the header band and sort controls, with a generous vertical margin of about 48px from the controls, reinforcing the conceptual transition from ‘controls’ to ‘content.’ The empty state content is centered horizontally in the column and presented as a small stack: an optional subtle icon or placeholder mark at the top, the main empty-state message just below it, and an optional call-to-action (such as “Start a discussion”) below that. Each element is separated by 16–24px of vertical spacing. The entire block takes up only a portion of the vertical space (roughly the central 30–40% of the viewport height), with large amounts of off-white space above and below to maintain the airy, minimal aesthetic. The visual styling remains monochrome and understated so that the page still feels calm even in the absence of content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Replaces the discussion list area when there are no items to show; scrolls as part of the main content column but typically fits entirely within a single viewport height.

name: Bookmarks
title: Bookmarks
description: Page where the user can view, filter, and manage their bookmarked discussion threads, and navigate into full discussions.

core_features: Fetch all bookmarked discussions for the current user using getBookmarkedDiscussions when the page loads or reloads.
Display bookmarked discussions in a list, showing discussion title, category name, tag names, reply count, view count, score, and last activity information from the Discussion model.
Allow users to open a bookmarked discussion by clicking its title, navigating to discussion.html with the appropriate discussionId URL parameter.

Provide controls on each bookmarked discussion entry to remove it from the current bookmarks view (local state update), with the assumption that bookmark persistence is handled elsewhere in the system.
Offer client-side sorting of bookmarked discussions (e.g., by last activity, creation date, or title) based on Discussion fields, controlled via URL parameters.
Offer client-side text filtering of bookmarked discussions (search within bookmarks) based on title, categoryName, and tagNames, controlled via URL parameters.
Provide pagination of bookmarked discussions on the client side, driven by URL parameters for page and page size.
Show an appropriate empty-state message when there are no bookmarked discussions after applying filters.
Allow users to run a global search from the header search bar, navigating to search_results.html with the query term in the URL.
user_workflows:
Viewing bookmarked discussions:
1. User opens bookmarks.html (via header navigation or direct URL).
2. On initial load, the page reads URL parameters for sort, order, q (search text), page, and perPage.
3. The page calls getBookmarkedDiscussions with no parameters.
4. The returned list is stored in page state.
5. The list is sorted, filtered, and paginated on the client based on the URL parameters.
6. The processed list is rendered in the bookmarks list component.

Opening a bookmarked discussion:
1. User scans the bookmarks list.
2. User clicks on a discussion title.
3. The page navigates to discussion.html with the query parameter discussionId set to the clicked Discussion.id.
4. The discussion.html page loads that discussion based on the discussionId parameter.

Filtering within bookmarks:
1. User types a search term into the bookmarks filter input in the toolbar.
2. User submits the filter (e.g., presses Enter or clicks a filter button).
3. The page updates the URL parameter q to the entered search term and resets page to 1.
4. Without additional API calls, the existing in-memory bookmarked discussions list is filtered on the client side based on q matching title, categoryName, or tagNames.
5. The filtered and paginated list is re-rendered.

Sorting bookmarked discussions:
1. User opens the sort selector in the bookmarks toolbar.
2. User chooses a sort field (for example, last activity or creation date) and sort direction.
3. The page updates the sort and order URL parameters accordingly and resets page to 1.
4. Using the existing in-memory list from getBookmarkedDiscussions, the page re-sorts the discussions on the client side.
5. The sorted and paginated list is re-rendered.

Paginating bookmarked discussions:
1. User scrolls to the bottom of the current bookmarks list.
2. User clicks a pagination control (e.g., next page or a specific page number).
3. The page updates the page parameter in the URL (and optionally perPage if changed).
4. Without calling getBookmarkedDiscussions again, the page recalculates which slice of the in-memory list to show.
5. The new page of bookmarks is rendered.

Removing a discussion from the bookmarks view:
1. User identifies a discussion they no longer want to keep in their bookmarks view.
2. User clicks the remove/unbookmark control in that discussion’s actions.
3. The page removes that discussion from the in-memory list of bookmarks on the client side.
4. Optionally, the page appends or updates a removedId URL parameter to reflect that this discussion has been removed from the current view (for bookmarking-state consistency messaging).
5. The bookmarks list is re-rendered without the removed discussion. Any server-side persistence of bookmark removal is assumed to be handled outside of this page’s defined interfaces.

Running a global search from the bookmarks page:
1. User types a search term into the header search bar.
2. User submits the search form.
3. The page navigates to search_results.html with query set to the search term in the URL.
4. The bookmarks page does not handle search results; the new page is responsible for loading and displaying them.
interactions: On page load: Trigger a call to getBookmarkedDiscussions, then apply client-side sorting, filtering, and pagination based on URL parameters before initial render.
Click on discussion title: Navigate to discussion.html?discussionId=<Discussion.id>.
Click on remove/unbookmark control: Remove the corresponding discussion object from the page’s in-memory bookmarks list and optionally update a removedId URL parameter, then re-render the list.
Change sort selector: Update URL parameters sort and order, reset page to 1, reapply client-side sorting and pagination, and re-render.
Change bookmarks filter text and submit: Update URL parameter q, reset page to 1, apply client-side filtering and pagination, and re-render.
Click pagination controls: Update URL parameter page (and, if applicable, perPage), compute the appropriate slice of the in-memory list, and re-render.
Header search submission: On submit, navigate to search_results.html?query=<searchTerm> without affecting bookmarks state.
page_functionality.state_logic: The bookmarks page uses URL query parameters as the primary external representation of UI state. Supported parameters include: sort (string: the field used for client-side sorting, such as 'lastReplyAt', 'createdAt', or 'title', mapping to Discussion fields), order (string: 'asc' or 'desc', controlling sort direction), q (string: a text query applied client-side against discussion.title, categoryName, and tagNames), page (integer: the current page number for client-side pagination), perPage (integer: the number of items displayed per page), and removedId (string, optional: identifier of a discussion removed from the current bookmarks view, used only for client-side feedback or highlighting). On initial load, the page reads these parameters, calls getBookmarkedDiscussions, stores the returned array in memory, and then applies filtering (q), sorting (sort and order), and pagination (page and perPage) before rendering. Any user interaction that changes sorting, filtering, pagination, or removal of bookmarks updates the URL parameters via history.pushState or history.replaceState, then recalculates the derived list from the in-memory data without additional API calls. Navigation to discussion.html or search_results.html passes data strictly through URL parameters (discussionId and query respectively). No state is stored in localStorage or other persistent client storage; all shareable and restorable state is encoded in the URL.

components.functionality: On page initialization, call getBookmarkedDiscussions to retrieve the full list of the current user’s bookmarked discussions. Store the returned array in an internal state structure that other components (toolbar, list, pagination) consume. After fetching, trigger a derivation step that applies client-side filtering, sorting, and pagination based on current URL parameters.

Expose controls for filtering and sorting the bookmarks list. This includes a text input for q (search within bookmarks) and selectors for sort field and sort order. Reads initial values from URL parameters and, on user changes, updates those URL parameters and notifies the data-fetcher component to recompute the derived list from the in-memory data.

Display pagination elements for the bookmarks list (such as previous, next, and direct page selection). Reads the total number of bookmarked discussions from the in-memory dataset and current page/perPage from URL parameters. On user interaction, updates the page (and optionally perPage) URL parameters and triggers re-derivation of the visible slice of bookmarks without additional interface calls.

Render the client-side processed list of bookmarked discussions for the current page, after applying filters and sorting. For each discussion entry, show title, categoryName, tagNames, and key Discussion properties such as replyCount, viewCount, score, createdAt, and lastReplyAt. When the processed list is empty, display an empty-state message indicating that there are no bookmarked discussions matching the current filters.

Represent a single bookmarked discussion row within the bookmarks-list. Binds to one element from the getBookmarkedDiscussions result, including discussion (Discussion object), categoryName, and tagNames. Provides a clickable title to open the discussion and a remove/unbookmark control. Clicking the title navigates to discussion.html?discussionId=<Discussion.id>. Clicking the remove control informs the bookmarks-data-fetcher to remove this discussion from its in-memory list and optionally updates the removedId URL parameter for state reflection.

Provide a global search form in the page header. When the user submits a search term, navigate to search_results.html with query set to the input value as a URL parameter. This component does not inspect or modify bookmark data; it only handles navigation based on the user’s search intent.

Coordinate reading and writing of URL query parameters (sort, order, q, page, perPage, removedId). On initial load, parse the URL to provide initial state to bookmarks-data-fetcher, bookmarks-toolbar, and bookmarks-pagination. When any of these components change sort, filter, pagination, or removal state, update the URL accordingly using history APIs without reloading the page, and notify dependent components of the new state.

layout.grid_system: Implicit centered column layout likely based on a 12‑column grid with a wide central content span (approx. 8–10 columns) and large left/right gutters.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a strict top-to-bottom, single-column composition: a full-width black navigation bar at the very top (global, outside this component set), followed by a tall, off-white header band for the Bookmarks page heading and the header search. Below this header band, a centered main content column contains (in order) the bookmarks toolbar, the vertically stacked bookmarks list (composed of bookmark-item cards), and finally the pagination controls. Data-fetching and URL state sync run invisibly around this column, driving state for search, filters, and pagination without adding visual clutter.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a strict top-to-bottom, single-column composition: a full-width black navigation bar at the very top (global, outside this component set), followed by a tall, off-white header band for the Bookmarks page heading and the header search. Below this header band, a centered main content column contains (in order) the bookmarks toolbar, the vertically stacked bookmarks list (composed of bookmark-item cards), and finally the pagination controls. Data-fetching and URL state sync run invisibly around this column, driving state for search, filters, and pagination without adding visual clutter.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The black navigation bar occupies roughly the top 8–10% of the viewport. Beneath it, an off-white header band takes up approximately 20–30% of the viewport height, with its content anchored toward the lower part of that band, leaving generous empty space above. The main bookmarks content column below uses about 60–70% of the page width, centered, with left and right gutters each around 15–20% of the width. Within this main column, cards and sections use 24–32px internal padding, 24–32px vertical gaps between cards, and 48–64px vertical separation between major sections (header band → toolbar, list → pagination).
layout_strategies.content_density: The density remains intentionally low: a small number of controls (filters, sort, search, pagination) arranged with significant whitespace, and only a handful of bookmark cards typically visible above the fold. Each bookmark-item card has spacious padding and comfortable line-height, with no dense clusters of icons or labels. Text is allowed to breathe, and there is a clear hierarchy: page heading and search, then bookmark cards, then pagination, with filters/sort controls visually subtle and light.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye is first drawn to the high-contrast black navigation bar at the top, scanning left to right. It then drops into the large, quiet off-white header band, where it quickly finds the left-aligned page title and then is pulled to the right by the search icon/form. From there, the gaze naturally falls straight down into the center of the page, landing on the first bookmark card in the list. Within each card, the flow moves from the avatar and author/thread meta on the left, across to the overflow menu on the right, then down into the main thread title and excerpt. Finally, attention ends at the bottom center of the list on the pagination controls, suggesting onward navigation.

layout.overall_description: The Bookmarks page feels like a calm, editorial reading surface framed inside a minimalist forum shell. A solid black navigation bar spans the very top of the viewport, above a broad off-white band that occupies about a quarter of the screen height. In that band, near its lower edge, a simple 'Bookmarks' heading anchors the left side while a single, restrained search control sits aligned to the right, leaving most of the band empty and quiet. Below this, a centered main column—about two-thirds of the page width—holds the functional content. At the top of this column, a slim row of understated filter/sort controls sits with ample breathing room, followed by a vertical stack of bookmark cards, each full-width within the column, bordered, and generously padded. Only one or two cards are typically visible without scrolling, preserving the sparse, reading-focused feel. At the very bottom of the column, surrounded by extra whitespace, a small pagination row is centered, offering subtle navigation without demanding attention. Non-visual infrastructure like data fetching and URL state syncing wrap around this column, keeping interactions smooth while the visual experience remains quiet and uncluttered.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The bookmarks-data-fetcher conceptually wraps the entire main content area (header band, toolbar, list, and pagination), but it has no permanent visible UI. It occupies the same central width as the main content column (about 60–70% of the page width), starting just below the global navigation and header band. When data is loading, it temporarily surfaces as a minimal overlay in the center of the main column: either a single off-white skeleton card or a simple monochrome spinner, vertically centered within the visible portion of the column. This overlay does not extend into the left and right gutters; it remains aligned with the card stack, reinforcing the single-column structure. Once loading completes, the overlay disappears and the actual bookmark-item cards occupy this same space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Logically wraps the main content column and can temporarily overlay that column during loading states. The overlay is centered within the column and scrolls with the page; it does not fix to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The bookmarks-toolbar sits at the top of the main content column, directly below the off-white header band and aligned to the same central width (approximately 60–70% of the page). There is a generous vertical gap of about 48–64px between the bottom of the header band and the toolbar, maintaining the airy transition from header to content. Within the toolbar row, filter and sort controls are laid out horizontally along a single line, hugging the left side of the column. Any secondary controls (e.g., a 'Clear filters' text link) appear to the right of the primary controls but still within this same row, separated by 16–24px. The row itself is low in visual weight: no heavy backgrounds, just subtle text labels and simple underline or border indicators, leaving the majority of the row’s height as whitespace. There is about 24px of vertical space between the bottom edge of the toolbar and the first bookmark-item card, so the toolbar feels distinct but not detached from the list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; it scrolls away with the content. It is anchored to the top of the main content column and does not stick to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The bookmarks-pagination component is positioned at the bottom of the main content column, directly below the bookmarks-list. There is a noticeable vertical gap—around 32–48px—between the last bookmark-item card and the pagination row, emphasizing that pagination is a separate, secondary action. The pagination controls themselves (e.g., previous/next text links and page numbers) are horizontally centered within the column while preserving the wide left/right gutters outside the column. Controls are spaced 16–24px apart to maintain the minimalist, spacious feel, and there is no heavy background; elements are likely simple text or subtle outlined buttons. The pagination row occupies only a small amount of height, with additional empty off-white space below it before the end of the viewport, ensuring it does not visually compete with the bookmark cards.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; it sits at the natural end of the bookmarks list within the scrollable page and does not fix to the bottom of the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The bookmarks-list is the main vertical stack occupying the central content column beneath the bookmarks-toolbar. It shares the same centered alignment and width (about 60–70% of the page), with the left and right gutters remaining empty. The top of the list starts approximately 24px below the toolbar, and each bookmark-item card within the list spans the full width of the list container. Cards are stacked in a linear column with 24–32px of vertical space between them, maintaining the low-density, editorial feel. The list grows downward as more bookmarks are present, extending beyond the fold and requiring vertical scrolling. The background of the list itself remains the off-white of the page, while individual cards appear as slightly elevated or bordered white panels, making the list read as a sequence of discrete, easily scannable pieces rather than a continuous block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; it fills the main content column between the toolbar and pagination, expanding vertically as needed and scrolling with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each bookmark-item renders as a full-width card within the central column, visually analogous to the forum post card in the mockup. The card background is a clean white or a slightly lighter shade than the page’s off-white, with a subtle 1px gray border (and optionally a very soft shadow) to clearly separate it from the background. The card has consistent padding of 24–32px on all sides. At the top of the card, a header row spans the full width: on the left, a circular avatar (around 32px diameter) is placed near the card’s left padding edge; immediately to its right, a compact block of meta text (e.g., thread title in a medium weight, forum name, author, and timestamp) is left-aligned and vertically centered with the avatar. On the far right of this header row, aligned to the card’s right padding edge, sits a minimalist overflow icon (such as a vertical ellipsis), occupying an invisible hit area about 24–32px square but visually appearing as a small icon to preserve the sparse look. Below the header row, separated by 16–24px of vertical whitespace, the body section runs full width: the thread title or key line (if not already in the header) appears first, followed by one or two lines of excerpt text, set with comfortable line-height and ample margin above and below. Optional tag labels or small metadata (e.g., reply count, last activity) may appear along the bottom of the card, aligned left, in a smaller, muted type. The entire card is clickable, with hover states expressed through a very subtle border darkening or shadow rather than bold color changes, keeping the minimalist aesthetic intact.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, but the entire card surface acts as a single large click target. It sits within the vertical stack of the bookmarks-list and respects the column’s horizontal centering and margins.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header-search-form is positioned within the tall off-white header band that sits directly under the global black navigation. This band spans the full browser width, but its contents are constrained to the same central width as the main content column (about 60–70% of the page), centered horizontally. The page title (e.g., 'Bookmarks') appears on the left side of this central band area, and the header-search-form is aligned to the far right edge of this same area on the same horizontal line. Vertically, both title and search are anchored in the lower portion of the band—roughly the bottom third—leaving 48–64px of empty off-white space above them, which reinforces the quiet, open feeling. Visually, the search form is very minimal: in its default state it may appear as a lone search icon with an invisible hit area of 24–32px square, or a slender, border-only input field that is no more than 30–40% of the central width. There is at least 16–24px of whitespace around it on all sides so it does not feel cramped. When focused or expanded, the input may extend slightly leftward, but it must remain visually light, using only strokes and text, no heavy fills, to match the monochrome editorial style.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; it scrolls away with the header band. It occupies a fixed position at the right edge of the header band’s central content area and does not stick to the top once the user scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The url-state-sync component is non-visual and conceptually overlays the entire Bookmarks page content area. It occupies no explicit space on the page and does not introduce any visible UI elements. Instead, it binds the state of the header-search-form, bookmarks-toolbar (filters/sort), bookmarks-list, and bookmarks-pagination to the browser’s URL. Spatially, it can be imagined as an invisible layer spanning the same bounds as the central content column and header band, reacting to user interactions within that area and updating the list without changing the visual layout or density.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Invisible, logical layer that spans the header and main content column; it has no independent position or scrolling behavior and simply follows the page layout.

name: User Profile
title: User Profile
description: Public profile page that shows a member's basic information and recent activity, and lets you start a private message with them.

core_features: Load and display a member's public profile information (display name, optional title, bio, avatar, join date, reputation, activity counts) using getMemberProfile based on the memberProfileId URL parameter.
Show a list of the member's recent discussions with navigation into each discussion.
Show a list of the member's recent replies with navigation into the related discussions.
Provide a 'Send Private Message' action that navigates to messages.html pre-addressed to this member via recipientProfileId URL parameter.
Provide a header search input that, when submitted from this page, navigates to search_results.html with the query URL parameter.
user_workflows:
View another member's profile:
1) User clicks a username or avatar on discussion.html, index.html, search_results.html, topic.html, or messages.html.
2) Browser navigates to user_profile.html with ?memberProfileId=<selected MemberProfile.id>.
3) On page load, profile-loader reads memberProfileId from the URL.
4) profile-loader calls getMemberProfile(memberProfileId) and stores the returned profile, recentDiscussions, and recentReplies in page state.
5) profile-header, profile-stats, and profile-bio render the profile data.
6) recent-discussions-list and recent-replies-list render the activity lists.

Open a discussion from the member's recent discussions:
1) User scrolls to the recent discussions list.
2) User clicks a discussion title row.
3) recent-discussions-list reads the clicked Discussion.id as activityDiscussionId.
4) The component updates window.location to discussion.html?discussionId=<activityDiscussionId>.
5) discussion.html loads and displays that discussion.

Open a discussion from the member's recent replies:
1) User scrolls to the recent replies list.
2) User clicks on a reply item (title or excerpt).
3) recent-replies-list reads the clicked Post.discussionId as replyDiscussionId.
4) The component updates window.location to discussion.html?discussionId=<replyDiscussionId>.
5) discussion.html loads and positions the user according to its own logic (e.g., at the top or to the specific reply).

Start a private message to this member:
1) While viewing user_profile.html, user clicks the 'Message' button in profile-header.
2) profile-header reads the current memberProfileId from URL state.
3) profile-header updates window.location to messages.html?recipientProfileId=<memberProfileId>.
4) messages.html loads and uses recipientProfileId to pre-fill the recipient in the messaging form.

Run a search from the profile page:
1) User types a term into the header search input.
2) User submits the form (pressing Enter or clicking a search button).
3) header-search-form prevents default page reload and reads the entered value as searchTerm.
4) header-search-form updates window.location to search_results.html?query=<encoded searchTerm>.
5) search_results.html loads and shows results for the query.
interactions: On initial page load, the profile-loader component reads memberProfileId from the URL and triggers getMemberProfile; if memberProfileId is missing or invalid, it sets an error state rendered by profile-error-state.
Clicking the 'Message' button in profile-header navigates to messages.html with recipientProfileId set to the current memberProfileId.
Clicking a discussion title in recent-discussions-list navigates to discussion.html with discussionId set to the clicked Discussion.id.
Clicking a reply item in recent-replies-list navigates to discussion.html with discussionId set to the clicked Post.discussionId.
Submitting the header search form in header-search-form navigates to search_results.html with query set to the entered search term.

If getMemberProfile returns successfully but some optional fields (such as bio, avatarUrl, title, or reputationScore) are absent, the respective components (profile-header, profile-bio, profile-stats) adjust their rendering to omit or generically placeholder those fields without using hardcoded content.

If the user navigates directly to a different memberProfileId via the browser address bar or internal navigation, profile-loader detects the change in memberProfileId and re-invokes getMemberProfile for the new id.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary page state is driven by the memberProfileId URL parameter and the result of the getMemberProfile interface. On load, user_profile.html parses memberProfileId from the query string (e.g., user_profile.html?memberProfileId=<id>). If memberProfileId is present, profile-loader calls getMemberProfile(memberProfileId) and stores the returned { profile, recentDiscussions, recentReplies } in memory for the duration of the page session. All render components bind to this in-memory state; no profile-related information is persisted outside the page or in localStorage. If memberProfileId is missing or cannot be used to retrieve a profile, the page sets a 'notFound' or 'error' state and renders profile-error-state instead of the normal profile components. Navigation away from the page uses URL parameters only: clicking 'Message' constructs messages.html?recipientProfileId=<current memberProfileId>; clicking a discussion or reply constructs discussion.html?discussionId=<selected discussion id>; submitting search constructs search_results.html?query=<search term>. If the URL's memberProfileId changes (for example, via manual address bar edit or client-side navigation), profile-loader listens for that change and re-runs getMemberProfile with the new memberProfileId, replacing the in-memory state so all bound components rerender with the new member's data.

components.functionality: Reads memberProfileId from the URL, invokes getMemberProfile with it, and stores the resulting profile, recentDiscussions, and recentReplies in page-level state for other components to consume. Handles loading, success, and error/not-found states.
event_handlers: onInit: When user_profile.html first loads, parse the memberProfileId query parameter. If present, call getMemberProfile(memberProfileId). If absent, set error state.
onParamChange: When the memberProfileId URL parameter changes while on this page, re-invoke getMemberProfile with the new memberProfileId and update bound state.
onLoadSuccess: When getMemberProfile returns successfully, store profile, recentDiscussions, and recentReplies in page state and clear any previous error state.
onLoadError: If getMemberProfile fails (e.g., invalid id or server error), set an appropriate error/notFound flag for profile-error-state to render.

components.functionality: Displays the member's primary identity information (displayName, optional title, avatar) and a 'Send Private Message' button that navigates to messages.html with the current memberProfileId as recipientProfileId.
event_handlers: onRender: When profile data is available, render the display name, title (if present), avatar (or a non-hardcoded placeholder if avatarUrl is missing), join date derived from createdAt, and reputationScore if present.
onClickMessageButton: When the 'Message' button is clicked, read URL.memberProfileId and navigate to messages.html?recipientProfileId=<memberProfileId> without modifying any other parameters.

components.functionality: Shows basic numeric stats about the member's activity such as total discussions created, total replies posted, and reputation score, if these values are available.
event_handlers: onRender: When profile data is available, render each stat if its corresponding value is defined; omit stats whose values are not provided by the API response.

components.functionality: Displays the member's bio/about text if provided, or hides the bio section completely if no bio is available.
event_handlers: onRender: If bio is a non-empty value, render it in a read-only text block. If bio is undefined or empty, do not render a bio block or label.

components.functionality: Lists the member's recent discussions with key metadata (such as title and createdAt). Each item is clickable to navigate to the corresponding discussion.html page.
event_handlers: onRender: When recentDiscussions is available, iterate over the array and render each Discussion as a list item showing its title and any other available metadata from the Discussion model.
onClickDiscussionItem: When a discussion item is clicked, read that item's Discussion.id as activityDiscussionId and navigate to discussion.html?discussionId=<activityDiscussionId>.

components.functionality: Lists the member's recent replies (posts) with context such as a snippet of the body and the related discussion title if available from joined data. Each item navigates to the corresponding discussion.
event_handlers: onRender: When recentReplies is available, iterate over the array and render each Post as a list item showing an excerpt of body and timestamp derived from createdAt. If additional joined data (like related Discussion title) is provided by the interface, include it; otherwise render only what is available.
onClickReplyItem: When a reply item is clicked, read that item's Post.discussionId as replyDiscussionId and navigate to discussion.html?discussionId=<replyDiscussionId>.

components.functionality: Provides a search input in the page header that, when submitted, navigates to search_results.html with a query URL parameter.
event_handlers: onChangeInput: Update FormState.searchTerm in memory as the user types into the search field.
onSubmit: Prevent default form submission, read FormState.searchTerm as searchTerm, URL-encode it, and navigate to search_results.html?query=<searchTerm>. Do not call any interfaces from this page.

components.functionality: Displays an appropriate message or fallback UI if the profile cannot be loaded due to missing or invalid memberProfileId, or due to an error in getMemberProfile.
event_handlers: onRender: If profile-loader indicates an error or notFound state, render an error message or guidance based on whether memberProfileId was missing or the lookup failed. Hide this component when there is no error.
onRetryClick: If a retry action is offered, re-trigger getMemberProfile with the current URL.memberProfileId through profile-loader.

components.functionality: Shows a loading indicator while getMemberProfile is in progress, and hides when data is loaded or an error occurs.
event_handlers: onRender: When profile-loader.isLoading is true, render a non-specific loading indicator. When false, hide this indicator.

layout.grid_system: Implicit centered column layout likely based on a 12‑column grid with a wide central content span (approx. 8–10 columns) and large left/right gutters.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a linear, top-to-bottom flow with a full-width, black navigation bar at the very top containing the search form on the right. Below the nav, a tall, off-white profile masthead spans the full width and contains the profile header, stats, and bio, all aligned to a centered content column. Under this masthead, the main profile activity area appears as a vertical stack of large, full-width cards within the same central column: first the recent discussions list card, then the recent replies list card. Loading and error states temporarily replace or overlay this central column, while the data loader orchestrates which of those states is visible.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a linear, top-to-bottom flow with a full-width, black navigation bar at the very top containing the search form on the right. Below the nav, a tall, off-white profile masthead spans the full width and contains the profile header, stats, and bio, all aligned to a centered content column. Under this masthead, the main profile activity area appears as a vertical stack of large, full-width cards within the same central column: first the recent discussions list card, then the recent replies list card. Loading and error states temporarily replace or overlay this central column, while the data loader orchestrates which of those states is visible.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The black navigation bar occupies roughly the top 8–10% of the viewport height. Below it, the profile masthead consumes about 20–30% of the viewport height, with generous empty off-white space above and around the profile information to echo the mockup’s openness. The central column itself spans roughly 60–70% of the viewport width and is centered, with left and right gutters consuming about 15–20% each. Each activity list card occupies the full width of this column and about 40–60% of the viewport height when populated, with 24–32px internal padding and 32–48px gaps between cards. Vertical gaps between major sections (nav → masthead, masthead → first card, between cards) follow the 48–64px range.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is intentionally low. The navigation bar has very few elements and plenty of horizontal breathing room. The profile masthead shows only essential information (avatar, name, minimal meta, core stats, concise bio) with large vertical spacing between each element. The activity list cards show a limited number of items within the fold (typically 2–3), with each item itself likely following the sparse card style of the main forum posts. Text blocks, especially the bio and activity titles, are given ample line-height and margin, and there are very few buttons or controls visible at once.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow starts at the high-contrast black navigation bar at the top, reading left-to-right across any nav items and ending on the right-aligned search form. From there, the eye drops into the light off-white masthead, guided toward the center of the page by wide empty side gutters. Within this masthead, attention first lands on the profile header (avatar and username), then moves slightly downward to the stats row, then to the bio text. As the user scrolls or continues downward, the eye is drawn into the first large activity card (recent discussions) and then to the second (recent replies), following a simple vertical reading pattern. Overflow or action controls (like menus within lists) are aligned to the right edges of cards, acting as secondary focal points that do not compete with the main reading flow.

layout.overall_description: The page presents as a quiet, editorial-style profile view set against an off-white background. At the very top, a stark black navigation bar stretches full-bleed across the viewport, feeling solid and anchored; its contents are sparse, with the search control tucked to the far right. Below that, the interface opens up into a tall band of empty off-white space, within which a centered column holds the user’s profile identity: avatar, name, minimal metadata, a compact stats row, and a short bio, all spaced generously with clear separation. Left and right gutters remain empty, framing the central column and pushing the eye inward. As the user continues down, the masthead gives way to a sequence of large, card-like sections for recent discussions and recent replies, each card occupying the full width of the column with strong padding and clean typography. Only a handful of items are visible at a time, maintaining a calm, reading-first feel. When loading or error states appear, they take over this central column space, typically centering simple, monochrome indicators or messages while preserving the overall whitespace and structure, so the profile page always feels controlled and uncluttered.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The profile-loader operates as an invisible layout orchestrator tied to the central content column that begins directly beneath the full-width navigation bar. It conceptually occupies the entire vertical stack from the top of the profile masthead down through the activity cards, determining whether to show the loading indicator, error state, or the full profile content. Visually it does not render its own chrome; instead it controls whether the masthead (profile-header, profile-stats, profile-bio) and the two activity list cards are present in the centered column, or whether a single status display is shown in their place. Spatially, this means that the loader’s area aligns with the column that spans roughly 60–70% of the viewport width, centered, with 15–20% gutters on either side and extending from just below the nav bar to the bottom of the viewport and beyond as content scrolls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual container that controls which child components occupy the central column; it spans the full height of the page content area and does not affect fixed elements like the top navigation.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The profile-header is positioned near the top of the main content area within a broad off-white masthead that starts immediately beneath the full-width black navigation bar. The masthead itself is largely empty above and to the sides of the header, so the profile-header visually sits roughly in the upper-middle of this band, leaving about 48–64px of whitespace between the bottom of the nav bar and the top of the header content. Horizontally, the profile-header is constrained to the central content column that spans about 60–70% of the page width, centered, with wide empty gutters on the left and right. Within this column, the profile-header forms a subtle two-column layout: on the left, the avatar (small circular or square) and stacked text (username as a strong, larger title, with a smaller line beneath for handle or join date), and on the far right, aligned with the column’s right edge, an unobtrusive actions area (e.g., a simple text link or icon for 'Message' or overflow options). The left block occupies roughly 70–80% of the column width, while the right actions align flush to the right 20–30%. Vertical space around the header is generous: approximately 24–32px above the username and 24–32px below, before transitioning into the stats row. There is no card border around the masthead; the header feels like typography resting directly on the off-white background.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element anchored near the top of the scrollable main content area, directly under the fixed navigation bar; it scrolls away with the page and does not stick.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The profile-stats component sits immediately below the profile-header within the same off-white masthead band and within the same centered content column. There is a vertical gap of about 16–24px between the bottom of the profile-header (e.g., below the username/meta text) and the top of the stats row. The stats are arranged horizontally as a low-density row of 2–4 key metrics (e.g., posts, replies, join date), each represented by a short label and a number stacked or side-by-side. The stats row spans the full width of the central column but uses only a portion of its height, with plenty of off-white space above and below to preserve the airy feel. Metrics are left-aligned with the profile text, and there is no heavy visual divider; instead, subtle spacing (16–24px) separates each metric block. The rightmost part of the row is left mostly empty, aligning with the quiet, minimalist aesthetic and maintaining strong negative space along the right side. Below the stats, another 16–24px gap leads into the profile-bio text block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, horizontally oriented element that flows inline between the header and bio within the masthead; it resizes responsively within the fixed central column width but does not change position.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The profile-bio is the final element in the masthead stack, positioned directly beneath the profile-stats within the same centered content column. There is approximately 16–24px of vertical space between the stats row and the start of the bio. The bio presents as a simple text block with no border or background change, aligned to the left edge of the column and spanning roughly 70–80% of the column width to maintain optimal line length for reading, leaving a ragged right margin of off-white space. The text runs in one narrow column, with comfortable line-height and internal paragraph spacing (e.g., 8–16px between paragraphs) to keep the content feeling light. The bio extends only as far down as the content requires; below it, a larger gap of about 48–64px opens before the first activity card begins, visually separating the identity masthead from the activity section. The surrounding off-white space in the masthead remains unbroken, reinforcing the editorial, reading-focused character.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static text block that flows vertically under the stats and above the activity cards; it reflows text within the fixed column width on responsive breakpoints but maintains its alignment and generous margins.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The recent-discussions-list is presented as the first major card-like section beneath the profile masthead. It is positioned centrally on the page, directly under the masthead’s bottom edge, with a pronounced vertical gap of about 48–64px separating it from the profile-bio. The component occupies the full width of the central content column (about 60–70% of the viewport width), and each side is flanked by 15–20% width gutters of empty background. Visually it appears as a minimalist card: a subtle, slightly brighter or bordered rectangle sitting on the off-white background with 24–32px internal padding on all sides. At the top of the card, a quiet header row runs full-width: on the left, a small section title such as 'Recent discussions' aligned with the column’s left edge; on the right, an unobtrusive icon or text link (if any actions exist) aligned flush to the card’s right edge, mirroring the two-column header pattern seen in the mockup. Below this header row, the list of discussions flows vertically as full-width items, each item formatted similarly to the forum post cards (avatar and meta on the left, possible overflow icon on the right, title/body text beneath), but with only 2–4 items visible within the initial viewport to maintain low density. Vertical spacing between items in this list is around 24px, and there is 24–32px of padding between the last item and the bottom of the card. Beneath the card, another 32–48px gap leads to the recent-replies-list card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level card that participates in the vertical scroll; it stretches in height based on the number of visible items but remains tied to the centered column and does not overlap adjacent sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The recent-replies-list appears as a second, slightly subordinate card directly below the recent-discussions-list, continuing the central stack within the same content column. A vertical gap of about 32–48px separates the bottom edge of the discussions card from the top edge of the replies card, maintaining visual distinction while keeping the two activity sections strongly related. This card mirrors the dimensions and style of the discussions card: it spans the full width of the central column (60–70% of the page width) with matching 24–32px inner padding. Its header row at the top follows the same two-column layout—left-aligned title text like 'Recent replies' and any optional action or filter control on the far right—aligned flush with the card’s edges. The list of reply items stacks vertically beneath this header, with each reply item formatted similarly to a condensed post card or reply snippet, maintaining generous vertical spacing (16–24px between items). Fewer items may be shown by default than in the discussions list to subtly suggest secondary importance. The card ends with 24–32px of bottom padding before the off-white background resumes. Below this card, the page continues with a sizable buffer of 48–64px before any footer or subsequent content, allowing the last card to feel properly framed.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level card fixed within the vertical flow; its height grows with content and scrolls naturally, always constrained to the centered column without sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header-search-form is placed within the full-width black navigation bar that sits fixed at the top of the viewport. Horizontally, it is aligned to the far right side of the nav bar, with a buffer of about 24–32px between its right edge and the browser window edge and similar spacing from any adjacent nav items on its left. The visual expression of the search form is extremely minimal, consistent with the mockup: either as a simple magnifying-glass icon or a very compact, borderless input field with a subtle underline. Regardless of visual style, the clickable/tappable hit area is approximately 24–32px in both width and height, but the visible icon or text is small so as not to dominate the bar. Vertically, the search form is centered within the nav bar’s height (around 64px tall), maintaining equal padding above and below. Because the nav bar is full-bleed black, the search control appears as a small, light-colored element at its right edge, balancing the left-aligned nav items and acting as a secondary visual anchor before the user’s gaze drops into the off-white profile masthead.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed in place within the navigation bar, which itself is fixed to the top of the viewport; the search form remains visible as the user scrolls the profile content beneath it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The profile-error-state replaces the normal profile content within the central column when an error occurs. It is positioned in the same space typically occupied by the masthead and activity cards: centered horizontally within the 60–70% width content column and starting a short distance below the nav bar (with a 64–96px gap to keep the top airy). Visually, it appears as a simple, medium-sized card or text block on the off-white background. If styled as a card, it uses the same subtle card container as the activity lists, with 24–32px padding and a width matching the central column. The error message is centered or left-aligned within this container, with a brief title line and one or two lines of descriptive text, and possibly a single, understated action link (e.g., 'Retry') aligned to the left or center. There is generous whitespace above and below the message (at least 32px internal padding) so that even the error state feels calm and uncluttered. Below the error state, the page can continue with empty space or a simple footer, but no other primary profile content is shown concurrently, making the error clearly understandable.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily occupies the central column area in place of other profile components, scrolling normally with the page; it does not overlay the fixed navigation bar and disappears entirely when content successfully loads.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The profile-loading-indicator appears while the profile data is being fetched and occupies the same region where the masthead and activity content would normally be. It is centered within the central column both horizontally and, as much as possible, vertically within the initial viewport. After the fixed black nav bar at the top, there is an empty off-white space of about 20–30% of the viewport height, and then the loading indicator sits roughly in the middle of the remaining area. The indicator itself is extremely minimal: for example, a small monochrome spinner or a simple 'Loading profile…' line of text in a muted gray, with perhaps 8–16px of space between icon and text. The width of the indicator is narrow—no more than 30–40% of the column width—emphasizing the surrounding negative space. No cards, borders, or extra decorations are shown; the off-white background of the page remains visible around the indicator, maintaining the calm aesthetic while clearly signaling that the page is in a transient state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily shown in the central column instead of other profile components; it scrolls with the page if the user scrolls, but because it is compact and placed high in the viewport, it typically remains visible without requiring scrolling while loading is in progress.

name: Messages
title: Messages
description: Compose and send private messages to other members and review previously sent messages.

core_features: Display a message composition form with recipient (optionally pre-filled from recipientProfileId URL parameter), subject, and message body fields, and submit via sendPrivateMessage.
Validate private message content before sending, including presence of recipientProfileId and subject/body text and disallowing obvious external links in the body.
Send a private message using sendPrivateMessage and surface the returned statusMessage and message details in the UI.
Retrieve a paginated list of sent private messages using getSentMessages with page and pageSize parameters.
Display the list of sent messages, including recipient display name, subject, sent timestamp, and read status.
Provide pagination controls for navigating through pages of sent messages and updating the page URL parameter accordingly.
Allow navigation to a recipient’s public profile (user_profile.html) by clicking the recipient’s name or avatar in a sent message row, passing memberProfileId=recipientProfileId.
Allow use of the global/header search bar while on this page, navigating to search_results.html with query URL parameter when submitted.
user_workflows:
Compose and send a message from a member profile:
1. User clicks a "Message" action from user_profile.html, which opens messages.html with recipientProfileId set in the URL.
2. On page load, the Messages controller reads recipientProfileId from the URL and pre-fills the recipient field in the composition form.
3. User enters a subject and a short body (for example, a 3–5 sentence request for portfolio feedback) without including external links.
4. User submits the compose form.
5. The form validates that recipientProfileId is present, subject and body are non-empty, and that the body does not contain disallowed link patterns.
6. On successful validation, the page calls sendPrivateMessage with recipientProfileId, subject, and body.
7. On success, the UI displays the statusMessage returned by sendPrivateMessage and optionally adds the returned message to the top of the sent messages list.
8. If the API returns an error, the UI shows an error state and allows the user to correct and re-submit.

Compose a new message starting from the Messages page without a pre-set recipient:
1. User opens messages.html directly (without a recipientProfileId URL parameter) via the user menu.
2. On page load, the Messages controller initializes the compose form with an empty recipient field and loads the first page of sent messages via getSentMessages.
3. User attempts to type or select a recipient in the recipient field (implementation-specific) but cannot successfully send until a valid recipientProfileId is available in the form state.
4. If the user submits the form without a valid recipientProfileId, validation fails and an inline error explains that the recipient needs to be selected (e.g., by starting from a member profile).
5. Once a valid recipientProfileId is present (for example, if the user navigates from a profile link and the page reloads with the URL parameter), steps 3–7 from the first workflow apply.

Review previously sent messages:
1. User opens messages.html (with or without recipientProfileId); page parameter may or may not be present.
2. The Messages controller determines the current page index from the page URL parameter; if not provided or invalid, it uses the first page.
3. The controller calls getSentMessages with the current page and configured pageSize.
4. When getSentMessages returns, the sent messages list component renders each PrivateMessage in the messages array.
5. User can scroll through the list to read subjects, recipients, sent times, and read/unread indicators.
6. User can click on a recipient’s name or avatar in any message row, navigating to user_profile.html with memberProfileId set to that message’s recipientProfileId.

Paginate through sent messages:
1. After the sent messages list is rendered, the pagination component calculates the number of available pages based on totalCount and pageSize returned by getSentMessages.
2. User clicks a pagination control (e.g., next page or a specific page index).
3. The pagination component updates the page URL parameter to the selected page and triggers a navigation or state refresh.
4. On navigation or state change, the Messages controller reads the updated page parameter and re-calls getSentMessages with the new page value.
5. The sent messages list re-renders with messages for the newly selected page.

Use search from the Messages page:
1. While on messages.html, user enters a query into the header search bar.
2. User submits the search form.
3. The header search form navigates to search_results.html, including the entered search term as query in the URL parameters.
4. The Messages page is left and search_results.html handles the query display logic.
interactions: On page load (messages.html): read URL parameters recipientProfileId and page; initialize compose form state and pagination state; call getSentMessages with the resolved page and a configured pageSize.

Recipient field interaction: if recipientProfileId is present in the URL on load, the recipient field is locked or clearly indicated as selected; if not present, the field remains empty and is required for message sending, with validation preventing submission until a valid value is set.
Subject input interaction: user types text; the field is validated for non-empty content on blur and on form submit.

Body textarea interaction: user enters message content; validation checks for required content and that the body does not contain obvious external link patterns (for example, substrings resembling URLs). If invalid, an error message is shown and sending is blocked.

Compose form submission: when the user triggers submit, the form runs validation; if valid, the page calls sendPrivateMessage; while awaiting response, the submit button is disabled and an in-progress state is shown; on success, the form clears or optionally retains recipient, and the statusMessage is shown; the returned PrivateMessage may be inserted into the sent messages list.

Sent messages list click interaction: clicking on a recipient’s display name or avatar in a message row triggers navigation to user_profile.html with memberProfileId set to that row’s recipientProfileId.
Pagination interaction: clicking next/previous or a page number updates the page parameter in the URL and triggers a refresh flow that calls getSentMessages for the new page.
Header search interaction: submitting the search term from the header search form triggers navigation to search_results.html with query set to the entered term.

Error handling interaction: if getSentMessages or sendPrivateMessage returns an error, an error component displays the error information and provides a way to retry the failed action (e.g., retry button for loading sent messages, re-submit button for sending a message).
page_functionality.state_logic: The messages.html page relies on URL parameters and internal transient state only. On initial load and on every navigation back to this page, the controller reads the URL parameters: (1) recipientProfileId (string, optional): if present, it is stored in the compose form state as the current recipientProfileId and used immediately for sending messages; the recipient field in the form is pre-filled or locked to that value so the user cannot accidentally change it without changing the URL. If absent, the recipient field is empty and the form validation requires a recipientProfileId before submission.
