(2) page (number, optional): if provided and valid, this value is used as the current page index when calling getSentMessages; if not provided or invalid, the page defaults to the first page. The controller calls getSentMessages with the resolved page and a configured pageSize and binds the returned messages, totalCount, page, and pageSize into the sent messages list and pagination components. When the user interacts with pagination controls, the controls update the page URL parameter (e.g., messages.html?page=<selectedPage>[&recipientProfileId=<currentRecipient>]) and trigger a re-run of getSentMessages for that new page. When the user navigates from a user profile, user_profile.html constructs the messages.html URL including recipientProfileId; the Messages page then uses that parameter on load to pre-populate the recipient in the composer. No localStorage or other persistent client storage is used; all persistent state is derived from URL parameters and API responses from getSentMessages and sendPrivateMessage.

components.functionality: Orchestrates the Messages page by reading URL parameters, initializing state, coordinating calls to getSentMessages and sendPrivateMessage through child components, and handling global error states.

Provides inputs for recipient, subject, and message body; performs validation (including detecting disallowed external links in the body); and triggers sendPrivateMessage when the user submits a valid form.

Displays the current page of sent private messages returned from getSentMessages, including recipient display name, subject, createdAt timestamp, and read status, and exposes row-level events such as navigating to the recipient’s profile.

Represents a single PrivateMessage entry within the sent messages list, mapping its fields to display attributes and exposing click events on the recipient name or avatar that navigate to the corresponding user_profile.html page.

Renders pagination elements based on totalCount, page, and pageSize from getSentMessages and allows the user to change pages by updating the page URL parameter and signaling the controller to reload messages.
Accepts a search term from the user while on the Messages page and, on submission, navigates away to search_results.html with the query URL parameter set to the entered term.

Displays status and feedback messages related to message sending (e.g., statusMessage from sendPrivateMessage) and loading sent messages (e.g., loading state, errors), and optionally offers retry actions.
Catches and displays unexpected errors occurring in child components such as failures from getSentMessages or sendPrivateMessage and provides a way to reset or retry those operations.

Below this, a single centered content column holds stacked full-width cards: first the message composer card, then the sent messages list made of individual message cards, with pagination controls anchored at the bottom of the column. Error and status elements appear inline within this same central column, in predictable positions near the top of the content.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a strictly linear, top-to-bottom flow that mirrors the mockup: a full-width global black navigation bar sits at the very top (assumed outside this page scope), followed by a tall, quiet off-white header band containing the page title and a right-aligned search control. Below this, a single centered content column holds stacked full-width cards: first the message composer card, then the sent messages list made of individual message cards, with pagination controls anchored at the bottom of the column. Error and status elements appear inline within this same central column, in predictable positions near the top of the content.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The navigation bar occupies roughly the top 8–10% of the viewport height. Directly beneath, an off-white header band extends downward for roughly 20–30% of the viewport height, most of which remains intentionally empty except for the heading, status, and a small search control, reinforcing the spacious feel. Beneath that, the main content column occupies about 60–70% of the viewport width, centered, with generous left and right gutters taking up the remaining 30–40% (15–20% per side). Within the column, each major card (composer and message rows) uses 24–32px internal padding and is separated vertically by 24–48px gaps, with an even larger gap (48–64px) between the header band and the first card and between the list and the pagination controls.

Only a few controls appear in the header (page title, subtle status text, a single search icon or compact field), and the main column shows one primary interaction surface—the composer card—plus a small number of message cards within the initial fold. Text lines are loosely arranged with comfortable line lengths and ample white space around and inside cards. The list view avoids compact, table-like density in favor of individual card rows with plenty of breathing room, echoing the reading-focused, editorial feel of the mockup.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow starts at the high-contrast black navigation bar at the top, then drops into the softer off-white header band where the eye is caught first by the left-aligned page title and any status text. From there, attention is gently pulled to the right by the compact, right-aligned search control before being drawn downward by the large negative space to the first prominent card: the message composer. Within the composer and message cards, the eye moves left-to-right across the top header row (avatar and meta information on the left, overflow or utility icons on the far right), then flows downward into the message content or form fields. Pagination at the bottom forms a low-key endpoint and escape valve for navigation, without competing for attention with the primary reading and composing tasks.

layout.overall_description: The Messages page reads like a quiet, monochrome editorial layout: after the full-width black navigation bar, you enter a tall, off-white band that feels mostly empty, with a simple 'Messages' heading on the left, a subtle status line beneath it, and a small, right-aligned search control that lightly punctuates the space. Below this calm header area, a single, centered content column—about two-thirds the width of the browser on desktop—floats in the middle of the page, framed by wide neutral gutters on both sides. The first element in this column is a large, card-like message composer with generous padding and a two-column header row (avatar and meta on the left, an unobtrusive utility control on the right), followed by a vertical stack of equally calm, full-width sent-message cards, each separated by comfortable vertical gaps. At the very bottom of this column, minimal pagination controls sit centered with extra breathing room, acting as a soft boundary to the page. Errors and status states appear as simple, inline cards or text lines within this same central column, preserving the linear reading flow and the overall sense of quiet spaciousness.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The messages-page-controller acts as the main page container immediately below the global black navigation bar. It stretches full-width edge to edge in the browser but defines a centered content column that occupies roughly 60–70% of the viewport width, with symmetrical left and right gutters consuming the remaining 30–40%. The controller provides generous top padding (around 48–64px) from the bottom of the navigation bar to the start of the header band content, creating the impression of a large off-white band that covers the upper 20–30% of the viewport. All child components (header search, status indicator, composer card, sent messages list, pagination, and error surfaces) are vertically stacked within this central column, following a strictly linear order.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, block-level layout frame that scrolls with the page; responsible for centering the main column and maintaining consistent gutters and vertical spacing across all child components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header-search-form sits within the upper off-white header band, anchored to the right edge of the central content column. Visually, it appears as a compact, icon-based search control or a very short search field, aligned horizontally with the page title on the left but flush with the column’s right edge, leaving ample blank space between them. It is positioned high within the header band, approximately 16–24px below the top of the central column’s content, with no heavy borders—just a simple, flat control that maintains the minimalist aesthetic. A generous vertical gap (48–64px) is preserved below this header area before the composer card begins, keeping the search visually secondary to the main reading and composing area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element pinned to the right side of the central column within the on-scroll header band; it scrolls away with the rest of the content rather than remaining sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The messages-status-indicator is positioned in the same off-white header band as the page title, aligned to the left edge of the central content column directly beneath the title text. It appears as a short, single-line status label (for example, 'All messages sent' or '3 unsent drafts') in a smaller, lighter typographic style, with about 8–16px of vertical spacing below the page title and 24–32px of negative space below it before the page transitions into the first major card. Its low visual weight and close proximity to the title keep it informative but unobtrusive, reinforcing the calm, editorial feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline text within the header band that scrolls naturally with the page; it does not reserve space beyond its single text line and reflows as the column width changes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The message-composer-form is the first major card in the central column, positioned directly beneath the header band with a generous vertical gap of about 48–64px, so it enters the viewport as the primary focal point after the header. The card spans the full width of the central column (roughly 60–70% of the page on desktop), with 24–32px of internal padding on all sides and a flat, very light background that subtly stands off from the off-white page. Its header row follows the two-column pattern from the mockup: on the left, an avatar circle and a 'New message' label with small meta text below; on the right, a discreet icon (such as an overflow or close icon) aligned to the card’s right edge. Below this header row, the form fields (recipient, subject, body, and send controls) stack vertically in a single column, each separated by 16–24px, so that the card’s overall height often occupies about 40–60% of the viewport, emphasizing composing as the primary action.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width card within the main scroll flow; it maintains its central alignment and padding at all viewport sizes, with height expanding as more content or validation messages appear.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sent-messages-list is placed directly below the message-composer-form, separated by a comfortable vertical gap of about 48px to clearly distinguish the composing area from the history area. It occupies the same central column width as the composer card and consists of a vertical stack of sent-message-row cards that each span the full column width. If a section title like 'Sent messages' is used, it appears left-aligned at the top of this region with 8–16px below it, followed by the first message card. The overall list reads as a calm, scrollable column of evenly spaced cards, rather than a compact table, preserving the airy, reading-focused tone of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static vertical list within the main scrollable column; the list’s height grows as more messages are present, pushing pagination controls downward while keeping each card’s spacing consistent.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each sent-message-row appears as an individual, full-width card within the sent-messages-list, aligned to the central column and sharing its width constraints. The card has 24–32px of padding on all sides and a minimum height of roughly 96–120px, giving each message enough breathing room even when the content snippet is short. Its header row mirrors the mockup’s two-column arrangement: on the left, a small recipient avatar with the recipient name and a one-line meta detail (such as send time) stacked or side-by-side; on the right, a vertical ellipsis or similar overflow icon aligned with the top of the card and the right edge. Beneath the header row, a short excerpt of the message body runs full width inside the card with comfortable line spacing, and each card is separated from the next by a vertical gap of about 24–32px, resulting in a low-density, readable stack.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static card items within the list; they maintain their internal two-column header and full-width body text while reflowing their text content as the column width shrinks on narrower viewports.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination-controls sit at the bottom of the central content column, directly beneath the last sent-message-row with an ample vertical gap of about 48–64px to visually separate navigation from content. They are horizontally centered within the column and kept compact, often just a previous/next pair and a couple of page numbers, with minimal styling to maintain the minimalist aesthetic. Surrounding white space on all sides ensures they do not compete with the message cards for attention, acting instead as a quiet endpoint and navigational aid for users scrolling to the bottom of the list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline control at the bottom of the main scroll area; it moves downward as the list grows and does not stick to the viewport edges.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The messages-error-boundary wraps the main content area of the central column and, when triggered, replaces or overlays the usual content region beneath the header band with a simple, flat error card. This error card spans the full width of the central column and is positioned with the same top margin (about 48–64px) that the composer card would normally have, keeping the visual structure consistent. Internally, it uses similar padding (24–32px) and typography as other cards but may employ a subtle border or accent line to signal the error state, while still remaining visually minimal. The error message is concise and centered left-to-right within the column, with additional content (like a retry button) stacked vertically beneath it, and no extra elements above or below to preserve clarity.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline fallback container within the central column that conditionally replaces the normal card stack; it scrolls naturally with the page and respects the same margins and gutters as the rest of the layout.

name: User Settings
title: User Settings
description: Manage your account profile, localization preferences, and notification settings, and save updates to your account.

core_features: Load the current user's account settings using getAccountSettings and populate the settings form.
Allow the user to edit their display name and bind it to AccountSettings.displayName.
Provide a bio text area with validation to keep the description between 20 and 30 words, bound to AccountSettings.bio.
Offer a timezone selector bound to AccountSettings.timezone using the available enum values.
Provide a daily summary / email digest notification toggle bound to AccountSettings.dailySummaryEnabled.
Validate all inputs and submit updated settings via updateAccountSettings.
Display success or error feedback after updateAccountSettings returns.
Enable use of the header search bar that navigates to search_results.html with the search query in the URL while remaining on the settings page until navigation occurs.
user_workflows: Viewing current settings: 1) User navigates to settings.html (optionally with URL parameters such as section and status). 2) On page load, the account-settings-loader component calls getAccountSettings. 3) When settings are returned, the profile, localization, and notification controls are populated with AccountSettings.displayName, AccountSettings.bio, AccountSettings.timezone, and AccountSettings.dailySummaryEnabled. 4) The visible section of the page is determined by the section URL parameter, if present.

Updating profile information: 1) User edits the display name field in the profile-settings-form. 2) User edits the bio text area; on each change, a word count is computed and validated to ensure the bio stays between 20 and 30 words. 3) If validation passes, the user activates the Save Changes action, which triggers the settings-save-controller to collect the current form values. 4) settings-save-controller calls updateAccountSettings with displayName, bio, timezone, and dailySummaryEnabled taken from the current form state. 5) On successful response, the controller updates the in-memory settings state with the returned AccountSettings object and shows a success message using the settings-feedback-banner. 6) Optionally, the URL is updated to include a status parameter (e.g., status=updated) so that a reload can still show the success state.

Updating localization and notification preferences: 1) User selects a timezone from the timezone-preferences-control dropdown; the selected enum value is stored in the form state. 2) User toggles the daily summary control in notification-preferences-control, updating the form state for dailySummaryEnabled. 3) User activates Save Changes, which triggers the same updateAccountSettings call as profile changes. 4) On success, the updated AccountSettings.timezone and AccountSettings.dailySummaryEnabled values are reflected in the form controls and a success message is shown.

Navigating between settings sections via URL state: 1) User clicks a tab or navigation control to switch between sections such as profile and preferences in the settings-section-tabs component. 2) The component updates the browser URL to settings.html with the section parameter set to the chosen section value (e.g., section=profile or section=preferences), without reloading the page if possible. 3) The visible panel is switched based on the section parameter; on full reload, the account-settings-loader reads the section parameter and initializes the view accordingly.

Using the header search bar from the settings page: 1) User enters a term into the header-search-form input while on settings.html. 2) On submit, the form reads the current input value as searchTerm. 3) The page navigates to search_results.html with the query parameter set (search_results.html?query=searchTerm). 4) No settings-related interfaces are called as a result of the search; navigation simply leaves the settings page.
interactions: On page load, the account-settings-loader automatically invokes getAccountSettings; a loading state is shown in place of the form until the call resolves.
Typing in the display name input updates the local form state for displayName and marks the form as dirty (indicating unsaved changes).

Typing in the bio text area updates the local bio value, recalculates the word count, and shows validation feedback if the word count is below 20 or above 30 words; the Save Changes button can be disabled or an error message shown when the bio is invalid.
Changing the timezone dropdown updates the local form state for timezone to the selected enum value without immediately calling updateAccountSettings.
Toggling the daily summary notification switch updates the local form state for dailySummaryEnabled; the switch reflects the boolean value from AccountSettings when the page loads.

Clicking the Save Changes / Update Profile button triggers form validation for all controlled fields (displayName, bio word count, timezone selection, dailySummaryEnabled). If validation passes, it calls updateAccountSettings. If validation fails, error messages are displayed and no interface call is made.

After updateAccountSettings returns successfully, the page shows a non-persistent or URL-driven success message using the message returned from the interface and updates all bound form controls to the returned AccountSettings values.

If updateAccountSettings returns an error according to the implementation of the interface, the settings-feedback-banner displays an error state and the form remains editable so the user can correct issues and retry.
Clicking on a section tab (e.g., profile vs preferences) updates the URL parameter section and switches which subset of fields is visible, without changing the underlying AccountSettings data.
Submitting the header search form while on settings.html reads the search input value and navigates the browser to search_results.html?query=currentInputValue, leaving the settings page.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary page state is controlled through URL query parameters and the data returned from getAccountSettings. When settings.html loads, the page optionally reads a section parameter (e.g., section=profile or section=preferences). If section is not present or does not match a recognized value, a default section is selected. The visible form panels (profile fields vs localization and notification preferences) are determined by this section value. Additionally, a status parameter (e.g., status=updated or status=error) can be used to display feedback after redirects or reloads; when present, the settings-feedback-banner interprets it to show a corresponding message until the user dismisses it or navigates away. The search workflow uses a query parameter when navigating away from settings: submitting the header search navigates to search_results.html with a query parameter containing the entered search term. No user-specific state is persisted in localStorage or similar mechanisms; all persisted state comes from AccountSettings via getAccountSettings and updateAccountSettings, and transient UI state is held only in memory. The URL parameters are the sole mechanism for restoring which section was active and whether a feedback status should be visible after a page reload.

components.functionality: On initial page load, fetches the current user's AccountSettings using getAccountSettings and exposes the returned settings to child components as the canonical source of truth for displayName, bio, timezone, and dailySummaryEnabled.

Controls which subset of settings fields is visible by reading and updating the section URL parameter. Clicking a tab updates the section parameter and changes the active panel between profile-related fields and localization/notification-related fields.

Provides inputs for editing AccountSettings.displayName and AccountSettings.bio. Maintains local form state, tracks dirtiness, and validates the bio to ensure it stays between 20 and 30 words before allowing submission.

Displays a dropdown selector bound to AccountSettings.timezone using the enum values defined in the data model. Updates local form state when the user selects a timezone, and surfaces the selected value for use when calling updateAccountSettings.
Renders a toggle or checkbox bound to AccountSettings.dailySummaryEnabled to control whether daily summary or daily email digest notifications are enabled. Updates local form state when toggled.

Aggregates current form values from profile-settings-form, timezone-preferences-control, and notification-preferences-control. Performs final validation (including bio word count constraints) and, if valid, calls updateAccountSettings with displayName, bio, timezone, and dailySummaryEnabled. Updates the in-memory AccountSettings data with the returned settings on success.

Displays status messages after a settings update. On successful updateAccountSettings calls, it shows the message returned by the interface. It can also interpret a status URL parameter to show feedback after a page reload and can be dismissed by the user.

Provides a text input in the page header for entering a search term. On submit, reads the current input value and navigates to search_results.html with the query parameter set to the entered term, as defined by the outgoing navigation rules.

layout.grid_system: Implicit centered column layout likely based on a 12‑column grid with a wide central content span (approx. 8–10 columns) and large left/right gutters.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a strict top‑to‑bottom hierarchy: a full‑width black navigation bar at the very top (not described as a component here), followed immediately by a tall, off‑white header band that introduces the settings context and houses the header search on the far right. Below this header band, a single large settings card is centered in the viewport, acting as the main container for all settings‑related components. Inside that card, elements are arranged in a vertical sequence: any feedback banner appears at the very top, followed by a compact header row with the section tabs, then the profile settings form and its sub‑controls, and finally the save controller at the bottom. The data loader overlays this central card region when active, dimming or obscuring content beneath it.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a strict top‑to‑bottom hierarchy: a full‑width black navigation bar at the very top (not described as a component here), followed immediately by a tall, off‑white header band that introduces the settings context and houses the header search on the far right. Below this header band, a single large settings card is centered in the viewport, acting as the main container for all settings‑related components. Inside that card, elements are arranged in a vertical sequence: any feedback banner appears at the very top, followed by a compact header row with the section tabs, then the profile settings form and its sub‑controls, and finally the save controller at the bottom. The data loader overlays this central card region when active, dimming or obscuring content beneath it.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The black navigation bar occupies approximately the top 8–10% of the viewport height. Beneath it, the off‑white header band fills around 20–30% of the viewport height, keeping most of the area relatively empty aside from a heading block on the left and the small search control aligned to the right. The main settings card sits below this band and occupies roughly 50–60% of the viewport height on initial load, with generous top and bottom margins. Horizontally, the central content column (where the card lives) spans about 60–70% of the page width, leaving equal gutters of 15–20% on the left and right. Within the card, padding of 24–32px is applied on all sides, and vertical spacing between internal sections (tabs, form blocks, save actions) is in the 16–24px range, with 24–32px between major subsections like form blocks.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is intentionally low. Only a small number of controls are visible at a time: the header area feels sparse, with lots of empty off‑white space around the heading and search icon. The central card exposes only the most essential settings elements, each with ample white space around them. Form fields are stacked with generous vertical gaps, labels are not cramped, and buttons are not crowded by other controls. The overall layout ensures that a user typically sees one major logical grouping of settings (like profile info and two preference controls) within the initial fold, rather than a dense list of fields.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s attention moves from the high‑contrast black navigation bar across the top, then down into the pale off‑white header band. A left‑aligned page title area (implied) anchors the top of the content column, while the header search form at the far right provides a secondary point of focus. From there, the eye is drawn downward toward the centered, high‑contrast settings card. Within the card, the feedback banner (if present) sits at the top and is seen first, followed by the horizontal row of section tabs that invite switching between settings areas. The eye then flows down through the profile settings form and its individual controls (profile settings, timezone, notification preferences) and finally lands on the save controller at the bottom right of the card. When the loader is active, it visually dominates the central column, sitting above all other content and clearly indicating a transient loading state.

layout.overall_description: The User Settings page adopts a calm, editorial layout dominated by a single vertical spine of content down the center of the screen. A solid black navigation bar spans edge‑to‑edge at the top, consuming a modest slice of vertical space and establishing a strong, stable anchor. Below it, a tall off‑white band fills most of the upper viewport, with a subtle page heading area on the left and a small, icon‑driven search control aligned flush to the right; the rest of this band remains mostly empty, which accentuates the minimalism and slows the visual pace. Dropping further down, a large, off‑white or very light gray settings card is centered within the page, bounded by wide left and right gutters so that the main content column feels narrow and intentional. Inside the card, content is stacked in a generous vertical rhythm: an optional, full‑width feedback banner sits at the top edge, followed by a slim row of section tabs that hint at other settings areas. The profile form and preference controls appear below, each occupying the full width of the card with comfortable spacing between them. The save controls sit at the bottom right of this card, separated by additional whitespace to signal their importance. If loading is in progress, a semi‑opaque overlay and loader icon appear centered within the same column, temporarily overriding all other visual cues. The overall impression is a quiet, reading‑first configuration space with a clear central column, minimal ornamentation, and deliberate breathing room around each interactive element.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The account settings loader appears only when data for the settings page is being fetched or saved. It is positioned centrally within the main content column, visually aligned with the settings card. When active, it behaves as a semi‑transparent overlay that covers the full area of the settings card: from the card’s top edge (just below the off‑white header band) to its bottom edge, and spanning the same 60–70% width as the card itself. In the center of this overlay, a minimalist loader indicator (such as a thin monochrome spinner or simple text label) is placed both vertically and horizontally centered. The page’s black navigation bar and off‑white header band remain visible above, but the contents of the settings card (tabs, form fields, save button) are dimmed or visually subdued beneath the overlay, reinforcing that interaction is temporarily suspended.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves like a modal overlay anchored to the central content column. It is positioned absolutely or fixed relative to the main settings card container so that it scrolls with the card if the page is scrolled mid‑load, always covering the card’s interior area until loading completes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The settings section tabs form a slim, horizontal navigation row inside the main settings card, just below any feedback banner and near the top of the card. They extend nearly the full inner width of the card (around 90–100% of the card’s inner width), leaving a small margin on the left and right that matches the card’s internal padding (24–32px). The tabs are left‑aligned as a textual row with a quiet, editorial treatment—simple labels spaced out with 24px or so between them, no heavy pill backgrounds, and a subtle underline or weight change to indicate the active tab. Vertically, the tabs sit about 16–24px below the top edge of the card when no feedback banner is present, or 16–24px below the bottom edge of the feedback banner when one is shown. There is another 16–24px of whitespace below the tabs before the profile settings form begins, so the tabs feel like a distinct navigational strip rather than pressed against the form fields.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the card; they scroll with the page content. Positioned relative to the top of the settings card, always appearing above form content and below any feedback banner.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The profile settings form occupies the majority of the settings card’s vertical space and forms the primary content block within it. It is placed directly beneath the settings section tabs, separated from them by approximately 16–24px of vertical whitespace. The form spans the full inner width of the card (minus the consistent 24–32px side padding), arranging its fields in a single column to maintain the reading‑first, minimal aesthetic. Higher‑level profile fields (such as username, display name, bio) appear at the top of this form block, with each field separated vertically by 16–24px. Below these primary fields, additional preference controls like timezone and notification settings are incorporated as subsections (represented by their own components) and stacked with slightly larger gaps of 24–32px between them to visually group them. The overall form block is centered within the card both horizontally and visually, with generous whitespace above (below the tabs) and below (above the save controller), so that even when the form is long, it reads as a clear, linear column of inputs rather than a dense grid.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block content that expands vertically as needed. It scrolls with the page and defines the main height of the settings card; additional internal scrolling containers are avoided to keep interaction straightforward.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The timezone preferences control is a dedicated form field section within the profile settings form, positioned after the core profile fields and before the notification preferences. It occupies the full width of the form’s content column (within the card’s padding), aligning its label and input left with the rest of the form fields. Vertically, it is separated from the last profile field above by a slightly larger gap of about 24px, signaling that it is a distinct sub‑group. The section might consist of a single dropdown or select input, with its label placed directly above the control with 8–12px of spacing, preserving the minimalist vertical rhythm. Beneath the control, a short helper text line (if used) would sit with 4–8px spacing and low visual weight. There is another 24–32px of whitespace below the timezone section before the notification preferences, so each preference block feels like its own quiet island within the form.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline section within the form, stacked as part of the single vertical flow. It doesn’t have its own scrolling, and its vertical position adjusts naturally as form content above or below changes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The notification preferences control sits below the timezone preferences within the profile settings form, serving as another clearly separated subsection. It is aligned to the left with the rest of the form content and spans the same full inner width of the card’s content column. A 24–32px vertical gap separates it from the timezone section above, making it visually distinct. Within this block, individual notification options (for example, email, push, in‑app) are arranged as a minimal vertical stack of toggles or checkboxes, each on its own line with 12–16px of vertical spacing. Labels are simple, single‑line text aligned left; any supporting descriptions are in smaller, lighter text immediately beneath each option. The entire notification block ends with a comfortable 24–32px of blank space before the save controller row begins, preventing the actions from feeling jammed against the last preference option.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static subsequence within the single‑column form flow. It expands vertically as more notification options are added and scrolls together with the rest of the card content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The settings save controller is positioned at the bottom of the settings card, directly following the notification preferences block. A generous vertical gap of about 24–32px separates it from the last form element to emphasize that it is a distinct action area. Horizontally, the primary Save action is right‑aligned within the inner width of the card, sitting in the bottom‑right corner of the content area. If there are secondary actions (like Cancel or Reset), they appear to the left of the primary button, spaced by approximately 16–24px. The entire action row shares the card’s side padding (24–32px from the right edge and left edge) and sits 24–32px above the bottom edge of the card, mirroring the top padding and enclosing the main content in a balanced way. This positioning ensures that when a user scrolls to the bottom of the central content column, the last thing they see within the card is a clean, uncluttered band of whitespace leading into the Save control, giving it prominence without needing heavy styling.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and anchored to the bottom of the settings card’s content area. It scrolls with the rest of the page; there is no sticky behavior, preserving the calm, non‑dynamic editorial feel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The settings feedback banner appears as a slim, full‑width strip associated directly with the settings card. When present, it is positioned at the very top of the card’s content area, just inside the card’s top padding. It stretches from the left inner edge of the card to the right inner edge, maintaining the same 24–32px side padding as other card content. Vertically, the banner is relatively shallow—its height is determined by one or two lines of text, plus 8–12px of padding above and below the text. It pushes the settings section tabs downward, with approximately 16–24px of space between the bottom of the banner and the top of the tabs. Visually, it stands out via a slightly darker or lighter neutral tint compared to the card background (still monochrome and flat), but it avoids bright colors or heavy borders to maintain the minimalist aesthetic. It briefly becomes the first thing the eye encounters inside the card after the off‑white header band, then yields attention to the tabs and form content below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline element at the top of the card’s content stack. It appears and disappears by taking up vertical space—no overlay behavior—pushing subsequent content down when shown.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header search form is placed within the large off‑white band that sits directly below the black navigation bar, not inside the settings card itself. It is aligned to the far right of the central content column, roughly in the top‑right quadrant of this off‑white band. Horizontally, its interactive area sits flush with the right edge of the central column (about 60–70% page width), leaving a large neutral gutter beyond. Vertically, it is positioned about 24–32px below the bottom edge of the black nav bar, with ample empty space both above and below. The visual treatment is extremely minimal: likely an icon‑only magnifying glass or a compact text input with a subtle underline. The visible icon or control is small, but the clickable hit area is approximately 24–32px square around it, invisible but preserving usability. On the left side of the same band (not defined as a component here), a simple page title like “Settings” or “Account” would appear, leaving the rest of the horizontal space mostly empty. This arrangement draws the eye from the top nav down into the open space, then over to the small search affordance before guiding attention further down to the main card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element within the off‑white header band. It stays fixed in relation to the page structure (below the nav bar and above the main card) and scrolls out of view as the user scrolls down the page.

name: About
title: About
description: Static informational page that loads and displays the community’s About content via getStaticPageContent, with navigation to Help and integrated header search.

core_features: Load the About page title and body dynamically using getStaticPageContent based on the current page slug.
Display structured informational content about the community, its purpose, goals, and maintainers using the returned title and body.
Provide contextual in-page navigation (e.g., to specific sections of the About content) driven by URL parameters such as ?section=<sectionId>.
Offer a header search form that redirects to search_results.html with the query parameter ?query=<searchTerm>.
Enable navigation to help.html when a user clicks a 'Get Help' or 'Support' link rendered within the About content.
user_workflows:
View About information:
1. User navigates to about.html (via footer link or direct URL).
2. On page initialization, the page controller derives the pageSlug from the current path (about.html → 'about') or from an optional URL parameter (e.g., ?pageSlug=about).
3. The controller invokes getStaticPageContent(pageSlug).
4. While loading, the page may show a loading state (without hardcoded informational text about the community).
5. When the response arrives, the title and body are passed to the content view component.
6. The content view renders the title and body and updates the document title with the returned title value.

Navigate to a specific About section via URL:
1. User opens about.html?section=<sectionId>.
2. The page controller reads the section URL parameter and stores it in its in-memory state.
3. The controller calls getStaticPageContent as usual.
4. Once the content body is rendered, the content view searches within the rendered content for an element matching the requested sectionId (for example, an element with id=<sectionId> or a matching data attribute).
5. If found, the content view scrolls or focuses that element so the user lands on the relevant section.

Open Help from About content:
1. User scrolls through the About body content.
2. The dynamically rendered body includes one or more links intended for help/support (e.g., anchor tags recognized by a role, data attribute, or href pattern).
3. User clicks a help/support link.
4. The click handler intercepts or handles the event and navigates the browser to help.html, optionally appending context parameters (e.g., help.html?from=about&section=<currentSectionId>).

Search from the About page:
1. User types a term into the global header search input while on about.html.
2. User submits the form (presses Enter or clicks a search button).
3. The search form’s submit handler prevents default full-page form submission if necessary.
4. The handler reads the current input value as searchTerm.
5. If searchTerm is non-empty, the handler redirects the browser to search_results.html?query=<encodedSearchTerm>.
6. The search_results.html page (outside the scope of this spec) handles displaying results based on the query parameter.
interactions:
Page load:
- Trigger: Initial load or refresh of about.html.
- Behavior: Read URL parameters (pageSlug, section), call getStaticPageContent(pageSlug), manage loading and error states, then render content.

Click footer 'About' link:
- Trigger: User clicks the global footer 'About' navigation link.
- Behavior: Browser navigates to about.html (optionally with a ref parameter like ?ref=footer). On load, the page controller fetches content using getStaticPageContent and displays it.

Click 'Get Help' / 'Support' link inside content:
- Trigger: User clicks any link within the rendered body that matches the configured selector (e.g., data-role="help-link" or href pointing to help.html).
- Behavior: Navigate to help.html, optionally adding context such as ?from=about&section=<currentSectionId> so Help can adapt its content.

Use URL parameter to jump to a section:
- Trigger: Page loads with about.html?section=<sectionId>, or the section parameter in the URL changes (e.g., via internal navigation links that update the query string).
- Behavior: After content render, the content view scrolls/focuses to the matching section element if it exists.

Submit header search:
- Trigger: User submits the header search form on the About page.
- Behavior: Read the current search input value as searchTerm. If searchTerm is not empty, redirect to search_results.html?query=<encodedSearchTerm>. If empty, either keep the user on the current page or show simple inline validation (e.g., focusing the field) without submitting.
page_functionality.state_logic:
URL parameters drive page behavior and are not persisted outside the URL.

Recognized URL parameters for about.html:
- pageSlug (optional):
  - Type: string.
  - Purpose: Determines which static content slug to request via getStaticPageContent.
  - Default behavior: If absent, the pageSlug is derived from the current page's file name (about.html → 'about').
  - Effect: When pageSlug changes (e.g., about.html?pageSlug=about-v2), the controller re-invokes getStaticPageContent with the new slug and re-renders the page.

- section (optional):
  - Type: string.
  - Purpose: Indicates which section of the dynamic About content should be brought into view.
  - Effect on load: After getStaticPageContent completes and the body is rendered, the content view uses the section value to locate a matching element in the rendered content (e.g., by id or data attribute). If found, the view scrolls/focuses to it.
  - Effect on in-page navigation: If internal links within the body update the URL with a new section parameter, the content view reacts to that change and moves focus/scroll position accordingly.

- ref (optional):
  - Type: string.
  - Purpose: Indicates where the user navigated from (e.g., footer, header, email). This may be used only for analytics or conditional UI messaging but does not change the getStaticPageContent call.

Network and interface state:
- The only data interface used is getStaticPageContent(pageSlug).
- The controller maintains an in-memory state machine:
  - loading: true while awaiting response from getStaticPageContent.
  - loaded: true once data is received and rendered.
  - error: set if getStaticPageContent returns an error; the UI shows a generic error state without hardcoding About-specific content.
- No state is stored in localStorage or other persistent browser storage; all state is derived from the current URL and the latest response from getStaticPageContent.

components.functionality: Coordinates fetching and rendering of the About page content using getStaticPageContent, interprets URL parameters (pageSlug, section, ref), and manages loading/error states for child components.
event_handlers: onPageInit: On initial page load, parse the current URL for pageSlug, section, and ref parameters. Derive a default pageSlug from the file name (about.html → 'about') if no pageSlug parameter is present. Invoke getStaticPageContent(pageSlug) and set an internal loading state.

onContentFetchSuccess: Triggered when getStaticPageContent resolves successfully. Receives { title, body }. Updates the document title to the returned title. Passes the title and body to the about-content-view component. Sets loading to false and clears any previous error state.

onContentFetchError: Triggered if getStaticPageContent fails. Sets an error state that the about-content-view can use to render an error message or fallback UI (without injecting hardcoded About copy). Keeps previous content cleared or hidden as appropriate.

onUrlChange: Optional handler if the application supports client-side routing and URL changes without full reload. When the URL query string changes (e.g., new section or pageSlug), re-derives pageSlug and section. If pageSlug changed, re-invoke getStaticPageContent. If only section changed, notify about-content-view to adjust scroll/focus.

components.functionality: Renders the dynamic About content (title and body) returned from getStaticPageContent, applies section-based navigation behavior, and wires up internal links such as 'Get Help' or 'Support' to navigate to help.html.
event_handlers: onRender: Called when the controller provides new title and body data. Renders the title into the main page heading and the body into a content container. After rendering, checks if the controller has a pending section parameter and, if so, calls handleSectionFocus(sectionId).

handleSectionFocus(sectionId): Searches within the rendered content container for an element that matches the sectionId (for example, an element with id equal to sectionId or a known data attribute). If found, scrolls or focuses that element to bring it into view.

onInternalLinkClick: Delegated click handler attached to the content container. When a link inside the body is clicked:
  - If the link targets help (identified by href pointing to help.html or a data-role such as data-role="help-link"), prevent default if necessary and navigate to help.html, optionally adding context parameters (e.g., help.html?from=about&section=<currentSectionId>).
  - If the link updates the section parameter in the URL (e.g., ?section=team), update the browser URL accordingly and trigger handleSectionFocus with the new sectionId.

onStateChange: Responds to changes in loading or error states from the controller. When loading is true, shows a generic loading indicator. When error is present, shows an error state without embedding static About text. When loaded successfully, shows the dynamic content.

components.functionality: Provides a search input in the global header that, when used on the About page, redirects users to search_results.html with the appropriate query URL parameter.
event_handlers: onInputChange: Updates the internal value of searchTerm as the user types into the header search input field.

onSubmit: Triggered when the user submits the search form (via Enter key or search button). If searchTerm is non-empty, URL-encodes the term and redirects the browser to search_results.html?query=<encodedSearchTerm>. If empty, prevents navigation and can optionally focus the input field to prompt entry.
onPageContextLoad: Optionally initializes the search input with an empty value when the About page loads, ensuring no stale query persists across page navigations unless explicitly preserved via URL.

layout.grid_system: Implicit centered column layout likely based on a 12‑column grid with a wide central content span (approx. 8–10 columns) and large left/right gutters.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the mockup’s linear, top‑to‑bottom structure: a full‑width black navigation bar at the top, followed by a tall, off‑white header band that feels mostly empty except for the page title on the left and the global search control aligned to the right, and below that a single, large, centered card containing the About content. All components sit within a centered main column, with wide left and right gutters to preserve the editorial, reading‑focused feel.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the mockup’s linear, top‑to‑bottom structure: a full‑width black navigation bar at the top, followed by a tall, off‑white header band that feels mostly empty except for the page title on the left and the global search control aligned to the right, and below that a single, large, centered card containing the About content. All components sit within a centered main column, with wide left and right gutters to preserve the editorial, reading‑focused feel.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The black navigation bar occupies about 8–10% of the viewport height (roughly 64–80px on desktop). Below it, an off‑white header band takes up about 20–30% of the viewport height, with most of that space left intentionally empty to create visual calm; the title and search live near the upper portion of this band, not filling it. The main About content card sits centered below this header and initially occupies about 50–60% of the viewport height, expanding vertically as needed for longer content. Horizontally, the central content column spans roughly 60–70% of the viewport width, leaving 15–20% gutters on both sides. Internal padding inside the card and header band follows 24–32px values, and vertical gaps between major sections follow 48–64px.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density remains deliberately low: only a handful of navigation links in the black bar, a single line of header information plus the search icon in the off‑white band, and one primary About content card visible within the initial fold. Paragraphs and headings in the About content are generously spaced with 16–24px vertical gaps, and there are no dense clusters of controls. Icon hit areas (for search and any overflow menu) are at least 24–32px, but visually minimal. The overall effect is a quiet, open layout that emphasizes reading comfort over information density.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye first lands on the high‑contrast black navigation bar and scans its left‑to‑right items (including the About and Help links). From there, the eye drops into the large, low‑contrast off‑white band and is drawn to the left‑aligned About page heading. The global search, anchored on the right side of this same band, provides a secondary focal point that briefly pulls attention laterally. Finally, the eye settles naturally into the large, centered About content card just below, moving left‑to‑right across any header meta and then down through the body text. The visual flow ends on any subtle action affordance in the card header (e.g., an overflow icon) but remains primarily reading‑oriented.

layout.overall_description: The About page appears as a calm, monochrome, editorial‑style layout. At the very top, a full‑width black navigation bar stretches edge‑to‑edge across the viewport, about 8–10% of the screen height, with a small logo or title on the far left and simple text links such as About (highlighted) and Help arranged horizontally. Beneath this, the page opens into a tall off‑white field that takes up roughly the upper third of the remaining viewport. In this band, the main content column is centered; on its left, near the top of the band, a quiet About heading sits with ample breathing room above and below, while on the far right edge of that same column the global header search control appears as a small, minimal icon‑based touch target. Most of this band remains intentionally empty, gently guiding the eye downward. Below a generous vertical gap, the centered About content card begins: a white or very light card that spans about 60–70% of the viewport width, framed by wide neutral gutters on both sides. Inside, a compact header row may present a small circular community avatar and About metadata on the left, balanced by a subtle overflow icon on the right, followed by well‑spaced paragraphs of About text in a single column. The overall composition feels airy and quiet, with large vertical separations between the nav, header band, and About card, encouraging focused reading rather than interaction overload.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-page-controller acts as the structural wrapper for the entire viewport. It spans 100% of the page width and at least 100% of the viewport height, with a neutral off‑white background behind the main content. At the very top of this controller’s stack, it reserves a full‑width horizontal band for the global navigation bar: a black strip approximately 64–80px tall (about 8–10% of viewport height on desktop) that runs edge‑to‑edge. Within this bar, content is horizontally centered inside an invisible main column: on the left portion, a compact site logo or wordmark and text links such as About and Help are arranged left‑to‑right; the About link is visually indicated as the current page with a subtle monochrome treatment (e.g., underlined or slightly bolder), while Help sits adjacent as a secondary option. Directly below this navigation bar, the controller defines a tall off‑white header band that extends across the full width but still uses the centered main column for its contents. This band occupies roughly 20–30% of the viewport height, with a large amount of empty vertical space. Near the top of this band, aligned to the left edge of the main column, the page‑level heading for the About page is placed with approximately 32px of padding from the top of the band and 24–32px of left padding relative to the column. At the same vertical level, but aligned to the right edge of the same main column, the controller positions the global-header-search-form. Below this header band, the about-page-controller centers the main content column: it constrains the usable content width to about 60–70% of the viewport, leaving symmetrical 15–20% gutters on both sides that always remain empty. Within this column, it places the about-content-view as a full-width card inside the column, separated from the header band by a generous 48–64px vertical gap. The entire stack (nav bar, header band with title and search, then the About card) scrolls together in a standard document flow, preserving the simple top‑down reading order.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a standard page‑level container that controls vertical stacking and horizontal centering. It is not fixed or floating; instead, it scrolls naturally with the document. The navigation bar and header band remain at the top of the page and scroll off‑screen as the user moves down through long About content. The main column is responsive: on large screens it maintains the 60–70% width with wide gutters, while on smaller screens it relaxes toward near‑full width (e.g., 100% minus 16–24px side padding) but retains generous vertical spacing between sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-content-view is rendered as the primary, centered card within the main column defined by the controller. Horizontally, it spans the full width of that column, which is roughly 60–70% of the viewport width on desktop, leaving 15–20% neutral gutters on both sides outside the card. The card itself sits on a subtly distinct background (white or slightly lighter than the page’s off‑white) and may have a very fine 1px gray border to echo the flat, editorial card style from the forum posts. The top edge of this card begins approximately 48–64px below the bottom of the off‑white header band, creating a strong vertical separation and making the card feel like a distinct, prominent object. Inside the card, there is consistent padding of 24–32px on all sides. At the top of the card, a header row about 56–72px tall mirrors the forum post header structure in a simplified way: on the left, a small circular community avatar or monochrome icon (24–32px diameter) followed by the About label and any short metadata (e.g., community name or last updated) arranged in a horizontal line or compact stack; on the right, aligned to the card’s inner right edge, a small vertical ellipsis icon or similar overflow control appears, with an invisible tap area of around 24–32px. This header row occupies the full card width and leaves about 16–24px of vertical space below it before the body content starts. The main About text flows beneath as a single full‑width text column, with paragraphs, subheadings, and optionally lists stacked vertically. Line length is kept comfortable by the main column width, and each paragraph is separated by 16–24px of vertical spacing to maintain low density. The card’s height varies with content but, on first load, is intended to occupy roughly 50–60% of the viewport height, making it the dominant element within the fold. As the user scrolls, the card extends downward, and any overflow is handled by standard page scrolling, with no internal scrollbars.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The card participates in the normal vertical document flow within the centered column; it is not fixed or sticky. On larger screens, it maintains its constrained central width to protect readable line lengths. On tablets and mobile, it adapts to nearly full width with side padding of about 16–24px, while preserving the same internal padding and vertical spacing. The header row remains locked at the top of the card and does not become sticky during scrolling; user focus is on reading, not on controls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The global-header-search-form is positioned within the off‑white header band directly beneath the black navigation bar. It is anchored to the far right edge of the main content column, sharing a horizontal baseline with the About page heading on the left. Vertically, it sits approximately 32px below the bottom edge of the black nav bar, so both the heading (left) and the search (right) appear near the top of the off‑white band, with a large expanse of empty space below them. In its default, collapsed state, the search form is represented as a minimalist magnifying glass icon with no surrounding border or visible field, but with a generous invisible touch target approximately 24–32px square to remain usable. This icon is aligned so that its center sits flush with the right inner edge of the main column, leaving a small 16–24px gap to the actual viewport edge beyond the column gutter. When activated, the search input may expand horizontally leftwards from this icon, occupying up to 40–50% of the main column width, but still contained entirely within the off‑white header band, keeping the underlying About content card visually undisturbed. The input, when visible, follows the flat, monochrome style: a thin 1px border, no heavy shadows, and 8–12px internal padding; it sits on the same baseline as the heading, maintaining a clean, single‑row alignment across the header band.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The search form is part of the header band and scrolls away with it; it is not sticky or floating. In its idle state, it occupies minimal space (icon only) and does not disrupt the openness of the header. On interaction, any expanded search field overlays space within the header band without shifting the About card below, then collapses back to icon form when not in use. On smaller screens, the icon remains right‑aligned within the reduced column width, and the expanded input can grow to nearly the full width of that column, but always stays within the header band at the top of the page.

name: Help & Support
title: Help & Support
description: Help & Support provides static guidance articles and FAQs about using the discussion forum, including creating and managing discussions, replying, following topics, bookmarking threads, reporting content, and using search and filters. Content is loaded via getStaticPageContent and includes contextual navigation links to other pages.

core_features: Load and display static Help & Support content (title and body) via getStaticPageContent using a page slug derived from the URL.

Provide a structured view of help topics (e.g., sections for creating discussions, replying, following topics, bookmarking, reporting spam, search and filters) based on headings and anchors contained in the static page body.
Enable users to quickly find relevant help topics using an on-page keyword filter that searches within the loaded static help content.
Allow navigation to specific help sections using URL parameters, so a particular topic can be directly linked (deep-linking via ?section=...).
Expose contextual links in the help content that navigate users to browse discussion categories (categories.html) when they want to explore or start discussions.
Provide a header search form that forwards user queries to the main forum search results page (search_results.html) with the appropriate query URL parameter.
Highlight and aggregate support or feedback links (such as contact forms or feedback discussions) present in the static help content so users can seek additional assistance.
user_workflows:
Read general help content:
1) User navigates to help.html (via header, footer, or direct URL).
2) On initial load, the page slug for Help & Support is determined (for example, from the path or a default value).
3) The help-static-content-provider component calls getStaticPageContent with the resolved pageSlug.
4) When the response arrives, the help-content-viewer renders the returned title and body, including headings, paragraphs, and links.
5) The help-section-index component extracts section anchors/headings from the body and shows them as a clickable list for quick navigation within the page.

Open a specific help topic via URL:
1) Another page links to help.html with a section parameter (e.g., help.html?section=create-discussions).
2) On load, help-url-state-manager reads the section parameter.
3) help-static-content-provider fetches the help content via getStaticPageContent.
4) After content is available, help-content-viewer uses the section parameter to scroll to or highlight the corresponding anchor in the body.
5) help-section-index marks that section as active in the local navigation.

Search within Help content:
1) User opens help.html, optionally with a q parameter (e.g., help.html?q=bookmark).
2) help-url-state-manager reads the q parameter and sets the initial help filter term.
3) help-static-content-provider fetches the static content via getStaticPageContent.
4) help-keyword-filter-form initializes its input value from the q parameter.
5) As the user types or submits the filter form, the filter term updates the URL (q parameter) and triggers help-content-viewer to highlight or narrow visible sections whose text matches the term.
6) The user clicks a matching section in help-section-index or directly scrolls within help-content-viewer to read details.

Navigate from Help to browse categories:
1) User reads a section of the help content explaining how to browse or choose discussion categories.
2) The static body returned by getStaticPageContent contains a contextual link whose href points to categories.html.
3) When the user clicks that link within help-content-viewer, the browser navigates to categories.html, fulfilling the outgoing connection requirement.
4) The user then explores categories and applies what they learned from the help content.

Use global forum search from the Help page:
1) While on help.html, the user enters a search term in the header search form.
2) On submit, the help-header-search-form prevents any on-page filtering behavior and instead constructs a navigation URL search_results.html?query=<encoded searchTerm>.
3) The browser navigates to search_results.html with the query parameter set to the user’s input.
4) The search results page then handles the query as its own responsibility.

Locate support or feedback channels:
1) User navigates to help.html seeking direct support.
2) help-static-content-provider loads the body via getStaticPageContent.
3) help-support-links scans or receives structured segments of the body that identify support or feedback links (for example, an email link, a dedicated feedback discussion link, or a contact form URL) and renders them in a dedicated panel.
4) The user clicks one of these links to open the appropriate support channel (which may be another page, mail link, or external resource).
interactions: Page load and content fetch: When help.html loads, help-static-content-provider triggers an onInit event that calls getStaticPageContent with a pageSlug derived from the current route or a default Help & Support slug. On success, components bound to StaticPageContent (help-content-viewer, help-section-index, help-support-links) update automatically.

Error handling and retry: If getStaticPageContent fails, help-static-content-provider exposes an error state. The page shows a retry action; when the user clicks retry, an onRetry handler re-calls getStaticPageContent with the same pageSlug.

In-page section navigation: When a user clicks a section entry in help-section-index, an onClickItem handler updates the URL section parameter and scrolls help-content-viewer to the corresponding anchor in the body.

Help keyword filtering: When the user types in the help-keyword-filter-form input, an onChange handler updates a local filter term and synchronizes it with the q URL parameter. help-content-viewer then filters or highlights matching parts of the StaticPageContent body in real time.

Help filter submission: When the user submits the help-keyword-filter-form, an onSubmit handler ensures the q URL parameter reflects the current term (for deep-linking) and forces a re-evaluation of matches in help-content-viewer. No external API call is made; filtering is performed client-side on the already-loaded StaticPageContent.

Clear filter: When the user clicks a clear control in help-keyword-filter-form, an onClear handler removes the q parameter from the URL and resets help-content-viewer to show all help sections with no filtering.

Contextual navigation to categories: When a user clicks a contextual link within the rendered help body whose href targets categories.html, help-content-viewer’s onClickLink handler allows the default navigation so the browser goes to categories.html.

Header search to search_results: When the user submits the header search input, help-header-search-form’s onSubmit handler reads the input value, assigns it to a local searchTerm, and navigates the browser to search_results.html?query=<encoded searchTerm>, without calling any APIs.

Support link clicks: When a user clicks a link rendered by help-support-links, an onClickLink handler either navigates to the target URL (e.g., another internal support page, a feedback thread, or an external form) or opens the default mail client for mailto links.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The Help & Support page relies on URL parameters to manage view state without using localStorage. The base route is help.html. A pageSlug for getStaticPageContent is resolved from the route (for example, a fixed slug dedicated to Help & Support or a value derived from a path or parameter), but this slug resolution is internal and does not require additional API calls.

The primary URL parameters used for state are:
- section: help.html?section=<sectionId>
  • On initial load, help-url-state-manager reads the section parameter (if present) and stores it in in-memory state.
  • After getStaticPageContent completes, help-content-viewer maps sectionId to a specific anchor or heading inside the body and scrolls/highlights it.
  • When the user clicks different items in help-section-index, the section parameter is updated via the History API or full navigation, ensuring the URL always reflects the currently focused section.

- q: help.html?q=<term>
  • On initial load, help-url-state-manager reads the q parameter (if present) and sets the initial help keyword filter term.
  • help-keyword-filter-form initializes its input with this term and immediately triggers filtering in help-content-viewer so that matching sections are highlighted or others are visually de-emphasized.
  • When the user updates or clears the filter, the q parameter is updated or removed accordingly, enabling deep-linking to a specific filtered view of the help content.

These parameters never affect which API is called: getStaticPageContent is always invoked with the same Help & Support pageSlug. All behavior changes (section focus, filtering) are client-side transformations of the single StaticPageContent response. Navigation to other pages (categories.html, search_results.html) uses URL construction only and does not involve additional data interfaces on this page.

components.functionality: Fetches static Help & Support page content via getStaticPageContent when help.html is loaded and exposes loading, success, and error states to child components.

Renders the title and body returned by getStaticPageContent and supports anchored section navigation, in-page keyword highlighting/filtering, and contextual link handling (including navigation to categories.html).

Builds a table-of-contents style list of help topics by extracting section headings or anchors from the StaticPageContent body and lets users click to jump to specific sections, updating the section URL parameter.

Provides an input field for users to search within help content. Updates the q URL parameter and notifies help-content-viewer to apply keyword-based highlighting or filtering to the loaded StaticPageContent.
Global forum search available in the header. Reads the user-entered searchTerm and navigates to search_results.html with a query parameter, without calling any APIs from this page.
Displays key support or feedback links present in the StaticPageContent body (such as contact or feedback channels) in a dedicated panel to make them easier to find and click.
Coordinates reading and writing of URL parameters used by the Help & Support page (section and q), ensuring components reflect the current URL state and that user actions update the URL consistently.

layout.grid_system: Implicit centered column layout likely based on a 12‑column grid with a wide central content span (approx. 8–10 columns) and large left/right gutters.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps a strict top‑to‑bottom hierarchy: global black navigation bar at the very top (outside these components), followed by a tall, off‑white header band specific to the Help & Support page. Inside that band, the Help & Support heading and subtle descriptive text sit toward the left of the central content column, while the help-header-search-form is right‑aligned as the main control in this zone. Below this band, a single, large, centered help card spans the main content column. Within this card, a shallow header row is split into two columns: the help-section-index on the left and the help-keyword-filter-form aligned to the right. Beneath that header row, the help-content-viewer occupies the full card width as the primary reading area, and the help-support-links sit at the bottom of the card as a subtle footer section. The non-visual help-static-content-provider and help-url-state-manager wrap this central column logically but do not appear in the visual layout.

Inside that band, the Help & Support heading and subtle descriptive text sit toward the left of the central content column, while the help-header-search-form is right‑aligned as the main control in this zone. Below this band, a single, large, centered help card spans the main content column. Within this card, a shallow header row is split into two columns: the help-section-index on the left and the help-keyword-filter-form aligned to the right.
