There are no competing sidebars, no dense toolbars, and only a handful of controls per view, maintaining the calm, editorial reading experience from the mockup.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first meets the high‑contrast global navigation bar, then drops into the off‑white header band, reading the Help & Support title on the left before being pulled to the right by the help-header-search-form. From there, the large expanse of empty space naturally guides the gaze downward toward the centered help card. Inside the card, attention starts at the top left with the help-section-index, scanning its concise section links, then moves laterally to the right toward the help-keyword-filter-form. After this horizontal sweep, the eye moves down into the help-content-viewer body text, reading vertically through the article. Finally, the help-support-links at the bottom act as a low‑key terminal focus offering onward navigation. The non‑visual data and state components operate invisibly in the background without disrupting this flow.

layout.overall_description: The Help & Support page presents as a quiet, single‑column reading experience centered in the viewport. A solid black navigation bar spans the top edge of the browser, followed by a tall, off‑white band that feels mostly empty. Within this band, the Help & Support title and a short one‑line description sit left‑aligned within the central content column, while a minimal, right‑aligned search form balances the composition, echoing the mockup’s lone icon on a field of negative space. Below, a single large card floats in the center of the page, framed by wide left and right gutters. The card’s top edge contains a slim header row: on the left, a compact list of section links indexing the page; on the right, a small keyword filter input that mirrors the mockup’s right‑aligned controls. Beneath this row, the article content fills the card in a single, comfortable text column, with generous margins and line spacing that invite uninterrupted reading. At the bottom of the card, a lightly separated block of related help links appears, understated yet clearly available for users ready to move on. The entire composition is monochrome and flat, with no decorative elements, letting structure, whitespace, and typography define the visual rhythm. Invisible data and URL state components underpin the experience without adding any visual noise, preserving the minimalist, editorial character of the mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The static content provider is non‑visual and occupies no explicit space in the layout. Conceptually, it sits at the top of the Help & Support page’s main content tree, wrapping the portion of the page that includes the off‑white header band and the centered help card. It spans the full width of the central content column, aligned with the same left and right boundaries as the main card, but it does not render any visible container, borders, or spacing. Its presence should not alter the spacing between the navigation bar, header band, or card; it only ensures the help content is available before the help-content-viewer and related components render.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non‑visual background layer tied to the central content column; occupies zero layout height and width and does not affect flow or spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The help content viewer is the primary body of the large centered card. It begins directly below the card’s internal header row, which contains the section index and keyword filter. It stretches from the card’s left padding edge to its right padding edge, forming a single wide text column that aligns with the center of the page. Vertically, it consumes the majority of the card’s height, allowing the article to extend downward with generous line spacing and 24–32px padding on all sides. The top of the text sits approximately 16–24px below the header row to preserve the airy separation seen in the mockup. As the user scrolls, this content area grows naturally, pushing the card downward while maintaining wide margins to the viewport edges. The help-support-links are positioned clearly below this viewer, separated by ample white space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block element within the card that expands vertically with content; always full width within the card’s padded interior and never scrolls independently from the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section index is placed inside the top portion of the centered card as the left side of a two‑column header row. It aligns with the card’s left padding edge and occupies roughly the left one‑third to one‑half of the card’s internal width at larger viewports. The index is compact in height, usually not more than a few lines of text, and sits near the top of the card with approximately 24–32px padding above and to its left. Each section link in the index is stacked vertically with comfortable 8–16px spacing, creating a small, readable block rather than a dense list. To its right, separated by open space, the keyword filter form aligns along the same horizontal baseline. Below both elements, there is a clear vertical gap of 16–24px before the help-content-viewer begins, reinforcing the card‑as‑post structure from the mockup. On narrow screens, this index gracefully stacks above the keyword filter, still near the top of the card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, non‑sticky element anchored to the top-left of the card; participates in the card’s responsive header row, shrinking or stacking above the filter form on smaller viewports.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The keyword filter form sits inside the same card header row as the section index but is aligned to the right, echoing the mockup’s right‑aligned overflow or action icon. At larger screen sizes, it is positioned flush with the card’s right padding edge and horizontally aligned with the vertical midpoint of the section index block. The filter presents as a single, minimalist text input (possibly accompanied by a small icon) that occupies about 25–30% of the card’s internal width, leaving generous empty space between it and the section index. Above and below the filter field, there is open space within the card’s padding, preserving the low‑density aesthetic. When viewport width decreases, the filter shifts to a stacked layout, appearing directly beneath the section index but still aligned to the right edge of the card’s content area, with at least 16px of vertical separation to avoid visual crowding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, responsive element bound to the card’s top-right corner; participates in a two‑column header row at larger widths and gracefully stacks below the section index on smaller screens without becoming sticky or floating.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header search form lives in the tall off‑white band between the global navigation bar and the main help card. Within this band, it is anchored to the right side of the central content column, aligning its right edge with the right edge of the card below. Horizontally, it spans roughly 20–30% of the page width, appearing as a slim, single‑line input or compact search control that visually reads as a subtle element rather than a large search bar. Vertically, it sits in the upper half of the off‑white band, leaving generous space above and especially below it, creating the sense of a nearly empty field punctuated only by the title on the left and this form on the right. The area to its left remains mostly open, apart from the page heading, which reinforces the sparse editorial feel. As the user scrolls down, the search form moves out of view with the header band; it does not stick to the top.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element within the off‑white header band; right‑aligned inside the central content column and scrolling away with the header rather than remaining fixed or sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The support links panel appears at the bottom of the centered help card, acting as a quiet footer section for the article. It spans the full internal width of the card from left to right padding, aligning its text column with the help-content-viewer above. A clear vertical gap of approximately 24–32px separates it from the last line of article content, signaled by a simple increase in whitespace rather than heavy borders. The links themselves are presented as a short vertical list or two slender columns of text links, each spaced 8–16px apart to maintain an open, breathable feel. This block is visually lighter than the main body text, possibly using a smaller font size or lighter tone, ensuring it does not compete with the article but is easily discoverable once the reader reaches the end. Beneath the links panel, the card’s bottom padding of around 24–32px provides additional negative space before the background off‑white page color resumes.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static footer-like section within the card; always rendered immediately after the help-content-viewer and before the card’s bottom padding, expanding vertically only as needed for its links.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL state manager is completely non‑visual and occupies no explicit position in the rendered layout. Conceptually, it is associated with the central content column and binds the state of the help-header-search-form, help-keyword-filter-form, and help-content-viewer to the browser’s URL. It does not introduce any additional containers, spacing, or alignment shifts. All visible spacing and structure should remain unchanged whether this manager is present or not.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Invisible background behavior; has no box model impact and does not alter flow, positioning, or scrolling of any visible components.

name: Terms of Service
title: Terms of Service
description: Displays and manages interaction with the forum's Terms of Service, using dynamic content loaded via getStaticPageContent.

core_features: Load the Terms of Service title and body dynamically via getStaticPageContent, using a page slug derived from the terms.html route.
Render the returned terms content, including any structured sections, in a scrollable content area.
Support deep-linking to specific sections of the terms via a URL query parameter (e.g., terms.html?section={sectionId}) that controls which section is focused or scrolled into view.
Provide in-page navigation (table of contents) derived from the structure of the loaded body content, enabling users to jump to specific sections.
Expose a contextual navigation link to the Privacy Policy page (privacy.html), potentially including an optional URL parameter indicating that the user navigated from the terms page.
Handle loading and error states for the static content request, with an option for the user to retry loading if the request fails.
user_workflows: Viewing the Terms of Service: 1) User clicks the 'Terms' link in the global footer or navigates directly to terms.html. 2) On page initialization, the page derives a pageSlug value from the URL/path for terms.html. 3) The page calls getStaticPageContent with that pageSlug. 4) While the request is pending, a loading state is shown. 5) On success, the title is applied to the page header and document title, and the body is rendered in the content area. 6) If the URL includes a section query parameter, the page scrolls or focuses to the matching section within the rendered content.

Navigating within the Terms of Service: 1) After the body content is rendered, a table of contents is generated by parsing headings or structured markers from the loaded body. 2) The user clicks a table-of-contents item. 3) The page updates the URL query string to set section={targetSectionId} using history APIs without reloading the page. 4) The content area scrolls to the corresponding section anchor. 5) When the user uses the browser back/forward buttons, the page reads the section parameter and scrolls to the appropriate section again.

Retrying content load after an error: 1) If getStaticPageContent fails, the page shows an error state indicating that the terms could not be loaded. 2) The user activates a retry control. 3) The same pageSlug is used to call getStaticPageContent again. 4) On success, the title and body are rendered as in the initial workflow.

Navigating to the Privacy Policy: 1) Within the terms content or in a dedicated reference area, the user sees a link indicating that the Privacy Policy is available. 2) The user clicks the link. 3) The page navigates the browser to privacy.html, optionally appending a query parameter (e.g., privacy.html?source=terms) so that the destination can be aware of the navigation source.
interactions: Initial page load triggers an automatic call to getStaticPageContent with a pageSlug derived from the current route, without manual user input.
Clicking a table-of-contents item updates the section query parameter in the URL and scrolls the main content area to the corresponding section anchor.

Clicking in-text anchor links within the rendered body (e.g., references to specific clauses) updates the URL section parameter in the same way as the table-of-contents items, preserving deep-linkability.
Clicking a 'Retry' button after a failed content load reissues the getStaticPageContent request for the same pageSlug.
Clicking a 'Privacy Policy' reference link navigates the user to privacy.html, optionally including a source query parameter to indicate that the user came from the terms page.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state for this page comes from (1) the response of getStaticPageContent and (2) the URL and its query parameters. The pageSlug argument for getStaticPageContent is derived from the URL path for terms.html (for example, by mapping the route segment to the pageSlug), so the content is always requested based on the current route instead of a hardcoded constant. A section query parameter in the URL (e.g., terms.html?section={sectionId}) indicates which section of the rendered terms should be considered active. On initial load and on subsequent URL changes (via user interaction or browser navigation), the page reads the section parameter and scrolls to the corresponding section anchor if it exists. Changing the section parameter does not trigger a new getStaticPageContent call; it only affects client-side scrolling and highlighting within the already-loaded content. Optional additional query parameters (such as a mode parameter to toggle between different viewing modes) may be read to control purely presentational aspects, but no business logic or data fetching logic is driven by localStorage or other client-side persistence—only by the current URL and the getStaticPageContent response.

components.functionality: Initializes the page by requesting the Terms of Service content through getStaticPageContent, manages loading and error states, and exposes the resulting title and body to child components.
Displays the terms page title in the page header and synchronizes it with the document title using the title field returned from getStaticPageContent.

Renders the body string returned from getStaticPageContent into the main content area, interprets any markup (such as HTML or markdown) provided by the API, assigns stable IDs to headings or defined section markers, and responds to the current section URL parameter by scrolling to and focusing the corresponding section.

Analyzes the structured content of the rendered body (for example, by extracting headings or explicit section markers) to construct a navigable table of contents. When a user selects an entry, it updates the section query parameter in the URL and requests the content area to scroll to the chosen section. It also highlights the currently active section based on the section parameter and scroll position.

Provides a dedicated navigational control that routes the user from the terms page to privacy.html when they wish to view the Privacy Policy. It may optionally append a query parameter such as source=terms to the destination URL based on the current route or context.

layout.grid_system: Implicit centered column layout likely based on a 12‑column grid with a wide central content span (approx. 8–10 columns) and large left/right gutters.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom structure: a full-width black navigation bar at the very top (global), followed by a tall, mostly empty off-white band. Centered within this band sits a single dominant Terms of Service card that contains all visible page components related to this view: the terms title on the top-left of the card, a compact sections table-of-contents in the top-right of the card, the main terms body text below spanning the full card width, and a small privacy link anchored at the bottom-right of the card. The data-fetcher runs behind the scenes and does not occupy visible space.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom structure: a full-width black navigation bar at the very top (global), followed by a tall, mostly empty off-white band. Centered within this band sits a single dominant Terms of Service card that contains all visible page components related to this view: the terms title on the top-left of the card, a compact sections table-of-contents in the top-right of the card, the main terms body text below spanning the full card width, and a small privacy link anchored at the bottom-right of the card. The data-fetcher runs behind the scenes and does not occupy visible space.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The nav bar occupies roughly the top 8–10% of the viewport height. Below it, 20–30% of the viewport is reserved as empty off-white space before the Terms card begins, reinforcing the sense of calm. Horizontally, the main card is centered and capped at about 60–70% of the viewport width, leaving 15–20% gutters on both sides. Inside the card, padding is generous (about 24–32px on all sides). The header row (title + TOC) sits in the top ~20% of the card, with a gap of around 16–24px before the body text. The privacy link at the bottom has a breathing space of about 24–32px from the last line of the main body text.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density remains low: only one main card is fully visible above the fold, with plenty of vertical whitespace around it. Text lines are limited in width by the central column, with ample line height and spacing between sections of the Terms. The TOC is compact and understated, and the privacy link is small and unobtrusive, ensuring the visual weight stays on the title and body text.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first hits the high-contrast black nav at the top, then drops into the quiet off-white space below. This whitespace directs attention toward the center where the Terms card sits. Inside the card, the visual flow starts at the top-left with the prominent title, then is gently pulled to the top-right where the sections TOC offers navigational options. From there, the eye moves downward into the full-width body text for reading. At the very end of the scroll within the card, the small privacy link in the bottom-right corner provides a final interaction target, echoing the mockup’s pattern of a right-aligned control as the last point of focus.

layout.overall_description: The Terms of Service page appears as a quiet, editorial-style reading surface. A solid black navigation bar stretches edge-to-edge across the top of the viewport, thin but visually heavy. Beneath it, a broad expanse of off-white fills the screen with little else in view, allowing the user’s attention to settle. In the center of this space, a single, lightly outlined or subtly shadowed card is centered on the page, occupying about two-thirds of the viewport width and roughly half or more of its height. The card has comfortable padding, keeping all text elements away from its edges. At the top-left inside the card, a clean, prominent “Terms of Service” heading anchors the content. Aligned with it on the top-right, a compact list of section links (the TOC) mirrors the two-column header structure seen in the mockup: informational block on the left, small interactive block on the right. Below this shared header row, the card becomes a single, uninterrupted column of Terms text that scrolls as needed, with generous line spacing and clear separation between sections. When the user reaches the bottom of this content, a subtle privacy link is placed near the bottom-right edge of the card, slightly offset from the text above, acting as a quiet final call-to-action. Throughout, wide side gutters, large vertical gaps between the nav, the card, and any content below preserve the monochrome, low-density, reading-focused character of the original mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The static content loader operates at the page level and does not render any visible UI elements. Conceptually, it sits behind the main content column, initializing before the central Terms card is populated. Any loading or empty states are expressed through the terms-title-header and terms-body-renderer (for example, as placeholder text or skeleton blocks), rather than through a dedicated visual component. It therefore occupies no independent space in the grid and does not alter the centered-column layout or the surrounding whitespace.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, purely functional; it has no layout footprint and does not affect spacing, alignment, or flow beyond controlling when the other components can display their content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The title header is placed inside the main Terms card at the very top-left corner of its content area, sitting within a generous 24–32px padding from the card’s top and left edges. It forms the left side of a two-column header row, occupying roughly 70–75% of the card’s width. The heading text is set on a single column, left-aligned, with ample white space above (within the card’s top padding) and a vertical separation of about 16–24px below before the body text begins. Horizontally, its baseline aligns with the top of the terms-section-toc on the right, visually establishing a shared header band. On narrow screens, this header still appears atop the card, with the title stacking above the TOC if the available width shrinks, but the intention remains that the title is the first and primary element the eye encounters inside the card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the card’s flow; it scrolls away with the rest of the card content as the user reads down the Terms.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The body renderer sits directly below the combined header row (title on the left, TOC on the right), spanning the full internal width of the card minus side padding. It begins approximately 16–24px below the header components, maintaining a clear but not exaggerated separation. The text is laid out as a single vertical column with a comfortable maximum line length defined by the card width (about 60–70% of the viewport), and includes generous line-height and section spacing so that each clause or heading within the Terms feels distinct and readable. As the content is likely long, this region extends downward and becomes the primary scrolling area of the page; the surrounding off-white background remains static while the card content moves. Left and right margins inside the card are consistent from top to bottom, keeping the text visually centered within the main column. The bottom of this area leaves around 24–32px of space before the privacy link to signal the end of the legal text and the start of secondary navigation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block within the central card that grows vertically as needed; it participates in normal page scrolling and does not fix or float relative to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sections table-of-contents is positioned inside the main Terms card at the top-right, forming the right side of the two-column header row that mirrors the mockup’s avatar/meta plus overflow pattern. It is aligned horizontally with the terms-title-header, but anchored to the card’s right padding edge, occupying roughly 25–30% of the card’s width on larger screens. The TOC appears as a compact vertical stack of section links or labels, with subtle typography to keep it visually lighter than the main title. Its top aligns with the top of the title text, and its bottom generally remains within the upper band of the card so that the overall header still reads as a single cohesive row. A horizontal gap of about 24px separates the right edge of the title block from the left edge of the TOC block, preserving clarity between them. Below this header band, the terms-body-renderer spans the full width, so the TOC does not extend alongside the main text; it is strictly a header-level navigational element. On small viewports, the TOC may wrap beneath the title, still right-aligned, but maintaining a modest top margin so the hierarchy remains clear.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the card header; it scrolls away when the user moves down the long Terms text. No fixed or floating behavior is applied by default to preserve the simple, quiet reading experience.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The privacy link is placed at the very bottom of the main Terms card, aligned to the bottom-right corner of the card’s content area. It sits approximately 24–32px below the final line of the terms-body-renderer, separated by clean whitespace that signals a footer-like zone within the card. The link text is small and low-key, visually lighter than the main body text, ensuring it does not compete with the Terms content but is easy to find once the user has reached the end. Horizontally, it hugs the right padding edge of the card, creating a subtle echo of the mockup’s right-aligned overflow icon as the last interactive focal point. The left side of this footer zone remains mostly empty to preserve the minimalist feel, with no additional elements flanking the link.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element at the end of the card’s content; it appears only after the user scrolls through the Terms and moves with the rest of the page during scrolling.

name: Privacy Policy
title: Privacy Policy
description: Static informational page that retrieves and displays the site's privacy policy content, including data handling practices, cookie usage, and links to related legal documents.

core_features: Load and display the privacy policy content using the getStaticPageContent interface with the appropriate pageSlug.

Support deep-linking to specific sections of the privacy policy via URL parameters (e.g., privacy.html?section=... or hash fragments) to allow direct navigation to topics such as data collection or cookies.
Provide in-page navigation between privacy policy sections (e.g., table of contents or section links) by updating the URL and scrolling to the selected section.
Expose a cross-link to the Terms of Service (terms.html) from within the privacy policy content and/or a dedicated navigation control.
Handle error and loading states for the static content fetch (e.g., pending, error retrieving content) without hardcoding any policy text.
Preserve and reflect navigation context via URL parameters (e.g., from=footer or from=signup) so the Terms page can understand where the user came from if needed.
user_workflows: Workflow: View privacy policy from global footer -> User clicks 'Privacy' in the global footer -> Browser navigates to privacy.html (optionally with a from=footer parameter) -> Page initializes and determines pageSlug for the privacy policy -> static-page-loader component calls getStaticPageContent with pageSlug -> privacy-content-viewer renders the returned title and body -> (optional) privacy-section-nav reads URL parameters or hash to scroll to a specific section.

Workflow: Open a specific privacy section via direct link -> User opens a URL such as privacy.html?section=<section-id> or privacy.html#<section-id> from another page or external source -> Page initializes and calls getStaticPageContent with the privacy pageSlug -> After content is loaded, privacy-section-nav checks the current URL for a section parameter or hash -> Component locates the matching section within the rendered content and scrolls it into view, optionally highlighting it.

Workflow: Navigate to Terms of Service from privacy policy -> User scrolls through the privacy policy content -> User finds a reference link labeled for Terms of Service within the content or in a dedicated cross-link area -> User clicks link -> terms-link component triggers navigation to terms.html while preserving relevant context URL parameters (e.g., from=privacy, section=currentSectionId) -> Browser loads terms.html with these parameters.

Workflow: Handle loading and error states -> User navigates to privacy.html -> static-page-loader component calls getStaticPageContent with pageSlug -> While awaiting response, the component sets a loading state that can be rendered by the privacy-content-viewer (e.g., generic loading indicator text) without hardcoded policy data -> If the request fails, the component sets an error state that is surfaced to the user as a generic error message and may provide a retry interaction -> On success, the loading/error states are cleared and the policy content is rendered.
interactions: Click: Global footer 'Privacy' link -> Navigates to privacy.html with an optional from=footer URL parameter.

Page load: When privacy.html is loaded or reloaded -> static-page-loader reads the URL parameters (e.g., section, from, lang, version) and determines the pageSlug to pass to getStaticPageContent -> Initiates the API call and updates its internal loading/error/content state.

Content load completion: Once getStaticPageContent resolves successfully -> privacy-content-viewer receives the title and body data and renders them -> privacy-section-nav is notified that content is ready and may apply deep-link logic for section/anchor navigation.

URL change: User interacts with in-page section links (e.g., table of contents) -> privacy-section-nav updates the URL (e.g., privacy.html?section=<section-id> or updates hash) using pushState/replaceState or default anchor behavior -> Component scrolls to the corresponding section in the rendered body.

Click: Terms of Service cross-link inside the privacy policy -> terms-link constructs a URL to terms.html, optionally carrying over parameters such as from=privacy and section=currentSectionId -> Browser navigates to the new URL.

Retry interaction: If getStaticPageContent fails -> User clicks a generic 'Retry' control exposed by privacy-content-viewer -> static-page-loader reissues the getStaticPageContent call using the same pageSlug.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page does not have formal incoming route parameters, but uses URL search parameters and/or hash fragments to manage client-side state. On initialization, static-page-loader determines the pageSlug for the privacy policy (e.g., from the route or a fallback default) and calls getStaticPageContent(pageSlug). The following URL-based states are supported: 1) section: privacy.html?section=<section-id> identifies which logical section of the policy should be scrolled into view after content loads. privacy-section-nav reads this parameter, locates the corresponding section within the rendered body (e.g., by matching identifiers parsed from the content or associated data attributes), and ensures it is visible and optionally highlighted. 2) from: privacy.html?from=<source-id> records where the user came from (e.g., footer, signup, banner). This is not needed to fetch content but is preserved when constructing outbound navigation URLs, such as terms.html?from=privacy&source=<source-id>. 3) lang and version: privacy.html?lang=<locale>&version=<version-id> may be used to request locale-specific or versioned policy content, by influencing the pageSlug passed to getStaticPageContent or being appended to it (e.g., combining base slug with locale or version). The exact mapping from these parameters to pageSlug is handled internally by static-page-loader without hardcoding any policy text. 4) Hash fragment: privacy.html#<section-id> provides an alternative method for section deep-linking. privacy-section-nav listens for hash changes and, after content is loaded, scrolls to the matching section. Throughout, no policy text or static content is hardcoded; all displayable content comes from getStaticPageContent, with URL parameters only affecting which content is requested and how it is navigated.

components.functionality: Determines the appropriate pageSlug for the privacy policy based on the current route and optional URL parameters, calls getStaticPageContent(pageSlug), and manages loading, error, and success states for the privacy policy content.
Receives privacy policy title and body from static-page-loader and renders them, handling generic loading and error states and exposing the rendered DOM structure for section-based navigation.

Enables deep-link navigation to specific sections of the privacy policy, based on URL parameters or hash fragments and user clicks on in-page section links or table-of-contents entries. Updates the URL to reflect the currently active section and scrolls to it within the rendered content.
Constructs and handles navigation to terms.html when the user clicks on any Terms of Service reference inside the privacy policy, preserving relevant context parameters such as from and section.
Represents the global footer 'Privacy' link behavior as it relates to this page, ensuring navigation to privacy.html with an optional from=footer parameter and no static content assumptions.

layout.grid_system: Implicit centered column layout likely based on a 12‑column grid with a wide central content span (approx. 8–10 columns) and large left/right gutters.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom structure: a full-width black navigation bar at the very top, a tall off-white band beneath it containing the page title on the left and secondary controls on the right, and then a single, prominent, centered content card that holds the privacy policy text. The privacy-content-viewer takes the role of the forum post card from the mockup. The privacy-section-nav is integrated into the off-white band on the right side of the central column, mirroring where the search icon sits in the mockup. The terms-link lives at the bottom of the main content card as a subtle, inline call-to-action. The footer-privacy-entrypoint appears in the global footer at the very bottom of the page, inside the same centered column system.

The privacy-content-viewer takes the role of the forum post card from the mockup. The privacy-section-nav is integrated into the off-white band on the right side of the central column, mirroring where the search icon sits in the mockup. The terms-link lives at the bottom of the main content card as a subtle, inline call-to-action. The footer-privacy-entrypoint appears in the global footer at the very bottom of the page, inside the same centered column system.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The black navigation bar occupies roughly 8–10% of the viewport height and spans the full width. Below it, the off-white band takes up about 20–25% of the viewport height, with ample empty space, the page title, and the section navigation sitting in the central column. The central column itself occupies about 60–70% of the page width, with 15–20% gutters on both sides. The privacy-content-viewer card begins after a generous vertical gap of roughly 48–64px from the bottom of the off-white band and initially occupies about 50–60% of the viewport height, but extends vertically as needed for the full policy. Internal card padding is in the 24–32px range on all sides. The terms-link sits in a dedicated, spaced footer block of the card, and the footer-privacy-entrypoint is placed in a short footer strip with similar centered column alignment, separated from the main content by a 48–64px vertical gap.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content is intentionally sparse and calm above the fold. The hero region (off-white band) has only the title and a compact section navigation, surrounded by generous whitespace. The main privacy-content-viewer card uses comfortable line lengths and line heights, with 16–24px spacing between paragraphs and 32–48px space between major sections. Only a small number of obvious controls are visible at any time: global navigation, section navigation, and a discrete terms-link near the end. The loader, when active, appears as a single card-shaped placeholder rather than multiple busy indicators, maintaining the low-density, editorial reading feel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first moves across the high-contrast black nav at the top, then drops into the off-white band where it catches the bold 'Privacy Policy' heading on the left. From there, attention is gently pulled to the right toward the compact privacy-section-nav, which offers anchor links to major sections. The large empty space below the band guides the eye down into the centered privacy-content-viewer card, which becomes the main reading focus. Inside the card, the flow is left-to-right along the top header (title and meta), then vertically through the body text. Near the bottom of the card, the terms-link gives a final focal point before the gaze moves down to the global footer, where the footer-privacy-entrypoint provides a persistent way back to this page from elsewhere on the site.

layout.overall_description: The Privacy Policy page reads like a quiet, editorial article laid out in a minimalist forum shell. A solid black navigation bar sits at the very top, full-width and relatively shallow, anchoring the UI. Beneath it, a tall, off-white band stretches across the viewport with a wide, invisible centered column. On the left side of this column, the words 'Privacy Policy' appear in a restrained, typographic heading style, with plenty of breathing room above and below. On the right side of the same column, at roughly the same vertical alignment, a compact column of section links (the privacy-section-nav) offers quick jumps to major parts of the policy, without intruding visually on the open space. Below this band, a large gap of neutral background reinforces the sense of airiness before the main content begins. In the center of the page, a single white card—the privacy-content-viewer—floats within wide left and right gutters, about 60–70% of the page width. Its edges are clean, with subtle or no borders, and generous 24–32px padding inside. At the top of the card, a simple header row spans the full width: the document title and meta information on the left, and optionally a minimal utility affordance on the right, echoing the two-column header pattern from the mockup. Below that, the policy text flows in a single, readable column down the page, broken into clearly spaced sections. Near the bottom of the card, the terms-link appears as a small, right-leaning callout pointing users to the Terms of Service. When the content is still loading, a single, card-shaped loader occupies the same central area, preserving the layout’s quiet structure. At the very bottom of the page, separated by a tall chunk of whitespace, a light, monochrome footer runs full-width, with its internal content constrained to the same centered column. Inside this footer, the footer-privacy-entrypoint sits as a small textual link among other legal links, providing a consistent way to reach the Privacy Policy from anywhere on the site while maintaining the same restrained visual language.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The static-page-loader occupies the exact footprint reserved for the main privacy-content-viewer card, ensuring that the perception of layout does not shift between loading and loaded states. It is centered horizontally on the page within the main content column, spanning approximately 60–70% of the viewport width, with 15–20% gutters on both sides. Vertically, it begins after the same large off-white band and 48–64px gap that precedes the content card, and its initial height covers roughly 50–60% of the viewport. Visually, it appears as a single, large, off-white or light-gray rectangle with no decorative elements, optionally with a few subtle skeleton lines near the top where the title and first paragraphs will appear. The rest remains mostly empty to preserve the low-density, editorial feel. There are no additional loading spinners scattered around the page—any animation, if present, is minimal and centered within this card area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: When active, the loader is positioned relative to the scrolling page and replaces the privacy-content-viewer in the central column. It scrolls with the page if the viewport is shorter than the loader’s height. Once loading is complete, this component disappears entirely, yielding its space to the privacy-content-viewer without altering the surrounding spacing or alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The privacy-content-viewer is the main content card, visually echoing the single post card from the mockup. It is centered in the viewport within the main column, occupying about 60–70% of the page width, with generous 15–20% gutters of background color on the left and right. The top edge of the card sits a full 48–64px below the bottom of the off-white hero band, reinforcing a strong, airy separation between the page header and the reading area. The card has 24–32px padding on all sides, maintaining a quiet, spacious feel inside. At the top of the card, a horizontal header row runs full width: on the left, the 'Privacy Policy' title is rendered in a slightly larger type size, with an optional smaller meta line beneath it (such as 'Last updated' information). On the right side of this row, aligned to the top or middle of the title block, a small, unobtrusive utility affordance (such as a subtle overflow icon or print icon) may sit, echoing the overflow icon from the forum post header while remaining visually subdued. Below this header row, the policy body content flows in a single, full-width text column. Each major section (e.g., 'Information We Collect', 'How We Use Information', 'Cookies', 'Your Rights') begins with a section heading left-aligned to the card’s content edge, with 32–48px of vertical space above it to clearly separate sections. Paragraphs within sections are spaced with 16–24px between them, and list items, if any, are minimally styled to keep the page feeling like an editorial document rather than an app interface. The overall card may extend considerably beyond the viewport height, but it remains a single, uninterrupted reading surface. At the very bottom inside the card, the terms-link sits in its own subtly distinct block, separated by 32–48px of vertical whitespace from the last paragraph of the policy, visually acting as a quiet footer to the document.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The privacy-content-viewer is a standard scrollable block within the page’s document flow. It does not fix or pin itself; instead, it begins just below the hero band and extends downward as needed. On taller screens, its header and top content remain near the middle of the viewport when the page first loads, presenting a single, prominent card above the fold.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The privacy-section-nav is placed within the off-white band that sits directly below the black navigation bar and above the main content card. This band shares the same full-width background, but its active content is constrained to the central column. Inside this column, the page title 'Privacy Policy' is aligned to the left, roughly 20–25% in from the viewport’s left edge, while the section navigation is aligned to the right edge of the same column. The section-nav itself occupies about 25–30% of the column width, leaving ample empty space between it and the title. Vertically, it is centered within the band so that it feels balanced rather than crowded at the top or bottom. The nav is a compact vertical stack: a small, muted label like 'On this page' at the top, followed by a short list of the most important section links (e.g., 4–8 items) with 8–16px of vertical spacing between each. Each link is left-aligned to the nav’s own narrow column and set in understated typography, ensuring it reads as a secondary aide, not a competing main element. The surrounding off-white space is left mostly untouched, preserving the mockup’s impression of a tall, quiet header region. The section navigation does not extend into the card itself; instead, it sits just above it, visually connected to the title but clearly separate from the main reading surface.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The privacy-section-nav is static within the header band: it scrolls away once the user moves down through the privacy-content-viewer. It is not sticky or floating, in keeping with the calm, unobtrusive feel of the layout. On smaller viewports, it can collapse into a single line (e.g., a 'Sections' text control) aligned to the right, but it remains located in the off-white band above the content card.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The terms-link is embedded within the lower part of the privacy-content-viewer card, acting as a gentle, end-of-document cross-link. It appears after the final main section of the policy. There is a 32–48px vertical gap between the last paragraph of the policy and a lightly separated 'related documents' area. Within this area, the terms-link is right-leaning in its alignment: for example, it can appear as a short line of text like 'See also: Terms of Service' aligned to the right edge of the card’s content column or as a right-aligned inline link following a small label. The font size is equal to or slightly smaller than body text, and the link styling is minimal (perhaps a simple underline or subtle color shift within the monochrome palette). The surrounding whitespace ensures the link doesn’t feel crowded or promotional; it reads as a quiet, logical next step instead of a button-like CTA.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The terms-link is part of the normal document flow within the privacy-content-viewer. It scrolls with the rest of the card content and remains near the bottom of the page, only coming into view after the user has read or scrolled past most of the policy text.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The footer-privacy-entrypoint lives inside the site’s global footer, which spans the full width at the bottom of the page but constrains its content to the same centered column as the rest of the layout. The footer itself is a low-contrast, light gray or off-white bar with relatively modest height, separated from the bottom of the privacy-content-viewer card by a generous 48–64px vertical gap of background space. Within the footer’s central column, legal and utility links are arranged in a compact, single or double row of text, aligned either left or center depending on the site’s overall pattern. The footer-privacy-entrypoint is one of these text links—typically labeled 'Privacy Policy'—set in the same understated typography as the other footer links. It might appear as part of a small cluster such as 'Terms · Privacy · Cookies', with a small amount of horizontal spacing (8–16px) between each link. The link is not visually highlighted beyond standard link styling; it relies on its conventional location and label rather than visual emphasis. The generous whitespace above the footer ensures that this row of links feels like a quiet coda to the page rather than a cluttered appendix.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The footer-privacy-entrypoint is a static element at the bottom of the page within the footer. It appears only when the user scrolls to the end of the content and does not stick or float. It is consistently present across pages as part of the global footer, providing a stable entry point back to the Privacy Policy while adhering to the minimalist, low-key design.
